Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Homeostasis is called dynamic equilibrium because the body maintains an internal balance within
fluctuating limits. It describes the maintenance of nearly constant conditions in the internal
environment despite changes in the outside world.

A number of variables such as body temperature, blood sugar levels, blood pH, etc. are regulated
within a range of values (homeostatic ranges) and not at fixed values.

These variables are not given equal importance. There is a hierarchy of importance so that the
constancy of certain variables may be altered markedly to maintain others within their normal
range. For example, respiratory or metabolic compensation to maintain bloodspH.

The disruption of homeostasis either due to extreme enviroytmental challenge or a breakdown of
internal homeostatic mechanisms is termed as a disease.! ‘-

Solution to Question 3:

Feedback regulation is seen in the increased heart rate in response to a decrease in blood
pressure. This is because, in feedback regulation, the homeostatic response (increased heart rate)
occurs after the change (decrease in blood pressure) has occurred.

On the other hand, in feedforward regulation, the homeostatic responses take place before the
change has occurred. This is done in anticipation of the change to minimize fluctuations.
Examples of feedforward regulation include:

¢ The increased heart rate that occurs in an athlete just before a sprint begins.

* The smell of food triggers the secretion of salivary and gastric juices.

h

+ An increase in insulin before glucose absorption has raised blood glucose levels :

Solution to Question 5:

The gain of the feedback system in the above case is -2.
The gain of the system is then calculated by using the following formula:
Gain = C/E

Here, C is correction achieved from the deviated value and E is the remaining error
post-correction.

Calculation

12 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 13 ========================================
¢ The original value in the given case is 100 mmHg.
* The deviated value in the given case is 175 mmHg.
* The corrected value in the given case is 125 mmHg.

* C is given by correction achieved from deviated value, Corrected value - Deviated value = 125 -
175 = -50 mmHg.

* Eis given by the remaining error post-correction, Corrected value - Original value = 125 - 100 =
25 mmHg.

* Gain = -50/25 = -2.

The negative symbol indicates that the homeostatic mechanism acts in a direction (apposite to the

initial deviation. ,!

Solution to Question, 7* nae

Membrane(flitidity is increaséd by linoleic acid, an unsaturated fatty acid. All other options are
saturated fatty acids. “4

Longer and saturated fatty acids decrease the fluidity because their side chains are straight and
interact more strongly with each other. Conversely, unsaturated fatty acids increase the
fluidity because their side chains are kinked and less compact.

Cholesterol, also known as fluidity buffer helps to maintain the fluidity of the cell membrane.

Cell surface receptors are peripheral proteins.

Solution to Question 10:

The sequence of vesicular transport in a cell is ER - Cis - Trans - Cell membrane.

Rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) - It is the site of protein synthesis. Proteins are taken to the
Golgi apparatus through transport vesicles.

Cis end of Golgi apparatus - Also called as receiving end, it is closest to RER
Trans end of Golgi apparatus - Also called as releasing end, it is furthest away from RER.

Cell membrane - Synthesised proteins within secretory vesicles are released into the extracellular
space through the process of exocytosis.

Solution to Question 12:

Mature red blood cells will test negative for all mitochondrial markers are as they do not have
mitochondria.

Lack of mitochondria is the reason why red blood cells exclusively use anaerobic glycolysis to
generate ATP.

======================================== PAGE 16 ========================================
Catalase is the characteristic enzyme of peroxisomes. All other are lysosomal enzymes.

Solution to Question 14:

Gap junctions are absent in adult skeletal muscles.

Gap junctions are intercellular connections with a channel in between. They are present in
excitable tissues such as cardiac muscle, smooth muscle and neurons but not in skeletal muscles.

Solution to Question) : i
he 1h

. . Tee .
Tight junetions are not seen-in cardiac muscle.

Tight junctions form a water-tight seal between cells. They are characteristically seen in the
following:

* Intestinal mucosa

* Renal tubules

* Choroid plexus

* Sertoli cells

Functions of tight junctions are:

* Prevents the diffusion of macromolecules between cells.
¢ Maintains cell polarity.

* Seals the paracellular pathway.

16 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 17 ========================================
Solution to Question 16:

Spectrin is a cytoskeletal protein but not a cell adhesion molecule.

Cells are attached to each other or to the basal lamina by cell adhesion molecules (CAMs). Some
important classes of CAMs are:

* Integrins: heterodimers that bind to various receptors

* IgG superfamily of immunoglobulins

* Cadherins: Ca2+-dependent molecules that mediate cell-to-cell adhesion by homophilic reactions
* Selectins which have lectin-like domains that bind carbohydrates

Clinical significance No :

Pe,
* Cancer cells have abnormal CAMs and hence cause decreased cell adhesion arid allow individual
cancer cells to detach from the parent cancer. . am

* The decrease in the amount of E-cadherin maybé'thie'reason for ealy metastasis of numerous

tumors. wo” o . :

Solution to Question 18:

Integrin connects with fibronectin.

Integrins are transmembrane glycoproteins (integral proteins) that allow cells to attach to
extracellular matrix constituents such as laminin, fibronectin and collagen. It help n linking the
intracellular cytoskeleton functionally and structurally with the outside world;

Detailed Explanations
7 ra, =i"
Solution to Question 1: i os!

Parathyroid hormone\@TH) ) is an n.exdimple of a polypeptide hormone.
Cortisol is a steroid hormone, Adrenaline and thyroxine are derivatives of the amino acid tyrosine.

4 Aa
he a

Solution to Question 2:

Thyroxine crosses the plasma membrane for its action.

Thyroid hormones enter the nucleus and bind to thyroid hormone receptors. The
hormone-receptor complex in the nucleus binds to DNA via zinc fingers. This increases the
expression of a variety of different genes that code for proteins regulating cell function. Although
thyroxine is an AA derivative, there are transporters to move it into the cell, hence they are
intracellular receptors acting on the nucleus.

Epinephrine, glucagon, and insulin bind to cell surface receptors.

Solution to Question 3:

26 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 27 ========================================
Cortisol is not stored before release.

Usually, the polypeptide and protein hormones such as insulin, thyroxine or renin are stored in
secretory vesicles before release. They are synthesized as much larger polypeptide chains
(preprohormones) that are biologically inactive and then processed intracellularly by specific
proteases to yield the final hormone molecule which is stored in the secretory vesicle until needed.

On the other hand, steroid hormones such as cortisol are synthesised as needed and are not
stored. They are lipid soluble and easily diffuse across cell membrane to enter the interstitial fluid
and then blood.

Solution to Question 5:
Nicotinic cholinergic receptors activate an ion channel after binding with its agonist. These

receptors are called ligand-gated channels or ionotropic receptors.

Receptors for neurotransmitters are classified into two broad families based on structure and
function. They are:

¢ Ligand-gated channels (Ionotropic receptors)

* When bound to these receptors, a membrane channel is opened leading to a brief increase in
ionic conductance.

* Important for fast synaptic transmission.
¢ Examples - GABAA, GABAC, Nicotinic cholinergic, Glycine and Serotonin (5HT3 subtype).

* G-protein coupled receptors (Metabotropic receptors)

27 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 28 ========================================
* When bound to these receptors, secondary messengers are released that modulate voltage-gated
channels on neuronal membranes.

¢ Examples - GABAB, Muscarinic cholinergic, Catecholamines, Serotonin (all subtypes except
5HT3) and opioid receptors.

Solution to Question 13:

Second messengers mediate the intracellular responses to many different hormones and
neurotransmitters.

Cell surface receptors are substances that interact with the first messengers which are hormones
and neurotransmitters.

The second messengers are the intracellular signalling molecules. They bring about many
short-term changes in cell function by altering enzyme function, triggering exocytosis, and other
physiological effects. They can also lead to the alteration of transcription of various genes (long
term effect).

30 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 31 ========================================
Examples of second messengers are cAMP, cGMP, Ca2+ and IP3-DAG (Inositol triphosphate and
diacylglycerol).

Solution to Question 14:

Among the following calcium (Ca2+) acts as a second messenger.

Increased cytoplasmic Ca2+ binds to and activates calcium-binding proteins (calmodulin,
troponin) or activate protein kinases (or phospholipase C) to bring about physiological effects.

Cytoplasmic Ca2+ is increased by: +

* Direct entry of Ca2+ from the ECF through Ca2+ channels that could be liggitgated,

other second messengers such as IP3-DAG (Inositol.{riphosphate-and diacylglycerol).

Vasopressin does not use cAMP exclusively.

Vasopressin (ADH) acts on at least 3 kinds of receptors: V1A, V1B and V2

Solution to Question 17:

Vasodilatory actions of NO are mediated by cGMP.

NO is a hormone derived from the amino acid arginine. It activates guanylyl cyclases in vascular
smooth muscle cells resulting in the conversion of cGTP to cGMP (2nd messenger). cGMP
activates cGMP dependent protein kinase, which causes blood vessels to relax.

cGMP is the second messenger for nitric oxide and atrial natriuretic factor.

Solution to Question 20:
Among the given options, the most probable diagnosis is McCune-Albright syndrome as it is the
only gain-of-function mutation of G Protein.

G proteins or G protein-coupled receptor complexes can undergo a loss- or gain-of-function
mutation. Some examples are given below:

In McCune-Albright syndrome, a gain-of-function mutation in Gs° is associated with the
rough-bordered areas of skin pigmentation and hypercortisolism. This mutation occurs during
fetal development, creating a mosaic of normal and abnormal cells.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Lipid-soluble substances are transported across the cell membrane via diffusion.
* The rate of diffusion depends directly on lipid solubility.

* Characteristics of simple diffusion

¢ The only form of transport that is not carrier-mediated %

* Occurs down an electrochemical gradient (“downhill”) "

a A

* Does not require metabolic energy and therefore is paSsive 4
2 a
Permeability ‘ Ea %

* Describes the ease with which a solute diffuses through a membrane

* Depends on the characteristics of thee sont and the membrane £ ‘
* Small hydrophobic solutes (e.g's, Oa) ihhive the hehest permeabilities i in lipid membranes.

¢ Hydrophilic solutes (e.g., Na) must cross‘ ell membranes, through water-filled channels, or
pores. If the solute is an ionythen its flinx will depends on ‘both the concentration difference and the
potential difference across the membrane. ‘i

ha, |

Solution to Question 2 +
Among the following substances,” oxygen diffuses more rapidly through phospholipid membranes
as itisa lipid- -soluble substaice.

Phospholipid membranes are:
* Permeable to lipid-soluble substances (e.g., 02, CO2, NH3, steroid hormones)
¢ Impermeable to water-soluble substances (e.g., Na+, Cl-, glucose)

¢ Semipermeable to water

Solution to Question 3:

Fick’s law of diffusion explains passive diffusion along the concentration gradient.

Fick’s law of diffusion: The magnitude of the diffusing tendency is directly proportional to the
cross-sectional area across which diffusion is taking place and the concentration gradient, or
chemical gradient, which is the difference in concentration of the diffusing substance divided by
the thickness of the membrane.

Solution to Question 5:

42 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 43 ========================================
Simple diffusion is not carrier-mediated transport hence do not follow saturation kinetics.

¢ Facilitated diffusion, primary active transport, and secondary active transport all involve integral
membrane proteins and are called carrier-mediated transport.

+ All carrier-mediated transport system follows saturation kinetics.

* The figure shows that as the concentration of the diffusing substance increases, the rate of
simple diffusion continues to increase proportionately, but in the case of facilitated diffusion, the
rate of diffusion cannot rise greater than the transport maximum (Tm). Tm is analogous to

the maximum velocity (Vmax) level in enzyme kinetics.

Facilitated diffusion

Solution to Question’6: &
When carer proteins help: in the movement of substances in the direction of their chemical or
electrical gradients (downhill movement), no energy input is required and the process is called
facilitated diffusion.

Examples

* GLUT

* Urea Transporters :

* UTA 1, 2, 3, 4 in Kidney

* UTB in RBC

¢ Amino Acid Transporters

Other options are wrong because:

* Facilitated diffusion is a type of passive transport.
* It follows saturation kinetics.

* No energy input is required in facilitated diffusion.

Solution to Question 8:

44 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 45 ========================================
Difference between osmolarity &amp; osmolality:
* Osmolarity is the number of osmoles per litre of solution,
* Osmolality is the number of osmoles per kilogram of solvent.

* Therefore, osmolarity is affected by the volume of the various solutes in the solution and the
temperature, while the osmolality is not.

Solution to Question 10: he ‘eh 4

r
The absorption of salicylates like aspirin in the stomach is  ancexample of nonionic diffusion.

‘ss
¢ Some weak acids and bases are quite soluble i in cell menibranes i in the undissociated form, but
they cannot cross membranes in the charged (i.e. dissociated) form. Consequently, molecules of
the undissociated substance diffugesrom one side of the membrane to the other and then
dissociate. This phenomenors:alled nonionic diffusion.
Examples ~ * oo

¢ Ammonia excretion in collecting ite iets
° Excretiongs of. weak acids and bases in the kidney
° Absorption of salicylatésiin the stomach

¢ Absorption of bile acids in the distal ileum

Solution to Question 11:

When a carrier protein transports substances against their electrical and chemical gradients
(uphill movement) it requires energy and this type of transport is called active transport.

Solution to Question 13:..\°

Na+- K+ ATPase or Na+-K+ ATP pump catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATP to ADP and uses the
energy to extrude three Na+ from the cell and take two K+ into the cell for each molecule of ATP
hydrolyzed.

Sodium-Potassium Pump/ Na+— K+ Pump:

* Carrier protein located on the plasma membrane of all cells

* Pumps 3 Na+ out of the cell &amp; 2 K+ into the cell (coupling ratio 3:2)

* Binding sites on ° subunit:

* 3 intracellular sites- for Na+, ATP, and one phosphorylation site

¢ 2 extracellular sites- for K+ and oubain

Solution to Question 16:

Empagliflozin is an SGLT-2 inhibitor. SGLT-2 or sodium-glucose transport protein 2 is an
example of a cotransporter that plays a key role in glucose absorption from renal tubule

by secondary active transport. This process is blocked by SGLT-2 inhibitors, leading to renal loss
of glucose and improved glycemic control.

¢ A special property of the transport protein involved in secondary active transport is that a
conformational change to allow Na+ movement to the interior will not occur until a glucose

48 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 49 ========================================
molecule also attaches to the transport protein. When they both become attached, the
conformational change takes place automatically, and the Na+ and glucose are transported to the
inside of the cell at the same time.

* The figure shows glucose is transported uphill and Na+ is transported downhill. Energy derived
from the downhill movement of Na+ is maintained by the Na+-K+ ATPase pump on the
basolateral side.

Solution to Question 17: ot
a k

Glucose is absorbed i inl th he intesting by Secondary active transport (sodium co-transport).

The energy for aitieese transport is provided indirectly, by the active transport of Na+ out of the
cell. This maintains the conientration gradient across the luminal border of the cell so that more
Na+ and cénsequently miore glucose enter.

Though both secondary active transport and facilitated diffusion play a role in glucose absorption,
it is the initial active transport of sodium through the basolateral membranes of the intestinal
epithelial cells that provide the eventual force for moving glucose through the membranes as well.
So, secondary active transport is the single best answer for this question.

Solution to Question 18:

Exocytosis of neurotransmitters at the nerve terminals will be affected as a result of antibodies
against voltage-gated calcium channels.

After an action potential reaches the terminal endings of a nerve, the influx of Ca2+ via
voltage-gated calcium channels leads to the fusion of intracellular vesicles with cell membranes.
This results in the release of neurotransmitters into the synaptic space. This process will be
impaired in the case of Lambert-eaton syndrome.

49 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 50 ========================================
Exocytosis is the extrusion of membrane-bound vesicles. In exocytosis, intracellular vesicles move
to the cell membrane, fuse with it and then release their contents to the extracellular fluid. Solute
and water can be released from the cell via vesicles by the process of exocytosis.

Exocytosis can be:
Constitutive exocytosis:

* Involves prompt transport of proteins to the cell membrane in vesicles, with little or no

Solution to Question 19:

The non constitutive pathway of exocytosis is called the regulated pathway.
Exocytosis can be either constitutive or non constitutive(regulated):

* Regulated or nonconstitutive pathway: Proteins from the Golgi apparatus initially enter
secretory granules, where processing of prohormones to the mature hormones occurs before
exocytosis.

* Constitutive pathway: Involves the prompt transport of proteins to the cell membrane in
vesicles, with little or no processing or storage.

Solution to Question 21:

Vesicular transport has no relation to the concentration gradient.

Vesicular transport requires energy expenditure by the cell, so this is an active method of
membrane transport. Energy is needed to accomplish vesicle formation and vesicle movement

within the cell.

Solution to Question 22:

Transcytosis is seen in epithelial cells of intestine. Transcytosis is endocytosis across one

membrane of the cell followed by exocytosis across the opposite membrane.

Transcytosis across epithelia or endothelia: Some molecules, such as proteins, are too large to
diffuse through the membrane or cross the membrane transporters of epithelia. Instead they are

moved across epithelia by transcytosis.

Steps:

* The molecule is brought into the epithelial cell via receptor-mediated endocytosis.

52

Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 53 ========================================
* The resulting vesicle attaches to microtubules in the cells cytoskeleton and is transported across
the cell by vesicular transport.

« At the opposite side of the epithelium, the contents of the vesicle are expelled into the interstitial
fluid by exocytosis.

Significance:

¢ Transcytosis makes it possible for large proteins to move across an epithelium and remain
intact.

* It is the means by which infants absorb maternal antibodies in breast milk. The antibodies are
absorbed on the apical surface of the infant’s intestinal epithelium and then released into the
extracellular fluid. e

Solution to Question 23:

As exocytosis, begins > Tin intracellular Ca2+ > Ca2+interacts with a calcium sensing protein >
secretory vesicle docking and fusion — fused area of membrane opens, the vesicle contents diffuse
into the extracellular fluid while the vesicle membrane stays behind and becomes part of the cell
membrane.

Solution to Question 24:

Emeiocytosis requires Ca2+ directly.

Emeiocytosis is the movement of secretory granules to the plasma membrane of the cell, fusion of
the granule surface membrane with the cell membrane, rupture of membranes, and liberation of
granular contents into the pericapillary space.

Insulin secretion (as shown in the image below):

53 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 54 ========================================
* Glucose enters B cells by GLUT-2 transporters.
¢ It is phosphorylated and metabolized to pyruvate (Pyr) in the cytoplasm.

* The Pyr enters the mitochondria and is metabolized via the citric acid cycle. The ATP formed by
oxidative phosphorylation inhibits ATP-sensitive K+ channels, reducing K+ efflux.

* This depolarizes the B cell, and Ca2+ influx is increased. The Ca2+ stimulates the release of
insulin by exocytosis. Glutamate (Glu) is also formed, and this primes secretory granules,
preparing them for exocytosis.

It is promoted by Ca2+, T K+
It is inhibited by Mg2+, K+ depletion.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Equilibrium potential for an ion is calculated using the Nernst equation.

When there is a concentration gradient for an ion across the membrane, the ion tends to diffuse
down the concentration gradient. The electric potential that exactly opposes this net diffusion is
called the equilibrium potential for that ion. This is calculated using the Nernst equation.

59 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 60 ========================================
It is expressed as the potential inside the cell (intracellular potential relative to the extracellular
potential).

To calculate the value of the equilibrium potential:

Nernst equation

61 Concentration inside _
EMF (millivolts) = + — x log ————————— wok

Note: In this formula, the choice of concentration of the ion placed in the numerator and
denominator does not matter because the formula is onlysttsed to derive the numerical value and
not the sign ( +/- ). Mathematically, log4100/10) =1 and log (10/100) = - 1. Hence, changing the
numerator and denominator only changes the sig? ahd the numerical value remains the same.

This equation is used only to derive the numerical value of the potential. It is NOT used to derive
the sign/charge (+/-). i * fi tel
The sign/charge is derived from logi¢s;“
.
+ Ina positive (+) ion, if the conéeritration of the + ion is greater outside, then the + ion moves

from outside tolinside. Thevixitracellular potential becomes more + relatively. Hence, the potential
will be positive. tae

* In a positive (+) ion, if the concentration of the + ion is greater inside, then the + ion moves from
inside to outside. The intracellular potential becomes more - relatively (as + ion moves outside).
Hence the potential will be negative.

«In a negative (-) ion, if the concentration of the - ion is greater outside, then the - ion moves from
outside to inside. The intracellular potential becomes more - relatively. Hence the potential will be
negative.

* In a negative (-) ion, if the concentration of the - ion is greater inside, then the - ion moves from
inside to outside. The intracellular potential becomes more + relatively (as - ion moves outside).
Hence the potential will be positive.

Solution to Question 6: =~

lee |

The Nernst potential for K+ is -gomV.
Note:

* For K+ ions, the concentration gradient is directed outward and the electrical gradient is
directed inward.

* In general, all excitable cell membranes at rest are maximally permeable to K+ and thus their
RMP is close to equilibrium potential of K+ ion.

Solution to Question 7:

The ion with the highest positive equilibrium potential is Na+ that is +60mV.

64 Sold by @itachibot

Solution to Question 8:

The resting membrane potential (RMP) of a nerve fibre depends on the diffusion of both K+ and
Na+ and the Na+-K+ ATPase pump.

The most predominant factor, however, is the diffusion of K+ ions. This is because, at rest, the
permeability of the membrane of the nerve fibre to K+ ions is almost 100 times greater than that
to Na+.

Solution to Question 11:

The driving force is greatest for Ca2+.

Net driving force is the difference between the membrane potential and the equilibrium potential
for that particular ion. Hence Em - EX. Where:

Em = Actual membrane potential (mV) = -80mV

EX = Equilibrium potential for X (mV)

For the respective ions:

E Ca2+ = +120 ; Driving force = -80 — (120) = -200mV

E Na+ = +60 ; Driving force= -80 — (60) =-140mV

65 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 66 ========================================
E K+ = -90, Driving force = -80 — (-90) =10mV
E Cl-= -70, Driving force = -80 — (-70) =-10mV

Thus, Ca2+ will have the largest driving force and it will move inwards or into the cell.

Solution to Question 12:

The patch-clamp technique is used to record ion current flow through a single protein channel.

In the patch-clamp experiment, a small pipette is carefully maneuvered to seal off a portion of a
cell membrane. The pipette has an electrode bathed in an appropriate solution, thaF allows for the

recording of electrical changes through any pore in the membrane. o* 4

Solution to Question 13:

When the extracellular concentration of K+ is decreased (hypokalemia), there is increased
negativity of RMP.

RMP predominantly depends on the diffusion of K+ from inside to outside the cell. This diffusion
depends on the concentration gradient with the inside of the cell having a larger concentration of
K+ ions. When the extracellular concentration of K+ decreases, the concentration gradient for K+
rises. Therefore, more K+ ions diffuse out of the cell. This leaves the inside of the cell more
negative relative to the extracellular space. This increases the negativity of the RMP, leaving the
membrane hyperpolarised.

Solution to Question 5: “
The water content of lean body tissue is constant at 71-72 mL/100 g of tissue.

Lean body mass = Body mass - ( Adipose tissue fat mass + Bone Mass + Non-Adipose Tissue Fat
Mass)

Solution to Question 6:

The volume of a fluid compartment in the body can be measured by "indicator (dye)-dilution"
method. This method is based on the conservation of mass principle, which means that the total
mass of a substance after dispersion in the fluid compartment will be the same as the total mass
injected into the compartment.

Solution to Question 10: *
As the dye diffuses out of the capillaries, the measured concentration of the dye after equilibrium
will be less. So, as per the formula, the volume measured will be falsely high.
V = (Amount Injected - Amount Excreted) / Plasma concentration of dye

Detailed explanation:

* Dye x measures plasma volume:- dye x doesn't have the property of crossing blood vessel. If the
dye x have been used properly, for a normal person then the value would come around 3 L

¢ Dye y crosses capillaries, which means it can cross any blood vessel. This property is used to
measure the value of ECF. If the dye y is given, for a normal person then the value would come
around 14L

¢ Thus if dye y is given in place of x, then the value would be 14 instead of 3 which is a false and
the obtained value is high when compared to normal value

¢ Therefore the answer is falsely high.

Solution to Question 11:

Interstitial fluid volume is best measured using a combination of inulin and Evans blue.

Insterstitial fluid is measured indirectly. ECF minus plasma volume gives the interstitial fluid
value.

* Best measure of ECF - Inulin

* Best measure of Plasma Volume - Evans Blue (T1824)

Solution to Question 15:

1 equivalent of calcium is 20 g.
One equivalent is 1 mol of an ionized substance divided by its valency.

For calcium in solution, 1 mol is going to be 40g and its valency is 2. Therefore, 1 equivalent of
calcium is 40/2 = 20g.

Solution to Question 16:

85 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 86 ========================================
The concentration of osmotically active particles is usually expressed in osmoles. Normal plasma
osmolality is 280 to 290 mosmole/kg H2O

* Example

* NaCl dissociate into 2 particles- Na+ and Cl- in an ideal solution. Hence we can say that for every
1 mole of NaCl, there are 2 osmoles.

* If a solute is a non-ionizing compound such as glucose, the osmotic pressure is a function of the
number of glucose molecules present.

* NOTE: The body fluids are not ideal solutions, and although the dissociation of strong
electrolytes is complete, the number of particles free to exert an osmotic effect is reduced owing to
the interaction between the ions. Thus, 1mol of NaCl per litre body fluids contribute somewhat
less than 2 osmoles. The more concentrated the solution, the greater the deviation from the ideal
solution. 7 me

oO” rr, al
i

which is isotonic.) .s."" :

Note : gy ~_
*h en Aa
* Ifa cell is* placed into a hypotonic solution that has a lower concentration of impermeant solutes
(&lt;282 mOsm/L), water will diffuse into the cell, causing it to swell.

* If a cell is placed in a hypertonic solution having a higher concentration of impermeant solutes,
water will flow out of the cell leading to cell shrinkage.

Solution to Question 18: wok = ‘ Cj AS
Normal plasma osmolarity ( (~290'mOsmi/L) ) is mally due to to Naw: and its accompanying anions,
principally Cl- and HCO3;- cM *) a .%

* Osmolarity (mOsm/L) = 2 [Na+] ( cody ) +0. 055 [Glicose] (mg/dL) + 0.36 [BUN] (mg/dL)

¢ Because sodium and its associated anions (Ck@HCO3- ) account for about 94 per cent of the
solute in the extracellular ePiment plasma osmolarity (Posm) can be roughly approximated

as .
* Posm = 2.1 x Plas Sghdium co, ion
sm aR

* With a plasma'sédium coneghtration of 142 mEq/L, the plasma osmolarity would be estimated
from the fofmula above igibe about 298 mOsm/L.

* In conditions associated with renal disease, the contribution of two other solutes, glucose and
urea, should be included.

* Because urea easily permeates most cell membranes, it exerts little effective osmotic pressure
under steady-state conditions. (Ineffective osmoles)

* Contribution to plasma osmolarity:

¢ Na+ and associated anions: 270 mOsm/L
* Glucose : 5 mOsm/L

* Urea: 5 mOsm/L

¢ Plasma proteins: 2 mOsm/L

¢ Other ions: 8 mOsm/L

Solution to Question 19:

Proteins actually contribute only slightly to the total number of osmotically active particles (~1
mOsm) because of their low molar concentration and high molecular weight.

Protein concentration measured as g/L may be high. However, as their molecular weight is high,
the protein concentration in terms of moles per liter (molar concentration) will be low. Thus, they
contribute only slightly to the total number of osmotically active particles.

Detailed explanation: Terms like high, low are always used for comparison. Here we compare
plasma proteins and sodium (sodium is chosen because it contributes the maximum part of
osmolarity). Look at the values given below (just for understanding )

i. a
Molar concentration 0.75mosm/L(Low) 270mo0
sm/L
Molecular weight( 1 mole =) | 67,000 g(High) 23g
Cir ae
SK

The amount of water lost by’aweatin nish variable depending on physical activity and
environmental temperature. The volume of sweat hofmally is about 100 ml/day, but in very hot
weather or during heavy exengigaitid loss in.gweat occasionally increase to 1 to 2 L/hour.

Solution to Question’21: i
~4" >

i
Sweating Sloss ‘of water} Hyperosmotic volume contraction

* The osmolarity of ECF increases because sweat is hyposmotic (relatively more water than salt is
lost).

¢ ECF volume decreases because of the loss of volume in the sweat.

¢ Water shifts out of ICF; as a result of the shift, ICF osmolarity increases until it is equal to ECF
osmolarity, and ICF volume decreases.

¢ Plasma protein concentration increases because of the decrease in ECF volume. Although
hematocrit might also be expected to increase, it remains unchanged because of the water shifts
out of the RBCs, decreasing their volume and offsetting the concentrating effect of the decreased
ECF volume.

* So,Loss of hypotonic fluid causes hypertonic volume contraction.

Solution to Question 22:

88 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 89 ========================================
Calculated serum osmolality = 2 sodium + (glucose/18) + (BUN/2.8).

In the given clinical scenario, the patient with untreated type 2 diabetes mellitus has most likely
developed a hyperosmolar, hyperglycemic state. His serum osmolality will be significantly raised.

The principal determinants of osmolality of serum are the concentrations of sodium, glucose and
urea (blood urea nitrogen or BUN).

Osmolality is defined as a measure of the osmoles (Osm) of solute per kilogram of solvent
(osmol/kg or Osm/kg). The osmolality of the intracellular and extracellular fluids is maintained
between 290 and 310 mOsm in each compartment.

Solution to Question 25:

90 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 91 ========================================
In adrenal insufficiency there is hypo-osmotic volume contraction. Aldosterone deficiency leads to
increased sodium loss (loss of hyperosmotic fluid).

* The osmolarity of ECF decreases.
¢ ECF volume decreases because of the loss of volume.

* Water shifts into ICF; as a result of the shift, ICF osmolarity decreases until it is equal to ECF
osmolarity, and ICF volume increases.

* So, loss of hypertonic fluid causes hypotonic volume contraction.

Please note that in the diagram, the horizontal axis represents volume and vertical axis represents
osmolarity.

Solution to Question 26:

The total safety factor against edema is about 17 mmHg.
Summary of safety factors that prevent edema is:

* The safety factor caused by low tissue compliance in the negative pressure range is about 3 mm
Hg

¢ The safety factor caused by increased lymph flow is about 7 mm Hg
* The safety factor caused by washdown of proteins from the interstitial spaces is about 7 mm Hg

Therefore, the total safety factor against edema is about 17 mm Hg.

from macrophages outside the nervous system.

Solution to Question 3: . 5 ih

4 mh, Te J
In a spinal motor neuron; initiation of nerve impulse occurs in the axon hillock and initial
segment of the axon béeduse it has a’wety high density of voltage-gated Na+ and K+ channels.
Hence, this regionis:also knownjas the trigger zone for the action potential.
L t 5
Parts of a Netiren: on
* Cell body/perikaryon: contains many Nissl bodies.

* Dendritic zone where multiple local potential changes generated by synaptic connections are
integrated.

¢ Axon hillock: part of the cell body from which the axon arises.
* Initial segment: the first part of the axon where propagated action potentials are generated.

« An axonal process that transmits propagated impulses to the nerve endings. The unmyelinated
regions of a myelinated axon are termed as nodes of Ranvier.

¢ Synaptic knobs/nerve endings where action potentials cause the release of synaptic transmitters.

Note: Action potentials are generated at the initial segment in spinal motor neurons and the initial
node of Ranvier in cutaneous sensory neurons.

Solution to Question 5: 4O

In cutaneous sensory neurons, propagated action potentials are generated in the initial node of
Ranvier.

From a functional point of view, neurons generally have four important zones :

¢ A receptor, or dendritic zone, where multiple local potential changes generated by synaptic
connections are integrated

« A site where propagated action potentials are generated (the initial segment in spinal motor
neurons, the initial node of Ranvier in cutaneous sensory neurons)

« An axonal process that transmits propagated impulses to the nerve endings
¢ The nerve endings, where action potentials cause the release of synaptic transmitters

The cell body is often located at the dendritic zone end of the axon, but it can be within the axon
(eg, auditory neurons)

106 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 107 ========================================
Solution to Question 6:

The conduction velocity of a myelinated axon increases linearly with diameter.

Conduction velocity is increased by:

Increasing the fiber size: Increasing the diameter of a nerve fiber results in decreased internal
resistance; thus, conduction velocity down the nerve is faster.

Myelination: Myelin acts as an insulator around nerve axons and increases conduction velocity.
Myelinated nerves exhibit saltatory conduction. It is a rapid process that allows myélinated axons
to conduct up to 50 times faster than the fastest unmyelinated fibers. o 2

opening of more voltage-gated Na+ chanmels and further depolarization, setting up a positive
feedback loop. When the-Na+ channelslopen, Na+ moves into the cell down its electrochemical
gradient, driving the embrane potential toward the Na+ equilibrium potential.

Solution to Question 8:
The membrane conductance for potassium is maximum during repolarization (marked as point 3
in the above image).

Most of the increase in K+ conductance comes after the increase in Na+ conductance around the
time of repolarization.

Changes in membrane potential during an L
action potential hy az

lon permeability

Time (milliseconds)

— Action potential ——Na* ——K’
2]MARROW

ch a
‘a, Ss,

During the early porti Ss the actiomPotential, the sodium conductance is 1000 times greater
than potassium conductance. Therefore, far more sodium ions flow to the interior of the fiber than
do potassium.igns to the exterior. This causes the membrane potential to become positive at the
action potential onset. i"

When the membrane potential rises from —90 millivolts toward zero, this voltage change causes a
conformational change in the K+ channels which allows increased potassium efflux. Then, the
sodium channels begin to close and the potassium channels begin to open, and potassium
conductance begins to increase (and sodium conductance decreases).

The increased flow of K+ out of the cell causes membrane repolarization. The slow return of the
K+ channels to the closed state explains the after-hyperpolarization followed by a return to the
resting membrane potential.

Solution to Question 9:

Regarding the upstroke of a nerve action potential, there is a net inward current and the cell
interior becomes less negative. The direction of the current is either

* same as the direction of positive charge ( eg, Na+ )

108 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 109 ========================================
* opposite to the direction of negative charge (eg, Cl- )

* opposite to the direction of electrons

Since Na+ moves in, there is a net inward current.

Since the RMP increases, the cell interior becomes less negative.

In response to a depolarizing stimulus, some of the voltage-gated Na+ channels open and Na+
enters the cell and the membrane is brought to its threshold potential. The entry of Na+ causes
the opening of more voltage-gated Na+ channels and further depolarization, setting up a positive
feedback loop. The rapid upstroke in the membrane potential ensues. So, there is a net influx of
Na+, and the interior of the cell becomes positive during the upstroke of the action potential.

1

*
re

a i
Solution to ation 10: on
4 af
The slow return of the K+ channels to the closed state also explains the after-hyperpolarization.

In response to a depolarizing stimulus, some of the voltage-gated Na+ channels open and Na+
enters the cell. The entry of Na+ causes the opening of more voltage-gated Na+ channels and
further depolarization, setting up a positive feedback loop. The rapid upstroke in the membrane
potential ensues.

The membrane potential moves toward the equilibrium potential for Na+ (+60 mV) but does not
reach it during the action potential, primarily because the increase in Na+ conductance is
short-lived. The Na+ channels rapidly enter a closed state called the inactivated state and remain
in this state for a few milliseconds.

In addition, the direction of the electrical gradient for Na+ is reversed during the overshoot
because the membrane potential is reversed, and this limits Na+ influx; also the voltage-gated K+
channels open. These factors contribute to repolarization. The opening of voltage-gated K+
channels is slower and more prolonged than the opening of the Na+ channels. The net movement
of positive charge out of the cell due to K+ efflux at this time helps complete the process of
repolarization.

109 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 110 ========================================
The slow return of the K+ channels to the closed state also explains the after-hyperpolarization,
followed by a return to the resting membrane potential.

Thus, voltage-gated K+ channels bring the action potential to an end and cause closure of their
gates through a negative feedback process.

Note: Na+ channels: Positive feedback loop; K+ channels: Negative feedback loop.

Solution to Question 11: wok Ae Rs
L

The refractory period is the time’ period i in which a second stucfager impulse can elicit action
potential after the application of one stings! It can be absolute or relative.

During the rising and much of the falling phases of the spike potential, the neuron is refractory to
stimulation. This refractory period-is-divided i into afi

* Absolute refractory periody éorresponding to the period from the time the firing level is reached
until repolarization is about one-third depmplete

* Relative refractory, period, lasting from this point to the start of after-depolarization

During the. ahgbfute refractory"period, no stimulus, no matter how strong, will not excite the
nerve. However, during ihe relative refractory period, stimuli that are stronger than normal can
cause excitation.

The absolute refractory period for large myelinated nerve fibers is about 1/2500 second.

Solution to Question 12:

The relative refractory period starts from one-third of repolarization till the start of
after-depolarization.

The refractory period is a period during which another normal action potential cannot be elicited
in an excitable cell.

Solution to Question 13:

111 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 112 ========================================
The rheobase is an indicator of the magnitude of the current. It is the minimum intensity
(strength/magnitude) of stimulation required to generate an action potential when the stimulation
is of infinite duration.

Utilization time is the minimum time (duration) for which the rheobase has to be applied for the
excitation of tissue.

Chronaxie is the minimum time (duration) for which a stimulus double the strength of rheobase
has to be applied for excitation of tissue.

is "hy

Strength-duration curve

Intensity of stimulus

Duration of stimulus

A - Short duration, high-intensity stimulus
triggers action potential

C - Long duration, low-intensity stimulus
triggers action potential

©MARROW

Strength-duration curve gives the idea of the excitability of a tissue.

Strength-duration relationship arises because the same minimum electrical charge necessary to

excite an action potential can come from a current that is either brief but large or prolonged but
small. It is the product of strength and duration that determines excitability, and thus these two
parameters are inversely related in their effectiveness.

112 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 113 ========================================
Note: Chronaxie is a better index than rheobase and utilization time for comparing the excitability
of a tissue.

Solution to Question 14:

Slowly rising subthreshold currents fail to fire the nerve due to a process called accommodation,
which is a type of adaptation.

Accommodation is due to inactivation of voltage-gated Na+ channels. Due to the slow rise of
stimulus, only a few voltage-gated Na+ channels are activated at a time. These few channels are
inactivated by the time more channels are activated. So, at no moment, are a suffieient number of

Yh

channels activated to give an action potential. Me

When a membrane is depolarized rapidly, a large numberofNa+ channels-open at once, and an
action potential is produced. a, het

In contrast, decreased response to a long- lasting, dad sustained suprathreshold stimulus, in

general, is called adaptation. 1." ~ ’
. he na ra.

Solution to Question 15: x.

-_ k

x . bes a ty . eae
Type C fibers are the small unmyelinated nerve fibers:that conduct impulses at low velocities.
Greater the diameter of a given nerve fiber, the Greater its speed of conduction.

The nerve fibers are divided ito ‘types A (myelinated), B (myelinated), and C(Unmyelinated). The
type A fibers are furthes Su divided iat 8 , B, y, and 6 fibers.

1 Mi a,

Solution to Question 16: \"

The test performed here is used to check the proprioception. The large axons like A° nerve fibers
(12-20um) carry information regarding proprioceptive sensation and somatic motor function.

Solution to Question 17:
The type B fibers transmit the preganglionic autonomic fibers of both the sympathetic and
parasympathetic nervous systems.

Erlanger and Gasser classified the fibers into types A, B, and C, and the type A fibers are further
subdivided into ° , B, y, and 6 fibers.

* Type A fibers are the typical large- and medium-sized myelinated fibers of spinal nerves.

* Type B fibers are smaller fibers with minimal myelination. They transmit impulses of the ANS
including the bladder. (B for Bladder)

113 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 114 ========================================
* Type C fibers are the small unmyelinated nerve fibers that conduct impulses at low velocities.
They constitute more than one-half of the sensory fibers in most peripheral nerves, as well as all
the postganglionic autonomic fibers.

Solution to Question 18:
The reason for the loss of motor function without loss of pain sensation is that in the nerves to his
arm, A-fibers are more sensitive to pressure than C fibers.

Sleeping for a long period with the arms under the head puts pressure on a nerve. It leads to the
loss of conduction in large-diameter motor, touch, and pressure fibers, while Raiaysérisation
remains relatively intact. A-fibers (includes the motor fibers) are affected.

Solution to Question 22:

Biphasic Action Potential

* This type of record is obtained when both recording electrodes are placed on the surface of an
axon that has been stimulated.

* The record shows an upward deflection followed by an isoelectric interval and then a downward
deflection which denotes to the time taken for the electric potential to travel from one electrode to
another.

Solution to Question 26: “=~ te oY:

-h.- Le

id 2
Conduction in the chemical synapses-in CNS is always orthodromic.

When an action potential is,sét up in an axon, two impulses travelling in the opposite direction is

set up by depolarization oh either side of the initial current.
‘i bel as |
In the natural situation, impulse get in one direction only- from synaptic junctions to axon

terminals. Such conduction is.called orthodromic. Conduction in the opposite direction is called
antidromic, Under naturali¢onditions, impulse conduction is normally only orthodromic.
Antidromié:conduction i8-usually only seen when a nerve fibre is stimulated under artificial
conditions.

In a chemical synapse, conduction is always orthodromic. As the transmission in a chemical
synapse is due to the release of neurotransmitters from the presynaptic membrane which activate
receptors on the postsynaptic membrane, antidromic conduction (i.e. from the postsynaptic
membrane to presynaptic membrane) is not possible. Any impulse travelling in such direction
would die as it encounters the first synapse.

. Orthograde transport on

* Can be bi fast or slow axonal transport and it occurs along microtubules that run along the

¢ Fast axonal transport occurs at about 400 mm/day, and slow axonal transport occurs at 0.5 to 10
mm/day.

* Retrograde transport,

* In the opposite direction (from the nerve ending to the cell body), occurs along microtubules at
about 200 mm/day. Synaptic vesicles, nerve growth factor (NGF) and some viruses are
transported back to the cell body.

* Molecular motor involed in retrograde transport is Dyenin.

and the neuron is hyperpélarized. i *

Solution to Question 31:
Group III fibers carrying temperature, crude touch, and pricking pain sensations are 6-type A
fibers, not A-gamma type .
Alternative Classification Used in Sensory Nerves

¢ Group Ia. Fibers from the annulospiral endings of muscle spindles are ° -type A fibers in the
general classification.

* Group Ib. Fibers from the Golgi tendon organs are also ° -type A fibers.

¢ Group II. Fibers from most discrete cutaneous tactile receptors and from the flower-spray
endings of the muscle spindles are B- and y-type A fibers in the general classification.

121 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 122 ========================================
* Group III. Fibers carrying temperature, crude touch, and pricking pain sensations are 6-type A
fibers in the general classification.

* Group IV. Unmyelinated fibers carrying pain, itch, temperature, and crude touch sensations are
type C fibers in the general classification

Solution to Question 32:

Absolute refractory period is due to closure of inactive gates of sodium channel.

Voltage-gated sodium channels have 3 states that you can find them in. They can gither be closed,
open, or inactive. Si a

When the stimulus causes activation of the open gates, rae rushes into ths cell causing the
membrane potential to become more positive. After ae 64 millisecond, the inactive gate
automatically closes and no stimulus, no matter is ‘ong, can excite the nerve. This is called
the absolute refractory period. ‘s  %, s,

a f ae

Solution to Question 33:

Channel A: Na+, Channel B: K+
Na+ channels are fast activating and are responsible for depolarization phase of action potential.

K+ channels are slow activating and open for prolonged duration than the sodium channels. They
are responsible for repolarization phase of action potential. Also, the postassium channels return
slowly to their closed state, which is responsible for after-hyperpolarization.

Solution to Question 34:

The tetanizing frequency of the muscle, based on the given diagram, is 20 per second.

Tetanizing frequency = 1/contraction period. It is the frequency at which individual muscle fibres
twitch that fuse and cause sustained muscle contraction, where the contraction period is the time
taken from latent period to maximum force of contraction.

In this case, the time tracer was taken using a 100Hz tuning fork. Which means the tracer
recorded 100 waves per second.

h

Therefore, the duration of each wave in time tracer = 0.01 seconds. iS

In the image given in the question, between the point B (end of latent period) to point C
(maximum force of contraction), there are about 5 wanes) 4d nh,

Hence, the duration of contraction = 5 x 0.01 = 0,05,Seconds. _»=
hk ‘ a, he a
Substituting the value in the formula, we get;

Tetanizing frequency = 1/0.05 sec = 100/5.=.20/sec. t *,

dsc srengt feo

be =
E
g TTTTItT111T11tM1T ::
Tetanization:

The features of muscle contraction can be demonstrated by eliciting single muscle twitches. The
adding together of individual twitch contractions to increase the intensity of overall muscle
contraction is Summation. Summation occurs in two ways:

* by increasing the number of motor units contracting simultaneously, which is multiple Eber
summations.

¢ by increasing the frequency of contraction, which is called frequency summation and can lead to
tetanization.

When the frequency of a contraction reaches a critical level, the successive contractions eventually
fuse together, and the whole muscle contraction appears to be completely smooth and continuous,

124 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 125 ========================================
this is called tetanization. Beyond this point, a further increase in the frequency, will not result in
increased force of contraction.

Tetanization occurs in two ways:
* Complete tetanus occurs when no relaxation occurs between stimuli.

* Incomplete tetanus when periods of incomplete relaxation take place between the summated
stimuli.

Troponin is another regulatory protein. Troponin molecules are small globular units located at
intervals along the tropomyosin molecules. Each of the three troponin subunits has a unique
function:

* Troponin T binds the troponin components to tropomyosin.

* troponin I inhibits the interaction of myosin with actin.

======================================== PAGE 138 ========================================
* troponin C contains the binding sites for the Ca2+ that helps initiate contraction.

Solution to Question 5:

Actin, myosin, and tropomyosin are present in both skeletal muscle and smooth muscle. But
troponin is absent and calmodulin is present in smooth muscle.

Solution to Question 6:

The total length of each myosin filament is uniform, almost exactly 1.6 micrometers.

Solution to Question 7:

The thick filament or the myosin is made up of the heavy chain of myosin and light chain of
myosin.

The myosin molecule is composed of six polypeptide chains- Two heavy chains, each with a
molecular weight of about 200,000, and four light chains with molecular weights of about 20,000

th, a
each. o*

The two heavy chains wrap spirally around each other to oo a double Pex, which i is called the
tail of the myosin molecule.

One end of each of these chains is folded into a globular polypeptide structure called a myosin
head. These heads contain an actin-binding 'sife‘ahd a catalytic site that hydrolyzes ATP. Thus,
there are two free heads at one end of the double- helix, myosin molecule, ‘

The four light chains are also part of-the myosin head) two to eachhead. These light chains help
control the function of the head during muscle- contraction. eh o

aa hh i,

Band Band

The orderly arrangement of actin, myosin, and related proteins that produces this pattern. The
area between two Z-lines is called sarcomere.

The thick filaments, which are about twice the diameter of the thin filaments, are made up of
myosin, which are lined to form A bands; the thin filaments are made up of actin, tropomyosin,
and troponin. The array of thin filaments extends out of the A band and into the less dense
staining I bands.

The lighter H bands in the center of the A bands are the regions where, when the muscle is
relaxed, the thin filaments do not overlap the thick filaments. The Z lines allow for anchoring of
the thin filaments.

Actinin, titin and desmin are additional structural proteins.Titin connects Z-lines to the M line
and provides the scaffolding to the sarcomere. Desmin adds structure to Z-lines by connecting
Z-line to plasma membrane.

Solution to Question 15: , i"
he a |
When the action potential reaches the muscle fiber, there is influx of Ca2+ ions. This Ca2+ binds
to troponin C and tropomyosin is moved out of the way. Myosin heads can now bind to actin and
form so-called cross-bridges.

When a head attaches to an active site, this attachment simultaneously causes profound changes
in the intramolecular forces between the head and arm of its cross-bridge. This causes the head to
tilt toward the arm and to drag the actin filament along with it. This tilt of the head is called the
power stroke.

Immediately after tilting, the head then automatically breaks away from the active site.

Formation of cross-bridges is associated with hydrolysis of ATP and generation of force.

Sas coef
A feature of the myosin head tabi essentiqh iy uscle contrdetion is that it functions as an
adenosine triphosphatase (A! ase) enzyme, ee
Chemical Events during Muscle Cony ion eC
* Before contraction begins, the heads of the créss-bridges bind with ATP. The ATPase activity of
the myosin head immediatayy eaves the ATP but leaves the cleavage products, ADP plus
phosphate ion, bound to the ead. (Poi; fA)

¢ When the troponin-tropomyosi: comp ex binds with calcium ions, active sites on the actin
filament are uncovered and the yosin heads then bind with these sites. (Point B)
Naa!

* The bond’be een the aged of the cross-bridge and the active site of the actin filament causes a
conformational change in the head and the power stroke occurs. (Point C)

* Once the head of the cross-bridge tilts, the ADP and phosphate ion that were previously attached
to the head are released and a new ATP moelcule binds in that place. (Point D)

* The binding of the new ATP molecule causes detachment of the head of myosin from the actin.
(Point D)

* This cycle is then repeated.

Solution to Question 20:
Once the Ca2+ concentration outside the sarcoplasmic reticulum has been lowered sufficiently,
chemical interaction between myosin and actin ceases and the muscle relaxes.

* The SERCA pump uses energy from ATP hydrolysis to remove Ca2+ from the cytosol back into
the terminal cisterns, where it is stored until released by the next action potential.

¢ Thus during relaxation
* Ca2+ concentration in cytoplasmic decreases.
* Ca2+ concentration in sarcoplasmic reticulum increases.

Option a: Calcium ions do not move out of the cytoplasm. They are taken up by the sarcoplasmic
reticulum.

Solution to Question 21:

Activation heat is the heat that muscle produces whenever it contracts.
Heat Production in Muscle
* Resting heat: The heat given off at rest due to the basal metabolic processes.

* Initial heat: The heat produced in excess of resting heat during contraction. It is made up two
subtypes:

+ Activation heat: The heat that muscle produces whenever it is contracting, and. (=.
% “|

+ Shortening heat: The heat which is proportional in amount to the distance the ‘nuscle shortens.
Shortening heat is apparently due to some change in the e pabeture of the.muscle during
shortening. “5 =,

“s,

* Recovery heat: this is the heat liberated by the fetabolic processes that restore the muscle to
its precontraction state. The recovery heat of fawscle i is approximately equel to the initial heat.

* Relaxation heat: Heat released in a muse: that has contracted isotowlically to restore it to its
previous length. This extra heat is in-@ddition to reebvery heat. ah" .

ah fy
In a 100-m dash that takes LOWS}.85,% of the-energy consumed is derived anaerobically
by phosphocreatine, whic Is- -hydrolyzedto. creatine and phosphate groups with the release of

considerable energy. » sa

The ATP concentration ina ‘amu is low enough (4 millimolar) to sustain contractions for 1-2
seconds afterswhich ATP is.dephosphorylated into ADP. The ADP is re-phosphorylated by
phosphocteatine for the next 5 to 8 seconds.

ADP is re-phosphorylated by 3 sources.
* Phosphocreatine after 2 seconds upto 10 seconds.
* Glycolysis (anaerobic) after 10 seconds upto a minute

* Oxidative metabolism - after a minute upto 2-4 hours.

Solution to Question 23:

The length of the muscle fiber at the onset of contraction which is required to attain
maximum active tension is known as resting length.

Resting length is also known as the optimum length. It is about 2um. At this length, there is
a maximal overlap between the actin and myosin fibers and therefore, the maximum number of
cross-bridge binding sites are formed.

150 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 151 ========================================
Passive tension is the tension in a muscle when it is not stimulated. Total tension is the tension
developed when a muscle contracts isometrically in response to a maximal stimulus.

Active tension = Total tension - Passive tension

When the muscle fiber contracts, the active tension developed is proportional to the number of
cross-bridges between the actin and myosin fibers. Hence, at the resting length, maximum active
tension is produced and the muscle fiber contracts with a maximum force of contraction.

In the given image, the purple line represents the passive tension in the muscle (before
contraction) and its relation with the muscle length, while the red line represents the total tension
present (during contraction) and its relation with the muscle length.

Solution to Question 28:., \"
Each single motor neuron and the muscle fibers it innervates constitute a motor unit.
¢ The number of muscle fibers in a motor unit varies.

¢ In muscles concerned with fine, graded, precise movement, (eg. extraoccular muscles) each
motor unit innervates very few (three to six) muscle fibers.

¢ On the other hand, in human leg and back muscles, number of muscle fibers per motor unit can
be upto 600.

¢ Each spinal motor neuron innervates only one kind of muscle fiber, so that all the muscle fibers
in a motor unit are of the same type.

* On the basis of the type of muscle fiber they innervate, and thus on the basis of the duration of
their twitch contraction, motor units are divided into S (slow), FR (fast, resistant to fatigue), and
FF (fast, fatigable) units.

153 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 154 ========================================
Motor unit

Spinal cord

Motor
unit

Solution to Question 29: oh he re ra

The fast fatigable motor unit ig oid last, es ~

Each single motor neuron'and-the m ees it innervates constitute a motor unit. On the basis

of the type of muscle fiber they meee and thusoit the basis of the duration of their twitch
ae

contraction, motor units are we
* S (slow) os ee
¢ FR (fast, resistant il : a

¢ FF (fast, fatig its +s

S fibers have alow nena ratio (ie, small units) and FF fibers have a high innervation ratio
(ie., large'u its)

The recruitment of motor units during muscle contraction follows a general scheme, the size
principle.

+ In general, a specific muscle action is developed first by the recruitment of S muscle units that
contract relatively slowly to produce controlled contraction.

¢ Next, FR muscle units are recruited, resulting in more powerful response over a shorter period
of time.

* Lastly, FF muscle units are recruited for the most demanding tasks.

Solution to Question 31:

Tetanizing frequency = 1/contraction period = 1/40 ms = 25 Hz. *s, ‘by

Tetanizing frequency a ©, ~

¢ As the contractile mechanism does not have a refrac eviod, on Ss. stimulation of a
muscle, each new contraction occurs before the préteding one 1$,0 over. As a result, the second
contraction is added partially to the first, angle the total stréngth of contraction rises
progressively with increasing frequency. Sach. response ii is-called a tetanus-(tetanic contraction).

It is a complete tetanus when no relaxation_occurs between stimuli i

* Tetany occurs because enough cali! ions aréifatnitained in te muscle sarcoplasm throughout
the period of stimulation. a Ps: ’ a, :

i ak
Solution to Question 32: > Ae

Tetanic contraction ooguiiidue to poate accumulation of Ca2+ ions with rapidly repeated
stimulation of skeletahghuscle.

=e
+ With rapidly repeated stimulation, activation of the contractile mechanism occurs repeatedly
before an ‘relaxation has déceurred, and the individual contractions fuse into one continuous
contraction: Such a respoiise i is called a tetanus (tetanic contraction).

¢ A single action potential releases sufficient Ca2+ to cause a twitch contraction.

¢ However, the duration of this contraction is very short because Ca2+ is very rapidly pumped
back into the SR.

¢ If the muscle is stimulated a second time before the muscle is fully relaxed, there is progressive
accumulation of Ca2+ and the force of contraction increases. Thus, twitch forces are amplified as
stimulus frequency increases.

¢ At a high level of stimulation, intracellular Ca2+ increases and is maintained throughout the
period of stimulation, and the amount of force developed greatly exceeds that seen during a
twitch. The response is termed tetany.

Solution to Question, 35: r§ Oo
i

During theceontraction of the'skeletal muscle, the Ca2+ is released from the sarcoplasmic
reticulum.*Only statement,3 is correct.

Excitation contraction coupling refers to the process by which the membrane depolarization of the
muscle fibre (excitation) leads to the contraction of the muscle.

The steps of excitation-contraction coupling in skeletal muscle are as follows:

* Motor-neuron discharge causes the release of acetylcholine at the motor endplate, which binds
to the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors.

* This causes increased Na+ and K+ conductance in the endplate membrane, leading to the
generation of end-plate potential, which is a local potential.

* When the local endplate potential reaches a threshold for the muscle fibre, and an action
potential is generated.

¢ The generated action potential passes inward through the T tubules, leading to the spread of
depolarisation.

¢ The depolarization of the muscle fibre causes the release of Ca2+ from terminal cisterns of
sarcoplasmic reticulum and diffusion to thick and thin filaments.

158 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 159 ========================================
* The calcium released, binds to troponin C, uncovering myosin-binding sites on actin.

* The formation of cross-linkages between actin and myosin and sliding of thin on thick filaments
causes contraction of the skeletal muscle.

Steps in relaxation:

* The cytoplasmic calcium is pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum, by the action of
SERCA (sarcoplasmic endoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase).

* Once the calcium concentration is lowered, the interaction between actin and myosin ceases, and
the muscle relaxes.

When there is a decrease in extracellular calcium as in hypocalcemia, the voltage-gated sodium
channels are activated, which causes sodium influx. The i creased intraeellular sodium
concentration causes sustained depolarization, which nay lead to tetany.

depolarizing stimulus, which causes-tlté, release of calcium from thé.sarcoplasmic reticulum. The
increased calcium concentration-will eventually lead to muscle'contraction.

Solution to Question 1:

lower myosin ATPase activity than the ° isoform.

*° isoform predominates in the adult atria.

* B isoform predominates in the adult ventricles. wee"

Solution to Question 3:

Phosphorylation of phospholamban is responsible for the positive inotropic effect on the
myocardium observed after the stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system.

Phospholamban is a protein that regulates Ca2+ATPase in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. When
phosphorylated, phospholamban stimulates the Ca2+ ATPase, resulting in greater uptake and
storage of Ca2+ by the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

Increased Ca2+ uptake by the sarcoplasmic reticulum has two effects:
It causes faster relaxation (i.e., briefer contraction).

it increases the amount of stored Ca2+ for release on subsequent beats. e ;

This positive inotropic effect has three important features: increased peak tension, increased rate
of tension development, and faster rate of relaxation. ic

ain He Pe Ml

Faster relaxation means that the contraction (twitch). }is‘Shorter, allowing more time for refilling.

This effect is mediated via activation of 81 receptars: Which are,eoupled via a,Gs protein to
adenylyl cyclase. x i st

Activation of adenylyl cyclase leads to the Boduction ofCAMP, activation’ of protein kinases, and
phosphorylation of proteins that progeee the physiologic effect of jintreased contractility.

Solution to Question 4: pel ot

7 ie ty
* Phospholamban is a protein,that regulates SR Ca2+ ATPase (SERCA) in the sarcoplasmic
reticulum. In its dephosphorylated statesphospholamban inhibits the Ca2+ ATPase, resulting in
diminished uptake and. storage of Ca2+. iby the sarcoplasmic reticulum, while in its phosphorylated
state, there is disinhibition of the-Ca2+ ATPase leading to increased uptake and storage of Ca2+
by the sarcoplasnii¢ reticulums,
° Increaséd'€a2! uptake by the sarcoplasmic reticulum has two effects:
* It causes faster relaxation (i.e., briefer contraction).
* it increases the amount of stored Ca2+ for release on subsequent beats.

¢ Phosphorylation of phospholamban is responsible for the positive inotropic effect effect on the
myocardium observed after the stimulation of sympathetic nervous system.

* This effect is mediated via activation of B1 receptors, which are coupled via a Gs protein to
adenylyl cyclase.

* This positive inotropic effect has three important features: increased peak tension, increased rate
of tension development, and faster rate of relaxation.

¢ Faster relaxation means that the contraction (twitch) is shorter, allowing more time for refilling.

Solution to Question 5:

166 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 167 ========================================
Cardiac muscle cannot be tetanized due to long refractory period.

In cardiac myocytes, depolarization proceeds rapidly and an overshoot of the zero potential is
present. This is followed by a plateau before the membrane potential reaches the RMP (-gomV).
This plateau phase lasts for 200 msec or more. Repolarization is therefore not complete until
contraction is 50% completed.

Phases of cardiac action potential
* oO > Depolarisation and overshoot
* Due to opening of voltage-gated Na+ channels

be excited again, that is, itis)in its absolute refractory period. It remains relatively refractory until

phase 4. Thus, cardiac muscle cannot, be*tetanized,, at

Solution to Question 6:

The diagram displays the Length-Tension relationship for a cardiac muscle explaining the Starling
law of the heart.

167 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 168 ========================================
In the body, the initial length of the fibers is determined by the degree of diastolic filling of the
heart, and the pressure developed in the ventricle is proportional to the volume of the ventricle at
the end of the filling phase (Starling law of the heart).

* The developed tension increases as the diastolic volume increases until it reaches a maximum,
then tends to decrease.

* The decrease is due to beginning disruption of the myocardial fibers and not due to a decrease in
the number of cross-bridges between actin and myosin as seen in skeletal muscle.

Solution to Question 9:

Calmodulin is structurally and functionally related to troponin. In place of troponin, smooth
muscle cells contain a large amount of the regulatory protein calmodulin.

Calmodulin is similar to troponin; it is different in the manner in which it initiates contraction.
Calmodulin initiates contraction by activating the myosin light chain kinase. Troponin, on the
other hand, initiates contraction by causing a conformational change in the tropomyosin molecule
which then uncovers the active sites on the actin filament.

Solution to Question 10:

¢ Smooth muscles can maintain prolonged full tonic contraction with little use of energy (as little
as 1/300 the energy required for comparable sustained skeletal muscle contraction) and with little

169 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 170 ========================================
continued excitatory signal due to this mechanism.

* This is due to the fact that even after dephosphorylation of myosin, the cross-bridges continue to
cling on for some time.

* The excitation coupling mechanism occurring in smooth muscle is similar to the skeletal muscles
but for a few features:

* The process is slow and a low energy mechanism.

¢ There is no fixed length tension relationship as it shows the property of plasticity (smooth
muscles behaving like a viscous mass rather than a rigidly structured tissue).

* This mechanism is specially useful in vascular smooth muscles.

- iy a
a
~ as

Solution to Question 13:
Sympathetic stimulation (norepinephrine) of a smooth muscle produces inhibitory potentials and
relaxation.

Parasympathetic stimulation (or acetylcholine) causes depolarization and contraction in the
smooth muscle.

Smooth muscle can be divided into:

* Unitary smooth muscle - has low-resistance gap junctions between individual muscle cells and
functions in a syncytial manner

¢ Multiunit smooth muscle - made up of individual units with few or nil gap junctions.

Blood vessels have both unitary and multiunit smooth muscle in their walls.

170 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 171 ========================================
Option A: Unitary smooth muscle has an unstable membrane potential with continuous irregular
contractions independent of its nerve supply. This state of partial contraction is called tonus or
tone.

Option B: Dense bodies are present instead of Z lines in smooth muscle. These are bound to actin
filaments by ° -actinin.

Option C: Smooth muscle can exert variable tension at any given length. This property is called
plasticity. When a unitary smooth muscle is stretched, it first exerts increased tension. However, if
it is held at this length after stretching, the tension gradually decreases. This is referred to as
plasticity.

Tetanization Oo fat "

The features of miuscle contracti6n can be demonstrated by eliciting single muscle twitches. The
adding together, of individwal ‘twitch contractions to increase the intensity of overall muscle
contractioris Summation,Summation occurs in two ways:

* by increasing the number of motor units contracting simultaneously, which is multiple Hber
summations.

¢ by increasing the frequency of contraction, which is called frequency summation and can lead to
tetanization.

When the frequency of a contraction reaches a critical level, the successive contractions eventually
fuse together, and the whole muscle contraction appears to be completely smooth and continuous,
this is called tetanization.

Tetanization occurs in two ways:
* Complete tetanus occurs when no relaxation occurs between stimuli.

* Incomplete tetanus when periods of incomplete relaxation take place between the summated
stimuli.

Solution to Question 1:

Impulses are transmitted from one nerve cell to another cell at junctions called synapse. These are
the junctions where the axon or some other portion of one cell (the presynaptic eerie on

the dendrites, soma, or axon of another neuron or, in some cases, a muscle oppian cell (the

Solution to Question 4:

Nitric oxide is a neurotransmitter in the CNS.

Neurotransmitters are the substances that mediate chemical signaling between neurons. They can
be choline esters, biogenic amines, amino acids, and neuropeptides.

Two main classes of chemical substances that serve as neurotransmitters:

183 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 184 ========================================
¢ small-molecule neurotransmitter (acetylcholine)
* large molecule neurotransmitter (enkephalin)

They can act on ionotropic receptors (ligand-gated ion channels) and/or metabotropic receptors on
the postsynaptic membrane to produce a change in membrane potential to bring about their
action.

Criteria for the substance to be neurotransmitter:

* the substance must be demonstrated to be present in the presynaptic terminal.

* the cell must be able to synthesize the substance.

* should be released on depolarization of the terminal. _
* there should be specific receptors for it on the postsynaptic membrane. on a

¢ if the substance is applied exogenously to the postsynap; ti¢-membrane , atphysiologic
concentration, the response of the postsynaptic cell pe limic thes iPyivo response.

[5-HT]).

Solution to Question 6:
Neurotransmitter receptors that act through second messenger systems are called metabotropic
receptors.

Neurotransmitters bind to receptors on the post-synaptic membrane to activate ion channels in
one of the two ways:

* by gating ion channels directly (ionotropic receptors).

* by activating a second messenger (metabotropic receptor) for a prolonged postsynaptic neuronal
excitation or inhibition. Most commonly activated is a G-protein coupled membrane receptor.

The following four changes can occur usually after binding to a G-protein coupled receptor:

* Opening specific ion channels through the post-synaptic cell membrane.

184 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 185 ========================================
¢ Activation of cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) or cyclic guanosine monophosphate
(cGMP) in the neuronal cell.

¢ Activation of one or more intracellular enzymes.

¢ Activation of gene transcription. Activation of gene transcription is one of the most important
effects of activation of the second messenger systems because gene transcription can cause
formation of new proteins within the neuron, thereby changing its metabolic machinery or its
structure.

Solution to Question 10:

Neuromodulators typically have actions that are slower than neurotransmitters.

Neuromodulators are chemicals released by neurons that have little or no direct effects on their
own but can modify the effects of neurotransmitters.

They can modify the postsynaptic neuron’s response to a neurotransmitter and they can change
how much of a neurotransmitter the presynaptic neuron synthesizes or releases.

Neuromodulators also can be released by the presynaptic neuron along with the neurotransmitter.

muscle contraction.

high concentration at the neuromuscular junction.
