Solution to Question 13: ¢_! mh te ats
—- i a";

Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential ursP) Yis due to the’ influx of chloride ions (C1-).

If, binding of a neurotransmitteneauses the pdgtsinaptic membrane to become more negative
(hyperpolarized), it is called:an' inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP) because hyperpolarization
moves the membrane potential farther ‘from the threshold and makes the cell less likely to reach

an action potential, .%

a.

IPSP may be due to: at
*h an 7

* Increase in'Cl- influx. This action causes diffusion of negatively charged chloride ions from

outside the postsynaptic neuron to the inside, thereby carrying negative charges inward and

increasing the negativity inside. This leads to hyperpolarization.

* Increase in K+ efflux. This action causes positive ions to diffuse to the exterior, which causes
increased negativity inside the neuron, which leads to hyperpolarization.

¢ Activation of receptor enzymes that increase the number of inhibitory synaptic receptors or
decrease the number of excitatory receptors.

Solution to Question 14:

Activation of receptor enzymes that increase the number of inhibitory synaptic receptors can
cause the effect shown, namely, the generation of IPSP.

In the figure given, there is an increased negativity in the motor neuron following the action
potential in the interneuron. So, the synaptic potential is IPSP. This can be caused at the

======================================== PAGE 190 ========================================
postsynaptic membrane due to Cl- influx, K+ efflux or activation of receptor enzymes that increase
the number of inhibitory synaptic receptors or decrease the number of excitatory receptors.

Other options:
* Opening of chloride ion channels through the post-synaptic neuronal membrane.
* Increase in conductance of potassium ions to the outside of the postsynaptic neuron.

¢ Increase in conductance of sodium ions inside the neuron causes the generation of an EPSP.

Solution to Question. 20: Pa
mis, Mieg!

ms

Treatment\with\AChE inhibitors (e.g., neostigmine) prevents the degradation of ACh and prolongs
the action of ACh at the muscle end plate.

Myasthenia gravis:
* It is caused by the presence of antibodies to the ACh receptor.

* Is characterized by skeletal muscle weakness and fatigability resulting from a reduced number of
ACh receptors on the muscle end plate.

* The size of the EPP (end plate potential) is reduced.

* Treatment with AChE inhibitors (e.g., neostigmine) prevents the degradation of ACh and
prolongs the action of ACh at the muscle end plate, partially compensating for the reduced
number of receptors.

Solution to Question 21:

193 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 194 ========================================
Denervation supersensitivity develops in the muscle after the nerve supplying it is cut. Since the
nerve to her right dilator pupillae is damaged, the right eye becomes excessively sensitive to the
sympathomimetic drug. So, the right eye becomes much more dilated than the left.

Denervation Supersensitivity:

¢ When the motor nerve to skeletal muscle is degenerated, the muscle gradually becomes
extremely sensitive to acetylcholine. This is called denervation hypersensitivity or supersensitivity.

¢ Normally nicotinic receptors are located only in the vicinity of the motor endplate where the
axon of the motor nerve terminates. When the motor nerve is severed, there is a marked
proliferation of nicotinic receptors over a wide region of the neuromuscular junction.

¢ Denervation supersensitivity also occurs at autonomic junctions. Smooth muscle; unlike skeletal
muscle, does not atrophy when denervated, but it becomes hyperresponsive t to ‘the Chemical
mediator that normally activates it. ’

7 F
a A

* This hyperresponsiveness can be demonstrated by using pharmacologic tools rather than actual
nerve section. Prolonged use of a drug such as reserpiné can be used to deplete transmitter stores
and prevent the target organ from being exposed to norepinephrine for an extended period. Once
the drug usage is stopped, smooth muscle and’ cardiac musclé-will be supersensitive to subsequent

release of the neurotransmitter. is af", € 4"

b ori, He “i, ,
Option a) If the symapthetic nerve*wotld have régtfedated, both-tight and left eye would have
been equally sensitive to the drug and so, would, have dilatedecfptaly.

Option b) Parasympathetie nerves supplythe spinchter pilpillae muscle. So, if stimulated they
would constrict the eye not dilate it. Also, the drug added was a sympathomimetic agent. So,
parasympathetic nerves are not stinfulated i in the first place

Le

Option d) If the left eye was, dathaged, it wotild“also have denervation supersensitivity. Again, both
eyes would dilate equally, but more than that of normal.

Solution to Question 22:.,

Neurotransmitters should be released on depolarization of the terminal.
¢ Neurotransmitters are the substances that mediate chemical signaling between neurons.

¢ The transmission of information at chemical synapses involves the release of neurotransmitter
from a presynaptic cell, diffusion across the synaptic cleft, and binding of the neurotransmitter to
specific receptors on the postsynaptic membrane to produce a change in membrane potential.

Criteria for the substance to be neurotransmitter

* the substance must be demonstrated to be present in the presynaptic terminal
* the cell must be able to synthesize the substance.

¢ should be released on depolarization of the terminal.

* there should be specific receptors for it on the postsynaptic membrane.

¢ if the substance is applied exogenously to the postsynaptic membrane at physiologic
concentration, the response of the postsynaptic cell must mimic the in vivo response

Solution to Question 27:

In electrical synapses, the cytoplasm of adjacent cells is directly connected by gap junctions
(connexons) that allow free movement of ions from the interior of one cell to the interior of the
next cell.

The transmission in electrical synapses is bidirectional and there is no synaptic delay. Unlike
chemical synapses, electrical synapse has cytoplasmic continuity.

196 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 197 ========================================
Note:

Most of the synapses used for signal transmission in the central nervous system are chemical
synapses.

Solution to Question 3:

The neurotransmitter released by A delta fibers, which carry sharp pricking pain, is glutamate.

The substance released by C fibers, which carry diffuse, chronic, burning pain is substance P.

Solution to Question 4:

The most important neurotransmitter of reward pathway is dopamine.

203 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 204 ========================================
Reward pathway (mesolimbic pathway) connects the ventral tegmental area in the midbrain to the
nucleus accumbens and limbic subcortical areas. This pathway is involved in reward behaviour,
addiction and in psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia.

Solution to Question 8: 4" we

\

The hyperkinetic fe tinee of Huntington's disease is due to the loss of intrastriatal GABAergic and
cholinergic neyrons. The loss f'the GABAergic pathway (which is an inhibitory pathway) to the
globus pallidtis‘external segment and loss of cholinergic neurons in many parts of the brain

decreases ta ibition, catising the hyperkinetic feature of the disease to develop.

Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant trinucleotide repeat disorder with multiple CAG
repeats. The abnormal gene responsible for the disease is located on chromosome 4.

Solution to Question 9:

Botulinum toxin prevents the release of Ach at the presynaptic membrane.

Botulinum toxins A, B, and E are toxic to humans. They cleave components of SNARE - a complex
of proteins necessary for the exocytosis of acetylcholine. Toxins A and E cleave SNAP-25 whereas
toxin B cleaves vesicle-associated membrane protein (VAMP) also known as synaptobrevin.

Cleavage of SNARE proteins prevents the release of acetylcholine as the vesicles containing Ach
are unable to bind to the presynaptic membrane.

205 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 206 ========================================
Solution to Question 10:

Tetanus toxin affects the release of GABA.

Tetanus toxin escapes normal lysosomal degradation and crosses the synapse to the GABA-ergic
presynaptic inhibitory interneuron terminals. The toxin blocks the release of inhibitory
neurotransmitters (glycine and gamma-aminobutyric acid) across the synaptic cleft. This blockage
of the inhibitory neurotransmitters causes spastic paralysis.

Solution to Question 12:

Acetylcholine increases during REM sleep.

In REM sleep, there is increase in acetylcholine and decrease in norepinephrine and serotonin.
The reverse of this pattern leads to wakefulness.

Acetylcholine Increased
Serotonin Decreased
Norepinephrine Decreased

Solution to Question 13:

Sweat glands are innervated by sympathetic cholinergic fibres (not adrenergic fibres).

Pre-ganglionic fibres of the sympathetic nervous system, pre-ganglionic fibers of the
parasympathetic nervous system and post-ganglionic parasympathetic fibres release acetylcholine.

Post-ganglionic sympathetic fibres release noradrenaline in most of the sites, except in certain
places such as sweat glands, piloerector muscles and blood vessels in some skeletal muscles where

acetylcholine is released. i,

Solution to Question 2:

Fast vibration sense is mediated by Pacinian corpuscles.

Pacinian corpuscle is the most sensitive mechanoreceptor in the somatosensory system. It is
responsive to high-frequency (30-500 Hz) vibratory stimuli and pressure. They are rapidly
adapting receptors.

Option A: Merkel's disc sense touch and sustained pressure. They are slow adapting receptors.

Present in epidermis.
mJ

Option B: Meissner's corpuscle sense tap and flutter. They are present in der Ipapillae. They
are rapidly adapting receptors.

Option D: Ruffini endings sense skin stretch and futeyil vibration a

Solution to Question 3:

Meissner's corpuscles are phasic (rapidly adapting) receptors.

Merkel's disc, Ruffini's end organs, and muscle spindle are tonic (slowly adapting) receptors.

Solution to Question 4:

The distance of two-point discrimination is greatest at the back of the scapula.

217 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 218 ========================================
The relative tactile acuity of a given region can be determined by the two-point discrimination
test. Here, calipers are used to determine the minimum distance between the two points which
can be perceived as two points. Greater the density of receptors > Smaller the receptive field >
More precise the localization.

For e.g., Stimulus points on the back, for instance, must be separated by at least 65 mm before
they can be distinguished as separate, whereas on the fingertips two stimuli are recognized if they
are separated by as little as 2 mm. The lips have the greatest acuity (two-point discrimination
threshold ~ 1 mm).

Clinical significance:

Blind individuals benefit from the tactile acuity of fingertips to facilitate the ability to read

Braille. The dots forming Braille symbols are separated by 2.5 mm. my, *

Two-point discrimination is used to test the integrity of the.dorsal column nfedial lemniscus)
a

system. ww i, %
™

Solution to Question 5:

The sensory receptor shown in thesabove image is phcinian corpustle. The Pacinian corpuscle is a
rapidly adapting receptor andi is an encapsulated receptor. ¢!

When a stimulus of the same Sréhoth i is eontinuously given’ to a sensory receptor, the frequency
of the action potentials genérated by thésreceptor gradually decreases. This phenomenon is known
as receptor adaptation. Depending .gn- “whether the’ receptor stops responding to a stimulus rapidly
or slowly, they are classified as-rapidly adapting’ (phasic) or slow adapting receptors (tonic).
* Nw a %
oy al

i ‘
7

Solution to Question 6: is

Mechanorécéptors and exteroceptors have the same classification of receptors.

Exteroceptors are concerned with the external environment or receptors which receives external
stimuli. Mechanoreceptors are cutaneous receptors for touch and pressure. Thus
mechanoreceptors can be considered to be exteroceptors.

Nociceptors are pain receptors which detect physical and chemical damage occuring in the tissue.
Interoceptors are concerned with the internal environment.

Chemoreceptors are receptors stimulated by a change in the chemical composition of the
environment in which they are located. These include receptors for taste and smell as well as
visceral receptors such as those sensitive to changes in the plasma level of 02, pH, and osmolality.

Teleceptors (distance receivers) are concerned with events at a distance, example -
photoreceptors.

Proprioceptors provide information about the position of the body in space at any given instant.

218 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 219 ========================================
Solution to Question 7:
The frequency of a train of action potentials in an afferent fibre reflects the intensity of the
stimulus given to its receptor.
Intensity of stimulus applied to a sensory receptor can be determined by the following:
¢ Variation in frequency of action potentials generated
¢ Variations in the number of receptors activated

Option A : Weber-Fechner Law states that magnitude of sensation felt is directly proportional to
the log of the intensity of the stimulus.

Option B: A touch receptor will not generate a constant rate of discharge, even if thé, receptor is
continuously stimulated. This is called desensitization or adaptation. If the Ks site were true (if
option B was correct), when we have our clothes on, we would have constant ring of neurons

unnecessarily, in response to touch stimulus. ach o

Solution to Question 9:

Bradykinin is the most potent algogenic substance.

An algogenic substance is a chemical released at the site of injury which mediates pain. These
include bradykinin, serotonin, histamine, potassium ions, acids, acetylcholine, and proteolytic

enzymes. le
Prostaglandins and substance P enhance the sensitivity of pain endings bu do not directly excite

Solution to Question 12:

Weber-Fechner Law states that the magnitude of sensation felt is proportionate to the log of the
intensity of the stimulus.

Other Laws in Physiology:

* Doctrine of specific nerve energies - When the nerve pathways from a particular sense organ are
stimulated, the sensation evoked is that for which the receptor is specialized no matter how or

where along the pathway the activity is initiated. he

* Law of projection - No matter where a particular sensory pathway is stimulated along its course
to the cortex, the conscious sensation produced is referred-to the location Sie the receptor. This

principle is called the law of projection. a

Solution to Question 13: hae ane ‘ a.

This patient is having phantom estrbatn. This pean is based.on Brtical plasticity.

Solution to Question!14: a,"
rus,

Perceptioh Sf nérmal sensory stimuli as painful is allodynia.

Hyperalgesia is an exaggerated response to a noxious stimulus. An example of primary
hyperalgesia is the extreme sensitivity of sunburned skin, which results from sensitization of the
skin pain endings by local tissue products released due to burns — histamine, prostaglandins, and
others.

Causalgia is a type of neuropathic pain.

Solution to Question 15:

There are anandamide-containing neurons in the periaqueductal gray and other areas concerned
with pain.

There are 2 types of endogenous cannabinoids: 2-arachidony] glycerol and Anandamide. They
bind to CB1 and CBz2 receptors. They have an anti-nociceptive effect.

221 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 222 ========================================
Descending pathways from the mesencephalic periaqueductal gray inhibit transmission in
nociceptive pathways. This descending pathway includes a synapse in the ventromedial medulla
(Raphe nucleus) and the release of endogenous opiates. So periaqueductal gray matter releases
endogenous opiates.

Solution to Question 16:

Visceral pain is poorly localized, unpleasant and associated with nausea and autonomic
symptoms. It often originates from thoracic and abdominal cavities, transmitted through small
type C pain fibers. 2 5

It often radiates (or is referred) to other somatic structures perhaps due to convergence of somatic
and visceral nociceptive afferent fibers on the same second*order neurons{in the spinal dorsal
horn that project to the thalamus and then to the pri omatosensoby cortex.

L
When pain is referred, it is usually to a structure ee developed.from tthe same embryonic
segment or dermatome as the structure in al e pain cpieipates. This principle is
called the dermatomal rule. 4 ae

Example: the heart and the arm haverthe same sue) origin. e

Solution to Question 17:

Alveoli are pain insensitive structures. Alveoli of lungs and parenchyma of liver are almost
completely insensitive to the pain of any type.

However, the liver capsule is extremely sensitive to both direct trauma and stretch. The bile ducts,
arterial walls, bronchi and the parietal pleura are very sensitive to pain.

Solution to Question 18:

Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) of pain relief is based on the gate theory of
pain. This method uses electrodes to activate A° and AB fibers near the injury to relieve pain.

Gate control theory operates at the substantia gelatinosa of the dorsal horn of the spinal cord.

222 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 223 ========================================
The relief may result from inhibition of pain pathways (Inhibition of C fibers and A-delta fibers) in
the dorsal horn gate, by stimulation of large-diameter touch-pressure afferents(A-beta fibers). This
is called the gate-control hypothesis.

Interesting clinical application: Touching or shaking an injured area decreases the pain due to the
injury. Stimulation with an electric vibrator at the site of pain also gives some relief.

The same mechanism is probably responsible for the efficacy of counterirritants. Stimulation of
the skin over an area of visceral inflammation produces some relief of the pain due to the visceral
disease. The old-fashioned mustard plaster works on this principle.

Solution to Question,19: ra

4,
Hyperalgesia ‘stan exaggerated response to a noxious stimulus.

° Hyperalgesia and allodynia signify increased sensitivity of nociceptive afferent fibers.
* Causes of hyperalgesia

* The excessive sensitivity of the pain receptors, which is called primary hyperalgesia

¢ Facilitation of sensory transmission, which is called secondary hyperalgesia.

An example of primary hyperalgesia is the extreme sensitivity of sunburned skin, which results
from sensitization of the skin pain endings, by local tissue products from the burn — histamine,
prostaglandins, and others.

Secondary hyperalgesia frequently results from lesions in the spinal cord or the thalamus.

Solution to Question 20:

At normal skin temperatures, TRPV3 is the most active receptor.

Solution to Question 21:

In the given clinical scenario rectal temperature would be the preferred site to measure the core
body temperature.

During low or high temperatures like in Siachen, the axillary and the oral temperatures are highly
misleading and should not be used to measure the core body temperatures

Infrared tympanic thermometer measures the temperature of the eardrum which is representative
of core body temperature. However, more often the thermometer is not inserted adequately or for
safety reasons, they are manufactured in such a way that they do not reach deep into the ear
canal, or the ear canal contains cerumen which can disturb the measurement:fence the infrared
thermometer more commonly delivers the temperature ona canal cerumen and hence not a
reliable method of measuring the core body temperature, ' a

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:
The principle that in the spinal cord the dorsal roots are sensory and the ventral roots are motor is
known as the Bell—Magendie law.
Laws in Sensory Physiology

* Muller's doctrine of specific nerve energies: When the nerve pathways from a particular sense
organ are stimulated, the sensation evoked is that for which the receptor is specialized, no matter
how or where along the pathway the activity is initiated.

231 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 232 ========================================
* Law of projection: No matter where a particular sensory pathway is stimulated along its course
to the cortex, the conscious sensation produced is referred to the location of the receptor.

* Weber-Fechner Law: Magnitude of sensation felt is directly proportional to the log of the
intensity of the stimulus.

* Labelled line theory: All the sensations from the different parts of the body travel along specified
paths. For example, in the posterior column, fibers from the lower parts of the body are placed
medially.

Solution to Question 2: ei

project to the somatosensory region of the cerebral. cort X, r;

and the postcentral gyrus is intact. Still, the, ,pefSon cannot perceive the sensation, so the pathway
is broken between the thalamus and the gyrus, which, His to be at the tertiary neuron.

Solution to Question 3:

Decussation of ascending spinal pathways occurs mainly within the spinal cord and medulla
oblongata. Most of the ascending pathways and tracts do decussate or cross; however, some
remain uncrossed.

Dorsal column pathway crosses in the medulla oblongata.

The anterior and lateral spinothalamic tract crosses obliquely in the spinal segments.

Solution to Question 5:

The dorsal column—medial lemniscal system is composed of large, myelinated nerve fibers and
thus transmit signals to the brain, at high velocities of 30 to 110 m/sec.

Option B: Dorsal column carries fine touch and anterolateral column carries crude touch

Solution to Question 6:

Processing of tactile stimulation occurs in Brodmann’s area in 3, 1, 2.

The human cerebral cortex is divided into about 50 distinct areas called Brodmann’s areas based
on histological structural differences.

Areas 3, 1, 2 which constitute primary somatosensory area I, and areas 5 and 7, which constitute
the somatosensory association area process sensory information like tactile stimulation.

Solution to Question 8:

Somatosensory area has the largest representation for lips.

The relative sizes of the cortical receiving areas of the sensory homunculus are proportionate to
the use of the part. The sizes of these areas are directly proportional to the number of specialized
sensory receptors in each respective peripheral area of the body.

Some body parts are represented by large areas in the somatic cortex—the lips the greatest of all,
followed by the face and thumb, whereas the trunk and lower part of the body are represented by
relatively small areas.

Solution to Question 9: ~
Ch rg, “Fae

*h en
In this casé, injury to the-lateral portion of the postcentral gyrus in the right hemisphere would
result in loss of sensation in the left facial area.

Each hemisphere receives somatic sensory information from the opposite side of the body in the
postcentral gyrus (somatosensory area).

The lateral portion of the postcentral gyrus receives somatic sensory information from the facial
and neck region while the medial portion of the postcentral gyrus received somatic sensory
information from the trunk and appendages.

Pain and temperature are Kot affected both in quality and intensity in the lesion of the

Solution to Question 11:
This patient most likely has Brown-Sequard syndrome. It is characterized by loss of pain and
temperature on the opposite side.

Hemisection of the spinal cord causes damage to ascending sensory (dorsal column pathway,
ventrolateral spinothalamic tract) and descending motor (corticospinal tract) pathways, which is
called the Brown-Séquard syndrome.

The lesion to fasciculus gracilus or fasciculus cuneatus leads to ipsilateral loss of discriminative
touch, vibration, and proprioception below the level of the lesion.

236 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 237 ========================================
The loss of the spinothalamic tract leads to contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation
beginning one or two segments below the lesion.

Damage to the corticospinal tract produces weakness and spasticity in certain muscle groups on
the same side of the body.

Solution to Question 12:

A patient with Brown Sequard syndrome will have a loss of vibration sense below the lesion on the
same side.

Brown-sequard syndrome is caused due to hemisection of the Spinal cord. It can be caused by
fracture-dislocation of the vertebral column, by a bullet or stab wound, or 3 an expanding tumor.
Clinical features: at os, ty -

Solution to Question 13: s ee )
hes ‘a
Itch-specific fibers, have been demonistrated i in the anterolateral spinothalamic tract. They are
carried by unmyelinated nerye fiber C. Itching can be produced by repeated local mechanical
stimulationof ‘skin, variety, Of chemical agents including histamine and kinins released in the skin

in responsé to tissue damage

Simple scratching relieves itching because it activates large fast conducting afferents (AB) that gate
the transmission in the dorsal horn in a manner analogous to inhibition of pain by stimulation of
similar afferents (Gate control theory).

Solution to Question 14:

Endogenous opioids, a group of neuropeptides that includes

beta-endorphin, dynorphins, and enkephalins.

Opioids cause both presynaptic and post synaptic inhibition reducing excitatory post-synaptic
potential (EPSP). Opioids are released from the descending analgesic pathway and bind with

opiate receptors on the afferent pain-fiber terminal. This binding suppresses the release of
substance P, blocking further transmission of the pain signal.

237 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 238 ========================================
Solution to Question 15:

Pain relief in acupuncture is by the release of endorphin.

The acupuncture endorphin hypothesis is the primary mechanism of acupuncture analgesia.
According to this hypothesis, Acupuncture is thought to activate afferent neurons leading to spinal
cord and midbrain centers that release endogenous opioids implicated in pain relief.

Solution to Question 18:

Solution to Question 19:

Pain and temperature in thalamus are controlled by the ventral posterior lateral nucleus. Pain and
temperature is carried by anterolateral spinothalamic tract.

Fibres from anterolateral spinothalamic tract and dorsal column synapse in the ventral
posteriorlateral (VPL) nucleus of thalamus.

afferents from mammillothalamic tract. It sends efferents to cingulate gyrus.

Option D: Intralaminar nucleus participates in arousal reaction, it recieves afferents from reticular

formation of brain stem.

Solution to Question 22:

Lesion in the medial lemniscus at the level of pons causes loss of tactile and proprioception on the
opposite side.

Fibers of medial lemniscus ascend ipsilaterally in the dorsal columns of the spinal cord to the
medulla, where they synapse in the gracilus and cuneate nuclei.

The second-order neurons from these nuclei cross the midline and ascend in the medial lemniscus
to end in the contralateral ventral posterior lateral (VPL) nucleus and related specific sensory
relay nuclei of the thalamus. % -: '

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Light passes first through the retinal ganglion layer, the most anterior/inner layer of the retina.
Light then passes through other layers of the retina until it reaches the photoreceptors in the
posterior/outer region of the retina.

Retina is organized into 10 layers or zones where distinctive components are clustered together to
form continuous strata.

¢ Light passes in the direction: Vitreous humor(anterior)— choroid(most posterior) encountering
the cell layers in that direction (ganglion cell layer—photoreceptors) ce"

* Visual processing/Phototransduction occurs in the opposite direction (PhgeKaceptors> ganglion
cell layer) +
mm,

Gangtion cen

3
a

Solution to Question 2:

Initiation of visual impulse is associated with photoisomerisation and hydrolysis of visual purple

Rhodopsin or visual purple, the photosensitive pigment, is composed of opsin (a protein
belonging to the superfamily of G protein-coupled receptors) and retinal (an aldehyde of vitamin
A). When light strikes the photoreceptors, retinal is chemically transformed in a process called
photoisomerization, which begins the transduction process.

Solution to Question 4:

Central fovea (foveola) contains only cones (rod-free area) and is a region of highest resolution.

Solution to Question 6:

Photoreceptors - rods and cones are the first-order neurons in the visual pathway.

Solution to Question 7:

The responses of the rods, cones, and horizontal cells are hyperpolarizing, and the;fesponses of
the bipolar cells are either hyperpolarizing or depolarizing, whereas amacrine-¢ells produce purely
depolarizing potential. "

Solution to Question 8: ak? << WI
le = i a, "a
Amacrine cells release acetylcholine. _ = = 4 1,

«‘

Amacrine cells are one of the nefironal cell types of the retina. They transmit signals in two
directions, either directly from*bipolar cells.t6.ganglion cells or horizontally within the inner
plexiform layer from axons Of the bing ets to dendrites of the ganglion cells or to other
amacrine cells. "

Amacrine cells release GABA, , giite, dopamine, acetylcholine &amp; indolamine.
Other neuronal cell types .¢ the retina are: *

* The photoreceptorsxirods &amp; cones

* Horizontal cells a ~

* Bipolar ealls a

* Ganglion cells

a

Of these cell types, only neurotransmitters secreted by photoreceptors &amp; amacrine cells have
been discovered. Rods and cones release glutamate at their synapses with the bipolar cells.

Solution to Question 9:

All neural elements in the retina exhibit local graded potentials except ganglion cells which shows
action potential.

Solution to Question 132.)
Protanopes are dichromats with difficulty in identifying red color.
¢ Anomalous Trichromatic Vision

* Deficiency of red is Protanomaly.

* Deficiency of green is Deuteranomaly.

* Deficiency of blue is Tritanomaly.

¢ Dichromatic Vision (Individuals with only two cone systems)

¢ Blindness to red color Protanopia (Protanope).

¢ Blindness to green color Deuteranopia (Deuteranope).

¢ Blindness to blue color Tritanopia (Tritanope).

* Monochromatic Vision Have only one cone system.

* Color blindness is X-linked recessive.

252 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 253 ========================================
¢ Achromatopsia/ Achromasia- Color blindness is due to lesions of area V8 of the visual cortex.

Solution to Question 14:

The medial geniculate nucleus is the thalamic nucleus that conveys auditory information from the
brainstem to the primary auditory cortex.

Lateral geniculate nucleus is the thalamic nucleus that conveys visual information.

Solution to Question 15: e*

The superior olivary nuclei receive information from hotldars and begin’ the process of detecting

the direction from which a sound comes. Ti ,

The eighth nerves reach their respective cohlear, ntrlei in the niedulla from where the nerves
travel to both the ipsilateral and contralteral'superior olivaby nuclei in the ‘pons and hence giving
it the ability to detect the direction of the sound. i,

a rat hy,
The lateral part of superior olivary’ ipicltus does eitby comparingthe difference in intensities of
sound reaching the two ears, whereas the medial part of the superior olivary nucleus detects the
time lag between the signals entering thestwo ears. i

Solution to Question 16: os!
Jas "Haag i 4 q .
There are at least thréeways that the*auditory system determines loudness:

- As the sound becomes louder; the amplitude of vibration of the basilar membrane increases so
that hair cells éxcite the nae “endings at more rapid rates.

* As the amplitude i increasts- more and more hair cells on the fringes of the resonating portion of
the basilar membrane become stimulated.

¢ As the amplitude of vibration reaches high intensity, the outer hair cells become recruited at a
significant rate.

Solution to Question 17:

Umami taste is triggered particularly by the monosodium glutamate (MSG) used so extensively in
Asian cooking.

Taste Receptors
¢ Humans have five established basic tastes: sweet, salt, sour, bitter and umami.
* The taste receptors for these five basic tastes are of two major types:

¢ Ligand-gated channels (ionotropic receptors): for salt and sour taste

253 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 254 ========================================
* G-Protein Coupled Receptors (metabotropic receptors): for sweet, bitter and umami
¢ Salt and sour taste are mediated through epithelial sodium channels (ENaC)

* Na+ movement through ENaC salt taste

¢ H+ movement through ENaC - sour taste

¢ Sweet, bitter, and umami are sensed via GPCRs

* T1R2 and T1R3 families sweet taste

* T2R family — bitter taste

* Metabotropic glutamate receptor (mGluR4) — Umami

Solution to Question 19:

The olfactory system has the smallest range of intensity discrimination.

Smell is concerned more with detecting the presence or absence of odors , than with the
quantitative detection of their intensities. Concentrations that are only 10 to 50 times above
threshold values will evoke maximum intensity of smell, which is in contrast to most other

254 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 255 ========================================
sensory system of the body.

Solution to Question 20:

There are approximately 6 million cones and 120 million rods in each human eye.
Features of Photoreceptors

* Each rod and cone are divided into outer segment, inner segment, nuclear region and a synaptic
zone.

* Saccules and discs in the outer segment contain photosensitive pigments. . i

¢ If a person spends a considerable length of time in brightly lighted surroundihgs and then moves
to a dimly lighted environment, the retinas slowly becom, “aecustomed,tg:the dark.” This decline
in visual threshold is known as dark adaptation. It is near y maximal in about 20 min, much
longer than the time taken for light adaptation whieh is 5 min. = a

* When one passes suddenly from a dim toa brightly lighted eivironmenty sh? light seems
intensely and even uncomfortably bright) whtil the eyes, adapt to the ingréaséd illumination and the
visual threshold rises. This adaptation gornts over a apyned of aol and is called light
adaptation. ‘ ce

Solution to Question 21:

The site of lesion in Wernicke's hemianopic pupil is optic tract.

Wernicke hemianopic pupil is the light reflex, where, illumination of the blind half of the retina
shows no pupillary reaction while stimulating the normal side of the retina shows a pupillary
reaction. It is seen in lesions involving the optic tract. It is also seen in cases such as TB, syphilis
and tumours of the optic thalamus.

255 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 256 ========================================
Homonymous hemianopia (HH) involves loss of vision on the same side of the visual field in both
eyes. HH is characteristically seen in lesions involving the visual pathway posterior to the optic
chiasma.

Solution to Question 3:

In the given figure, the fiber in yellow innervates the ends of the muscle spindle. Therefore, it is
a gamma motor neuron.

Many much smaller gamma motor neurons are located in the spinal cord anterior horns (shown
as the yellow efferent fiber). These gamma motor neurons average 5 micrometers in diameter,
which go to small, special skeletal muscle fibers called intrafusal fibers or the muscle spindle.
These fibers constitute the ends of the muscle spindle, which help control basic muscle “tone.”

Option (b):

The alpha motor neurons (shown as the blue efferent fibre) are on an average 14 micrometers in
diameter. These fibers branch many times after they enter the muscle and innervate the large
skeletal muscle fibers at the motor endplate. Stimulation of a single alpha nerve fiber excites

269 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 270 ========================================
anywhere from three to several hundred skeletal muscle fibers, which are collectively called the
motor unit.

Option (c):
A-beta fibers are fibers for touch and pressure.
Option (d):

A-delta fibers are fibers for pain and temperature.

¢ Extrafusal fibers

Solution to Question 6:

The afferents for deep tendon reflexes innervate both - nuclear bag and chain fibers.

The afferents for the DTRs are the type Ia and type II nerve fibers. There are three types of
intrafusal muscle fibers: dynamic nuclear bag, static nuclear bag, and nuclear chain fibers. A
single Ia afferent fiber innervates all three types of fibers to form a primary sensory ending. A
group II sensory fiber innervates nuclear chain and static bag fibers to form a secondary sensory

ending.
Deep tendon reflexes are an example of stretch reflex or myotatic reflex. * a !

Solution to Question 8:

Withdrawal reflexes are not accompanied by the same response on both sides of the body. On the
ipsilateral side of the pain stimulus, flexors are stimulated (they contract) and extensors are
inhibited (they relax), and the arm is jerked away from the stove. On the contralateral side, flexors
are inhibited and extensors are stimulated (crossed extension reflex) to maintain balance.

It results in flexion on the ipsilateral side and extension on the contralateral side.

Somatosensory and pain afferent fibers elicit withdrawal of the stimulated body part from the
noxious stimulus.

272 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 273 ========================================
Pain (e.g., touching a hot stove) stimulates the flexor reflex afferents of groups II, III, and IV.

The afferent fibers synapse polysynaptically (via interneurons) onto motoneurons in the spinal
cord.

As a result of persistent neural activity in the polysynaptic circuits, an afterdischarge occurs. The
afterdischarge prevents the muscle from relaxing for some time.

Withdrawal reflex

Solution to Question9: |
. “

The maintenance of n posture depends on the Integrity of reflex arc.

The muscles tha sgpinbain upright posture—that is, support the body’s weight against
gravity—are cont lled by the’brain and by reflex mechanisms.

Three levels of the nervous system control movement:
* The spinal cord, which integrates spinal reflexes and contains central pattern generators.
* The brain stem and cerebellum, which control postural reflexes and hand and eye movements.

* The cerebral cortex and basal ganglia, which are responsible for voluntary movements.

There are 3-20 muscle fibers per Golgi tendon organ.

* Golgi tendon organ consists of a netlike collection of knobby nerve endings among the fascicles

Solution to Question 12:

Golgi tendon reflex (Inverse myotatic/Inverse stretch reflex) is disynaptic, is the opposite, or
inverse, of the stretch reflex.

When tension on the muscle—and therefore on the tendon—becomes extreme, the inhibitory
effect from the golgi tendon organ can be so great that it leads instantaneous relaxation of the
entire muscle. This effect is called the lengthening reaction; it is probably a protective mechanism
to prevent tearing of the muscle or avulsion of the tendon from its attachments to the bone.

======================================== PAGE 277 ========================================
The flexor reflex is organized to withdraw a pained or otherwise irritated part of the body from a
stimulus. Further, because of after-discharge, the reflex can hold the irritated part away from the
stimulus for 0.1 to 3 seconds after the irritation is over. During this time, other reflexes and
actions of the central nervous system can move the entire body away from the painful stimulus.

Solution to Question 14:

The resistance of a muscle to stretch is often referred to as its tone or tonus and muscle
spindle helps to control basic muscle tone.

The tonic (static) stretch reflex circuit includes both group Ia and group II afferentcfibers from
muscle spindles. Normally, there is ongoing activity in the Ia and II afferents;that helps maintain
a baseline firing of ° motor neurons; therefore, the tonic st¥etch reflex contributes to muscle tone.

Solution to Question 15: mn, er oo" A
The cell bodies of ° and y motor neurons t6 a given muscle lie closetogether. Both types are
activated by interneurons in their immediate viciiity and sometimes directly by neurons of the
descending pathways. During_many voluntary. and involuntaryymovements, they are coactivated,
ie., they are excited at almest-the same time. (° -ll coactivation)

; i am
The purpose of ° -ll coactivation: _ ¢ * : —

+ First, it keeps the length ofthe receptor portion’of the muscle spindle from changing during the

course of the whole muscle contraction. Hence, the muscle contraction is not opposed by the

stretch reflex. a a

i a
* Second, if the mus¢ele’spindle did, not contract and relax along with the large muscle fibers, the
receptor portion. of the spindléywould sometimes be flail and sometimes be overstretched.

Coactivation'thus ensuresithat information about muscle length will be continuously available to
provide for adjustment during ongoing actions and to plan and program future movements.

Solution to Question 16:

Righting reflexes operate to maintain the normal standing position and keep an animal's head
upright. These reflexes are a series of responses integrated for the most part in the nuclei of the
midbrain.

Solution to Question 17:
Pressure on the footpad of a decerebrate patient causes the limb to extend against the pressure

applied to the foot. This reflex is called the positive supportive reaction and is a righting reflex
integrated at the spinal level.

277 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 278 ========================================
Pressure on one side causes extension in that direction (e.g., on applying pressure on the right
footpad, extension of the right lower limb occurs), an effect called the magnet reaction. It is seen
in infants and following spinal transection.

From an evolutionary perspective, this reflex helps to prevent falling on the side where the
stimulus is given.

Solution to Question 18:

The corticospinal pathways are also called the pyramidal tracts or pyramidal system because of

their triangular shape as they pass along the ventral surface of the medulla oblongata’
mh
a

Solution to Question 19:

hs

The corticospinal tract contains crossed fibers (majority).

Fibers of the corticospinal tract originate from the primary motor cortex (about 30%), the
premotor and supplementary motor areas (30%), and the somatosensory areas posterior to the
central sulcus (40%).

Most fibers (80% fibers) decussate (cross) in the medullary pyramids and descend in the lateral
white matter of the spinal cord to form the lateral corticospinal tract. They control the movements
of the contralateral limbs.

The remaining 20% make up the ventral corticospinal tract. These fibers remain uncrossed until
reaching the spinal cord where axons terminate bilaterally on spinal interneurons antecedent to
motor neurons. These fibers control the movements of the axial musculature.

Solution to Question 24:
In decerebrate rigidity, there is rigidity in extensor muscles of all the four extremities (not all
muscles of the body).
Decerebration

* Complete transection of the brain stem between the superior and inferior colliculi permits the
brain stem pathways to function independently of their input from higher brain structures. This is
called a mid-collicular decerebration.

¢ This lesion interrupts all input from the cortex (corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts) and red
nucleus (rubrospinal tract), primarily to distal muscles of the extremities.

281 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 282 ========================================
¢ The excitatory and inhibitory reticulospinal pathways (primarily to postural extensor muscles)
remain intact.

* The dominance of drive from ascending sensory pathways to the excitatory reticulospinal
pathway leads to hyperactivity in extensor muscles in all four extremities which are called
decerebrate rigidity.

¢ The excitatory input from the reticulospinal pathway activates y-motor neurons which indirectly
activate ° -motor neurons (via Ia spindle afferent activity). This is called the gamma loop.

Option 4. Spinal shock is different from decebrate rigidity as it is a transient state that can occur
immediately after a spinal cord injury. It is characterized by a temporary loss or depression of
spinal reflexes below the level of the injury.

ae p

Solution to Question 26:

The Hopping and Placing reflex is absent in a decorticate animal as it is integrated in the cerebral
cortex. Rest of the reflexes are integrated below the cortex.

282 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 283 ========================================
Solution to Question 27:

Destruction of the anterior cerebellum in a decerebrate animal leads to an increased rigidity via
alpha motor neuron.

If the anterior lobe of the cerebellum is removed in a decerebrate animal, extensor muscle
hyperactivity is exaggerated (decerebellate rigidity). This cut eliminates cortical inhibition of the
cerebellar fastigial nucleus and secondarily increases excitation to vestibular nuclei. Subsequent
dorsal root section does not reverse the rigidity, hence proving that the rigidity is due to activation
of ° -motor neurons independent of the gamma loop.

Solution to Question 30:

Cog-wheel rigidity is not seen if there is a corticospinal tract involvement. Cogwheel rigidity is
seen in extrapyramidal tract involvement when there is a tremor superimposed on hypertonia
(e.g., Parkinsonism).

In an upper motor neuron lesion, there is damage to the corticospinal or corticobulbar fibres. If
the corticospinal fibres are damaged, it leads to diminished or loss of superficial reflexes, shows
an extensor plantar response (Babinski's sign) with loss of fine skilled movement and spasticity.

h

The given clinical vignette is suggestive of spinal cord transection, which is «characterized by an

exaggeration of reflexes and not suppression. % af ms,

Upper motor neurons typically refer to corticospinal, tract neurons. that innervate spinal motor

neurons. Damage to these neurons, as in transe¢tion of the spitial‘ cord, initially causes muscles to

become weak and flaccid but eventually leads.t6 spasticity, typertonia (ineteased resistance to

passive movement), hyperactive reflexes,'and abnormal’ plantar extensor reflex (positive Babinski
(" =,

Solution to Question 32: * i,

1%
The given clinical vignette is stiegestive of an.aeute stroke, most probably due to atrial fibrillation
induced thromboembolisr, Since the patient has developed motor weakness in the right side of
his body, he most likely,has an infaret“affecting the primary motor cortex ( broadmann area 4).

* The primary motor cortex (or just the motor cortex) can be defined as the region of the cortex
from which mreyements are-elicited with the least amount of electrical stimulation.

* It is essentially congruent with Brodmann's cytoarchitectonic area 4.

¢ The primary motor cortex (M1) is in the precentral gyrus of the frontal lobe, extending into the
central sulcus.

Solution to Question 33:

Commands for voluntary movements originate in cortical association areas.

The corticospinal and corticobulbar system (originating in the motor cortex located in precentral
gyrus) is the primary pathway for the initiation of skilled voluntary movement.

The movements are planned in the cortex as well as in the basal ganglia and the lateral portions of
the cerebellar hemispheres.

The basal ganglia and cerebellum funnel information to the premotor and motor cortex by way of
the thalamus.

Motor commands from the motor cortex are relayed in large part via the corticospinal tracts to the
spinal cord.

285 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 286 ========================================
Movement sets up alterations in sensory input from the special senses and from muscles, tendons,
joints, and the skin. This feedback information, which adjusts and smooths movement, is relayed
directly to the motor cortex and to the spinocerebellum.

Solution to Question 36:

287 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 288 ========================================
Damage to both paracentral lobules will cause paraplegia.

The paracentral lobule is the medial continuation of the precentral and postcentral gyri. It
controls motor and sensory innervations of the contralateral lower extremity. It is also responsible
for the control of defecation and urination.

Solution to Question 38:

The given clinical vignette is suggestive of spinal mass reflex which is seen in patients with a high
spinal lesion. A major portion of the body’s skeletal muscles goes into strong flexor spasm, and
not extensor spasm, in a spinal mass reflex.

288 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 289 ========================================
Mass Reflex: In a spinal animal or human being, sometimes the spinal cord suddenly becomes
excessively active, causing massive discharge in large portions of the cord. The usual stimulus that
causes this excess activity is a strong pain stimulus to the skin or excessive filling of a viscus, such
as over-distention of the bladder or the gut. The mass reflex involves large portions or even all of
the cord.

The effects are as follows:
¢ A major portion of the body’s skeletal muscles goes into strong flexor spasms;
* The colon and bladder are likely to evacuate;

* The arterial pressure often rises to maximal values, sometimes to a systolic pressure well over

200 mm Hg; and e

ah?

a

The mechanism is similar to the mechanism of epileptic seizures, whichamwolve reverberating
oy

circuits that occur in the brain instead of in the cord: ~
~ as

* Large areas of the body break out into profuse sweating.

Solution to Question 1: ~~

When dynamic y-motor neurons are activated at the same time as ° -motor neurons to muscle, the
number of impulses in spindle Ia afferents is greater than when ° discharge alone is increased.

¢ When ° -7 coactivation occurs, intrafusal and extrafusal fibers shorten together, and spindle
afferent activity can occur throughout the period of muscle contraction.

¢ Stimulation of y-motor neurons causes increase discharge in spindle Ia afferent, leading to reflex
contraction of the muscle.

* When a muscle contracts it leads to shortening of the muscle spindles and so impulses in the Ia
afferents decreases.

¢ Thus, muscles can be made to contract via stimulation of the ° -motor neurons that innervate the
extrafusal fibers or the y-motor neurons that initiate contraction indirectly via the stretch reflex.

* If the whole muscle is stretched during stimulation of the y-motor neurons, the rate of discharge
in the Ia fibers is further increased. Increased y-motor neuron activity thus increases spindle
sensitivity during stretch. This is the basis of the Jendrassik manouver.

296 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 297 ========================================
Solution to Question 2:

The figure shows the typical myogram recorded from a muscle involved in a crossed extensor
reflex. This myogram demonstrates the relatively long latency before the reflex begins and the
long after-discharge at the end of the stimulus.

* When a painful stimulus is applied to the body, the flexor withdrawal and the crossed extensor
reflexes are stimulated.

¢ About 0.2 to 0.5 seconds after the flexor reflex in one limb, the opposite limb pegs to extend.

This reflex is called the crossed extensor reflex. % .

+ After the painful stimulus is removed, the crossed extensor reflex has an even longer period of
after-discharge than does the flexor reflex. This prolonge¢-after- discharge’ results from
reverberating circuits among the inter-neuronal cells: he

ak,

Fatigue

After
discharge

Flexor contraction

Seconds

©MARROW

Compare the myograms of a flexor reflex with a crossed extensor reflex.

In the flexor reflex, the contraction occurs immediately after applying the stimulus and the
after-discharge is smaller.

Solution to Question 3:

Impulses are transmitted into the bulboreticular area from
* Cerebellum,
* Basal ganglia, and

* Cerebral cortex.

297 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 298 ========================================
The gamma efferent system is excited by signals from the bulboreticular facilitatory region of the
brain stem.

When the bulboreticular facilitatory region transmits excitatory signals through the gamma nerve
fibers to the intrafusal muscle fibers of the muscle spindles, it shortens the ends of the spindles
and stretches the central receptor regions, thus increasing their signal output. If the spindles on
both sides of each joint are activated at the same time, then reflex excitation of the skeletal
muscles on both sides of the joint increases, producing tight, tense muscles opposing each other at
the joint. The net effect is that the position of the joint becomes strongly stabilized.

Any time a person must perform a muscle function that requires a high degree of delicate and
exact positioning, the bulboreticular facilitatory region of the brain stem stabilizes the positions of
the proximal joints. 74

7
Ls
_ A

‘ys, ho

reticulospinal tract in the anterior column‘of the cord.The fibers of this pathway terminate on the
medial anterior motor neurons that excite the axial jaliscles of the body, that is the muscles of the
vertebral column and the extensor muscles of thé.limbs.

Lt ie!

Solution to Question 5: “4,

The rubrospinal tract is a part of the lateral brainstem pathway.

* The medial brainstem pathways : pontine and medullary reticulospinal, vestibulospinal, and
tectospinal tracts.

¢ These pathways descend in the ipsilateral ventral columns of the spinal cord and terminate
predominantly on interneurons in the ventromedial part of the ventral horn to control axial and
proximal muscles.

¢ The lateral brainstem pathways : The corticospinal tract and the rubrospinal tract.

* They cross the midline and project to interneurons in the dorsolateral part of the spinal ventral
horn to also influence motor neurons that control distal limb muscles.

¢ The rubrospinal tract excites flexor motor neurons and inhibits extensor motor neurons. This
pathway may play a role in the posture typical of decorticate rigidity.

298 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 299 ========================================
Solution to Question 6:

The command for voluntary movement originates in cortical association areas, then planned in
the cortex as well as in the basal ganglia and the lateral portions of the cerebellar hemispheres.
Hence, the neural activity first originates in the cortical association areas.

The basal ganglia and cerebellum funnel information to the premotor and motor cortex by way of
the thalamus.

Motor commands from the motor cortex are relayed in large part via the corticospinal tracts to the
spinal cord.

Solution to Question 7:

The lateral corticospinal and rubrospinal tracts are concerned with distal limb muscles and, with
skilled voluntary movements.

The ventral corticospinal tract and medial descending brainstem pathways (tectospinal,
reticulospinal, and vestibulospinal tracts) are concerned with the axial muscles - postural
adjustments and gross movements.

Note: Medial portion of the ventral horn innervate proximal limb muscles, particularly the flexors.
Lateral ventral horn innervates distal limb muscles.

Solution to Question 8:

Sham rage occurs in decorticate animals is the true statement.

Sham rage is a controversial term used to describe behavior such as biting, clawing, hissing,
arching the back and "violent alternating limb movements" produced in animal experiments by
removing the cerebral cortex, which is claimed to occur in the absence of any sort of inner
experience of rage.

The reason for this might be that the removal of the neocortex causes a loss of inhibition of the
areas involved in the rage reaction, causing those areas to become hyperactive, (due to an

i

uninhibited hypothalamus). c*
Removal of hypothalamus abolishes the sham rage. Oo _ oe
Sham rage may be triggered by very mild stimuli syel ie weak tou, iti is also non-goal directed.

Solution to Question 10: ~~
Pacinian corpuscle is not involved in the clasp knife effect.

‘

¢ When the muscles are hypertonic, the sequence of moderate stretch leads to muscle contraction,
strong stretch leads to muscle relaxation. Eg. Passive flexion of the elbow meets immediate
resistance due to stretch reflex in the triceps muscle. Further stretch activates the inverse stretch
reflex. The resistance to flexion suddenly collapses, and the arm flexes. This sequence of
resistance followed by a sudden decrease in resistance when a limb is moved passively is known as
the clasp-knife effect because of its resemblance to the closing of a pocket knife.

¢ Lengthening reaction: When the tension on the muscle becomes extreme, the inhibitory effect
from the Golgi tendon organ leads to an instantaneous relaxation of the entire muscle. It is a
protective mechanism to prevent tearing of the muscle.

Solution to Question 11: = . ,
ro it + a
Efferent axons from the dentate Shucleus terminate in Ventral lhtéral nucleus of the thalamus (VL)
and travel through superior ¢ éerebellar peguifete  h,"

The cerebellar cortex projects to the déep nuclei whieh in turn project to the brainstem. The deep
nuclei provide the only output for'the* spinocerebellum and the cerebrocerebellum:

* The medial spinocerebellum'projects to thefastigial nuclei and from there to the brainstem.

* The adjacent hemisphesie portions of ‘thé spinocerebellum project to the emboliform and globose
nuclei and from thereto the brainsten.

* The cerebrocefebellum projedts to the dentate nucleus (Purkinje cells of cerebellum relay into
this nucleu§) and from thgre either directly or indirectly to the ventrolateral nucleus of the
thalamus.

These fibres pass through the cerebellar peduncles:

¢ The superior cerebellar peduncle includes fibers from deep cerebellar nuclei that project to the
brainstem, red nucleus, and thalamus. It also contains the ventral spinocerebellar tract fibers.

¢ The middle cerebellar peduncle contains only afferent fibers from the contralateral pontine
nuclei.

* Inferior cerebellar peduncle a mixture of afferent fibers from the brainstem and spinal cord and
efferent fibers to the vestibular nuclei.

Solution to Question 13:

The given clinical findings are suggestive of dysmetria which is most commonly found due to a
lesion involving the cerebellum or the cerebellar pathways. Therefore, a lesion in the
spinocerebellar tract is most likely to be found in this patient.

¢ Dysmetria - In the absence of the cerebellum, the subconscious motor control system cannot
predict how far movements will go. Therefore, the movements ordinarily overshoot their intended
mark; then the conscious portion of the brain overcompensates in the opposite direction for the
succeeding compensatory movement. Dysmetria also results from lesions in the spinocerebellar
tracts because feedback information from the moving parts of the body to the cerebellum is

essential for cerebellar timing of movement termination.

302

Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 303 ========================================
Solution to Question 14:

Dysdiadochokinesia—Inability to Perform Rapid Alternating Movements.

* In cerebellar lesions, the motor control system fails to predict where the different parts of the
body will be at a given time. As a result, the succeeding movement may begin much too early or
much too late and no orderly “progression of movement” can occur.

* So, a series of stalled attempted but jumbled movements occurs instead of the normal
coordinated movements. This condition is called dysdiadochokinesia.

Flocculonodular Lobe is involved in the unsteady "drunk Ji So gait of thegddient.

Cerebellum has three parts based on function. ma ae i
+ 7,

* Vestibulocerebellum (or flocculonodular lobe)=Lhe oldest part.of the cerebellum which contains
nodulus in the vermis and the flanking floectilus. It has vestibular connections and is concerned
with equilibrium and gait. Loss of it can éause disturbance of equilibrium and postural

movements, especially during rapid aeyements, pak | ok

* Spinocerebellum: The region that receives proprioceptive input from the body as well as a copy
of the “motor plan” from the!motor cortex, By comparing thie plan with performance, it smooths
and coordinates movements that are origoing. Superior.” vermis is a part of spinocerebellum. Loss

of it can cause problems with coordination. o.”

* Cerebrocerebellum: They aie tie newest fuer a phylogenetic point of view, reaching their
greatest development in biimans. They, ifitéract with the motor cortex in planning and

programming ee es

Solution to Question 16:"

The two major outputs/ efferents of the basal ganglia are from Globus pallidus interna and
substantia nigra pars reticulata. Both are inhibitory (GABAergic) and both project to the
ventrolateral and ventroanterior thalamus.

Solution to Question 18:

Substantia nigra pars compacta (not pars reticulata) is the major source of dopaminergic neurons
to the striatum.

Neurons in the basal ganglia, like those in the lateral portions of the cerebellar hemispheres,
discharge before movements begin since the basal ganglia are involved in the planning and
organization of skilled movements.

¢ The putamen circuit has its inputs mainly from the parts of the brain adjacent to the primary
motor cortex. Then its outputs do go mainly back to the primary motor cortex and adjacent
premotor cortex. it is mainly involved in complex motor activity and skilled movement.

* The caudate circuit plays a major role in the cognitive control of motor activity, fe 1

Solution to Question 19:

The most likely reason behind the gradual decline in the therapeutic effect of L-dopa in a patient
suffering from parkinsons's disease is the continued degeneration of the dopaminergic neurons in
the substantia nigra. Since endogenous production of dopamine continues to decline,

the exogenous source of dopamine (i.e. L-dopa) alone fails to maintain the same level of
therapeutic effect.

* Parkinson's disease (Paralysis Agitans) was shown to result from the degeneration of
dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta. The fibres to the putamen (part of
the striatum) are most severely affected.

* Dopaminergic neurons and dopamine receptors are steadily lost with age in the basal ganglia in
healthy individuals, and an acceleration of these losses apparently precipitates*parkinsonism.
Symptoms appear when 60-80% of the nigrostriatal dopaminergic neurons degenerate.

. . Aut <5 sm

The ventrolateral thalamic nucleus is the’ Weeotaxic tafeet for the neurosurgeons to alleviate
symptoms in Parkinson's disease refractory to leyodopa. ak

¢ The two major outputs of thes Basal ganglia areifrom GPi and substantia nigra pars reticulata.
Both are inhibitory (GABAergic) ) and both. project to the- ventrolateral and ventroanterior
thalamus. From the thalamus, there i is.an excitatory. (presumably glutamate) projection to the
prefrontal and premotor cortex. This-completes; a full cortical-basal ganglia-thalamic-cortical loop.

Therefore stereotactic ablation, of ventrolateral'thalamic nucleus helps in alleviation of symptoms
in parkinson's disease refthet6ry to levodopa.

Solution to Question 22 ‘at

The striatum ra¢eives the afferent input.
*h en

Note: * “A

There are two main inputs to the basal ganglia: From the cerebral cortex (corticostriate pathway)

and from the thalamus (thalamostriate pathway). They are both excitatory (glutamate) and they

both terminate in the striatum.

The two major outputs of the basal ganglia are from GPi and substantia nigra. Both are inhibitory
(GABAergic) and both project to the thalamus.

From the thalamus, there is an excitatory (glutamatergic) projection to the prefrontal and
premotor cortex. This completes the cortical-basal ganglia-thalamic-cortical loop.

Solution to Question, 8x Pa

a.

Purkinje cells-ate the only output neurons (efferent neurons) from the cerebellar cortex.

The Purkinje cells are the largest neurons in the cerebellum. Their axons, which are the only
output from the cerebellar cortex, project to the deep cerebellar nuclei, especially the dentate
nucleus, where they form inhibitory synapses.

Solution to Question 9:

The flocculonodular lobe has connections with the vestibular apparatus.

The flocculonodular lobe forms the vestibulocerebellum, which projects to the vestibular nuclei
and is concerned with equilibrium and eye movements.

Solution to Question 10:

Climbing fibers are olivocerebellar fibers. They originate from the inferior olivary nucleus.

The cerehglluittcan be divided into:

The Vestibulocerebellum/Archicerebellum: Consists principally of the flocculonodular lobe and
adjacent portions of the vermis. It participates in balance, vestibular reflexes, and eye movements.

i™
Solution to Question 12K

The Spinocerebellum/Paleocerebellum: Consists of most of the vermis plus the adjacent
intermediate zones on both sides of the vermis. It coordinates movements of the limbs. Vermis:
Proximal muscles, Intermediate zones: Distal muscles

The Cerebrocerebellum/neocerebellum: Consists of the large lateral zones of the cerebellar
hemispheres. It receives all its input from the cerebral cortex (motor, premotor and
somatosensory). It transmits its output back to the brain, functioning in a feedback manner to
plan sequential voluntary body and limb movements.

Solution to Question 13:

A patient with a suspected cerebellar lesion will not have resting tremors. Resting tremors are
characteristic features of basal ganglia lesions. (Parkinson's disease).

318 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 319 ========================================
Intentional tremors are seen in cerebellar diseases.

Features of cerebellar disease

¢ Ataxia — Incoordinated, intermittent jerky movements with drunken gait.

¢ Hypotonia or atonia — Reduced or absence of muscle tone.

¢ Dysmetria — Inability to judge the force and range of movement resulting in past pointing.
* Dysdiadokokinesia — Failure to perform rapid alternating movements.

* Decomposition of movement — Lack of smooth flow of movement.

* Speech defect — Staccato speech or slurring speech.

¢ Pendular knee jerk (a feature of hypotonia). *,t5"

Solution to Question 16:

Solution to Question 18:

The peduncle highlighted in the image is the superior cerebellar peduncle. The anterior
spinocerebellar tract enters the cerebellum through it and terminates in both sides of the
cerebellum.

Dorsal spinocerebellar tract enters the cerebellum through the inferior cerebellar peduncle and
terminates on the same side as its origin. *, ery

Solution to Question 19: rh he
. es ty hh ey
The excitatory fibers in the cerebellum are;elimbing fibers, mossy fibers, aad granule cell parallel
fibers. sg hy,

cal co" ff,

The fundamental circuits of the ceiébellar cortex:

{ Ls

* Climbing fibers exert a strong'€Xcitatory efféct’on single Purkinje cells. Mossy fibers exert an
excitatory effect on granule ¢ells. > . i

+ Each granule cell sends an axon to the ‘nolecula Myer. where the axon bifurcates to form a T.
The branches of the T are straight and run long'distances; thus, they are called parallel fibers. The
parallel fibers form excitatory synapses on the dendrites of many Purkinje cells.

¢ The other neurons ( (basket cells, stolltte cells, and golgi cells) are inhibitory interneurons. Basket
cells and stellate cells feceive excitatory input from the parallel fibers and each inhibits many
Purkinje cells. Golgi cells receive excitatory input from the parallel fibers and mossy fibers. Their
axons project ‘tothe dendrites ‘of the granule cells where they form an inhibitory synapse.

* The output of the Purkinje cells is ultimately inhibitory to the deep cerebellar nuclei.

Solution to Question 20:

Inhibition of Purkinje cells by stellate cells is an example of feedforward inhibition.

Mossy fibers exert a weak excitatory effect on many Purkinje cells via the granule cells. The
granule cells also excite the basket and stellate cells via their parallel fibers. The basket and
stellate cells, in turn, inhibit the Purkinje cells (Feed forward inhibition).

Renshaw cells exhibit feedback inhibition.

Solution to Question 2:
The ventroposterior nuclear complex of the thalamus represents the main termination site for
ascending somatosensory information (which includes pain) in the diencephalon.
Thalamic nuclei have been divided into

A. Specific (Relay) nuclei because they have a specific and selective relationship with a particular
portion of neocortex

* Medial geniculate body for auditory information
* Lateral geniculate body is for information from retina
¢ Ventroposterior group for somatosensory information

B. Nonspecific nuclei project to several cortical and subcortical regions

325 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 326 ========================================
Solution to Question 3:
The amygdala has been called the “window” through which the limbic system sees the place of the
person in the world.

The amygdala receives neuronal signals from all portions of the limbic cortex, as well as from the
neocortex of the temporal, parietal, and occipital lobes, especially from the auditory and visual
association areas.

Components of the limbic system

* The term limbic lobe, or limbic system, is applied to the part of the brain that consists of a rim of
cortical tissue around the hilum of the cerebral hemisphere and a group of associated deep
structures—the amygdala, the hippocampus, and the septal nuclei. ad

a
* The papez circuit is a closed neural circuitry that starts and ends in the hipetmpus, which
forms the basal ganglia. It is also known as the medial limbie circuit. The,components of papez
circuit are- = /

Solution to Question 4:
Anterior nucleus of the Sehlamus is aedmponent of Papez circuit, which is important for short
term memory..." = *,

Papez Cinghgt ‘trefer figure), hel

The limbic’ system was studied by James Papez who described the Papez circuit. It consists of:
Mnemonic for components of Papez circuit-He Man Ate Cat

¢ He-Hippocampus

* Man-Mamillary body

¢ Ate-Anterior thalamus

* Cat-Cingulate gyrus

The fornix connects the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies, which are in turn connected to
the anterior nuclei of the thalamus by the mammillothalamic tract. The anterior nuclei of the
thalamus project to the cingulate cortex, and from there, connections to the hippocampus
complete a complex closed circuit.

The boundaries of the limbic system were subsequently expanded to include the amygdala,
septum, basal forebrain, nucleus accumbens, and orbitofrontal cortex.

Solution to Question 6: ~“\

Solution to Question 7:
In the hypothalamus, the suprachiasmatic nucleus functions as the principal pacemaker, or time
clock, for circadian rhythms.

The pacemaker receives input from the eyes and many other parts of the nervous system, and
these inputs mediate the effects exerted by the external environment (retinohypothalamic tract).

One output of the pacemaker goes to the pineal gland, a gland within the brain that secretes the
hormone melatonin.These neural signals cause the pineal gland to secrete melatonin during
darkness but not during daylight.

327 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 328 ========================================
Solution to Question 8:
Lesions involving the anterior and preoptic hypothalamic regions cause paradoxical
vasoconstriction, tachycardia, and hyperthermia.

The anterior hypothalamus activates mechanisms of cooling the body when the temperature
increases via the reflex response.

Stimulation of the anterior hypothalamus causes cutaneous vasodilation and sweating, thereby
cooling the body. Lesions in this region cause hyperthermia, with rectal temperatures sometimes

reaching 43°C (109.4°F). % &
i, ! *,

Solution to Question 10: re
“a <*

Functions of the categorieal hemisphére are logical, analytical, sequential thinking, and verbal
tasks, such as math, lahguage forms, and philosophy.

Human language functions depend more on one cerebral hemisphere than on the other. This
hemisphefe'is concerned.with categorization and symbolization and has often been called the
dominant hemisphere. However, the other hemisphere is specialized in the area of spatiotemporal
relations.

The concept of “cerebral dominance” and a dominant and non-dominant hemisphere has been
replaced by, one hemisphere for sequential-analytic processes (the categorical hemisphere) and
one for visuospatial relations (the representational hemisphere).

The representational cerebral hemisphere excels in nonlanguage skills, especially spatial
perception, and artistic and musical talents.

Solution to Question 11:

A lesion in the ventromedial nucleus of the hypothalamus, the satiety center, is responsible for
producing a voracious appetite and rage.

Lesions in the upper region of the lateral hypothalamus produce a profound loss of appetite.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

High-frequency stimulation of the midbrain reticular formation (the RAS) produces the EEG
alerting response and arouses a sleeping animal.

The Reticular Activating System (RAS) is a complex polysynaptic pathway arising from the
brainstem reticular formation and hypothalamus with projections to the intralaminar and
reticular nuclei of the thalamus which, in turn, project diffusely and nonspecifically to wide
regions of the cortex including the frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital cortices.

Collaterals funnel into it from the long ascending sensory tracts, the trigeminal, auditory, visual,
and olfactory systems.

338 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 339 ========================================
The reticular formation plays an important role in determining the level of arousal; thus, it is
called the ascending reticular activating system (ARAS).

Solution to Question 2:

Alpha-waves are recorded best in the parieto-occipital region in adults at rest, i.e. awake but
relaxed with eyes closed.

i.

Solution to Question 4:

Stage 2 of NREM sleep is marked by the appearance of sinusoidal waves called sleep spindles
(12-14 Hz) and occasional high-voltage biphasic waves called K complexes.

Solution to Question 6: ni
ct 4 ernie Naat

*h en
The cholinergic neurons‘ pontine reticular formation on stimulation, induce sleep.

When the activity of norepinephrine and serotonin containing neurons (locus coeruleus and raphe
nuclei) is dominant, there is a reduced level of activity in acetylcholine-containing neurons in the
pontine reticular formation leading to wakefulness. The reverse of this pattern leads to REM
sleep. A more even balance in the activity of these groups of neurons is associated with NREM
sleep.

Solution to Question 7:

The nucleus coeruleus (locus coeruleus) is rich in norepinephrine and the Raphe nuclei of ARAS is
rich in serotonin. These regions when stimulated induce arousal. Thus, the dorsal raphe nucleus is
not involved in NREM sleep.

Genesis and Regulation of NREM sleep

340 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 341 ========================================
¢ Electrical stimulation of the anterior hypothalamus and the adjacent basal forebrain region
rapidly induces sleep mediated by GABA-ergic inhibitory neurons called the NREM-on cells.

* These cells are thought to produce sleep by inhibiting the histaminergic cells in the posterior
hypothalamus as well as cells of the nucleus reticularis pontis oralis in the midbrain that mediate
arousal.

¢ They are maximally active in NREM sleep and inactive during waking and REM sleep.

* NREM sleep is produced by synchronized synaptic potentials in cortical neurons generated by
the rhythmic firing of thalamic relay neurons that project to the cortex. (Thalamocortical loop)

Solution to Question 10: o%)
ny ed
Melatonin is released sy the pingal ath (pinealocytes) that plays an important role in sleep
mechanisms.

In infants} Fhe pineal gland is large and the cells are arranged in alveoli. It begins to involute
before puberty and small concretions of calcium phosphate and carbonate appear (radiopaque
structure) = physiological calcification.

Solution to Question 11:
Melatonin plays a key role in regulating the circadian rhythm. Therefore it is helpful in restoring
the usual sleep wake pattern in patients with jetlag.

Diurnal variation in melatonin secretion may function as a timing signal to coordinate events with
the light-dark cycle in the environment (circadian rhythm).

Melatonin synthesis and secretion are increased during the dark period of the day and maintained
at low levels during the daytime.

Increased melatonin levels induce sleepiness.
