Solution to Question 12: wo 0 ns
4

Procedural memory includes skills and habits, which, once acdfuited, become unconscious and
automatic. This type of memoity is procesgsg’ in the striatum.

Ae
Implicit memory/Non- declafative nee It does not. require being conscious or awareness. E.g.,
Remembering how to brush your, teeth. They areoft 4 types:

* Procedural memory (skillsahd habits) in striatum, cerebellum, motor cortex
* Priming in neocortegn i,

* Non-associative learning (includes habituation and sensitization, e.g., Byhearting facts)
* Associative teaming: clasgiéaland operant conditioning

Explicit/declarative memoty: Memory of words,rules, language, etc. Associated with
consciousness/awareness. E.g., recalling first day in school.

They are of two types:

* Semantic memory (facts) e.g., Knowing capital of India: Parts of brain involved are temporal
cortex and prefrontal cortex.

* Episodic memory (events) e.g., Recalling first day in college: hippocampus, medial temporal lobe
and neocortex.

Solution to Question 13:

Compression or a lesion affecting the mammillary body will cause anterograde amnesia.

The Hippocampus, mammillary body and mammillothalamic tract are the primary structures
involved in the conversion of short-term memory to long-term memory. Any lesion involving this

342 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 343 ========================================
pathway will affect the ability to convert short-term memory to long-term memory, leading to
anterograde amnesia.

Solution to Question 14:

Sensitization is a neural mechanism involved in synaptic plasticity and learning (not
desensitization).

Neural basis of synaptic plasticity and learning

¢ Post-tetanic Potentiation ea

¢ Production of enhanced postsynaptic potential, which occurs after a brief tetanizing train of
stimuli in the presynaptic neuron Pit

* This enhancement lasts up to 60 seconds.
¢ Habituation = o, < a ima

* A simple form of learning in which neutral stimulus i is repeated many pis until the subject
becomes habituated to the stimulus and i igriores it 4,"

* A classic example of non- associative)! Sdarning ( (eg, fearning a sah by heart)

+ Sensitization: The opposite, of habituation,- 4, =

* Long-term Potentiation (LEP) re “ on

- A rapidly developing persistent.eahancement of the post-synaptic potential response to
presynaptic stimulation, aftenrapidly repeated'stimulation of presynaptic neuron

* Characteristically observéd' in the hippéeampus
> 4

* Long-term Depression! rf
i

* Is the opposite’ -of LTP ah
*h i. Aa
* Characterized by a decfease in synaptic strength
* Produced by slower stimulation of presynaptic neuron

* May be involved in the neural mechanism by which learning occurs in the cerebellum

Solution to Question 15:

Long-term potentiation occurs in the hippocampus.

LTP is a rapidly developing persistent enhancement of the post-synaptic potential response to
presynaptic stimulation, after rapidly repeated stimulation of presynaptic neuron.

NMDA receptors are involved in LTP.

Solution to Question 18:4,

A lesion in broca’s area will lead to an impairment of word-formation.
Broca’s area is in the inferior frontal lobe.

Broca area processes the information received from the wernicke area into a detailed and
coordinated pattern for vocalization and then projects the pattern via a speech articulation area in
the insula to the motor cortex. This then initiates the appropriate movements of the lips, tongue,

and larynx to produce speech.

The probable sequence of events that occurs when a subject names a visual object is shown in the
figure and flowchart.

344 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 345 ========================================
Information travels from lateral geniculate nucleus to

Primary visual cortex (Area 17) to high order visual
areas(Area 18)

Angular gyrus(Area 39)

Wernicke’s area(Area 22)

Broca’s area(area 44) via arcuate fasciculus 1

Broca’s area process it and vocalises

™%, 7 iy
i ahead

Solution to Question 19:

This condition is prosopagnosia, which occurs in the lesions of the inferior occipitotemporal lobe.

In humans, storage and recognition of faces are more strongly represented in the right inferior
temporal lobe in right-handed individuals (i.e non-dominant inferior temporal lobe).

Damage to this area can cause prosopagnosia, the inability to recognize faces. Patients with this
abnormality can recognize people by their voices, body language, etc. However, they cannot
identify the familiar people by seeing their faces. The left hemisphere is also involved, but the role
of the right hemisphere is primary.

345 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 346 ========================================
Solution to Question 20:

Forward digit span test is the best test among the given options.

Memory is of 3 types:

* Immediate

* Recent

* Remote

Immediate memory is attention dependent. Tests of attention and memory include

* Digit span test: Most important and best test for immediate memory

* Serial (100-7) subtraction test is the best for concentration Ps a
* Days of the week backwards es ale _

* Spell WORLD backwards =a o aha

* (20-3) test = a ce - ey

numbers to 3 numbers and so‘en:' The patient.is'asked to repeat until he/she fails to repeat all

numbers correctly. coh x, é

* Normally a person can repeat up to 5 digits (+/- 1) in backward direction (making option C
wrong)

¢ Eg : Examiner says 2 - 4 —8 —1 —5 —7 - 9. The person has to repeat it backwards. 9 -7 -5 -1 -8- 4- 2.
Serial (100-7) subtraction test

* 5 scores / steps are taken in to account

* 1st score — When the patient tells 93 (100 - 7 = 93)

* 2nd score - When the patient tells 86 (93 — 7 = 86)

+ And soon.. 100,93 , 86,79, 72, 65

Hence among the given options :

Digit span forward up to 7 digits with 2 skips allowed would be the answer of choice as it is the
best test for immediate memory

Solution to Question 24:
From the history, it is clear that the patient is suffering fron anterograde amnesia, most probably
due to a lesion is hippocampus.

Hippocampus is the primary structure within the temporal lobe involved in conversion of
short-term memory to long-term memory.

Hippocampal cognitive map is inappropriately reactivated during a deja vu experience.

Hippocampus does not store memory but only converts short-term memory to long-term memory.
Hence, hippocampal lesions cause only anterograde amnesia.

Solution to Question 25:

348 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 349 ========================================
Declarative memory involves the hippocampus and the medial temporal lobe for retention.
Priming - neocortex.
Procedural memory - striatum.

Associative learning- amygdala for its emotional responses and the cerebellum for the motor
responses.

Nonassociative learning - various reflex pathways.

Solution to Question 27:

Broca's motor speech area is likely to be affected in the vascular injury of the inferior frontal gyrus

A lesion in this region leads to motor/Broca's aphasia. Speech is non-fluent and interrupted by
many word-finding pauses. The speech is impoverished in function words that contribute to the
syntax but is enriched in meaningful nouns and verbs. However, comprehension of speech is
intact and the patient knows what he wants to say.

For example, a patient with Broca's aphasia may say "walk dog" instead of "I want to take the dog
for a walk".

Solution to Question 28:

349 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 350 ========================================
Prosopagnosia is difficulty in identifying known faces.

In humans, storage and recognition of faces is more strongly represented in the right inferior
temporal lobe in right-handed individuals, though the left lobe is also active. Damage to this area
can cause prosopagnosia.

Patients with this abnormality can recognize forms and reproduce them. They can recognize
people by their voices, and many of them show autonomic responses when they see familiar as
opposed to unfamiliar faces. However, they cannot identify the familiar faces they see. The left
hemisphere is also involved, but the role of the right hemisphere is primary.

The presence of autonomic response to a familiar face in the absence of recognition has been
explained by postulating the existence of a separate dorsal pathway for processing information

about faces that leads to recognition at only a subconscious level. % fe"

Note: ee

a A ie
¢ Autotopagnosia - inability to recognize or to orient any-patt of one's.oWii body

¢ Simultagnosia - inability to recognize multiple eleménts ina visual sib te one object or
some elements of a scene can be appreciated butsot the display-a as a whole (°

* Somatagnosia - inability to identify any patt "of the body,.either one ‘sown or another’s body

* Tactile agnosia (astereognosis) - inability to recognide objects by‘toueh, in the presence of intact
cutaneous and proprioceptive,hahd Sensation;,caused by a lesion in the contralateral parietal lobe

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

355 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 356 ========================================
The total number of airway generations present in between the trachea and alveolar sac is 23.

The tracheobronchial tree is divided into multiple generations based on Weibel’s lung model. It
begins at the trachea (designated as zero) and ends with alveoli (last generation). There are 2
zones:

* Conducting zone

* Consists of the first 16 generations

* Incapable of gas exchange

¢ Respiratory zone

* Consists of the last 7 generations (17-23)
* Capable of gas exchange ah

Terminal bronchiole is the transition point between the

Solution to Question 2:

Among the given options, bronchi (medium-sized airway) offer maximum resistance.

The best answer here is bronchi, as all the other options suggest smaller airways, while bronchi
are the only medium-sized airway.

Airflow can be derived from Poiseuille-Hagen formula:
Q =n APr4/8yL

Where

* Q is the flow rate

* mis pie symbol

+ AP is the pressure difference

356 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 357 ========================================
*ris radius

* 7 is viscosity

* Lis length

Also,

Flow rate (Q) = pressure difference (AP)/ resistance (R).
Therefore, substituting this in Poiseuille- Hagen formula,
AP/ R= 1 APr4/8nl

V/R=n 1r4/8nl

h

R= 8nl/nr4 * .-
Therefore it can be derived that airway resistance is inversely proportional to'the radius by
the power of four. 4 |

With Poiseuille- Hagen formula we might feel thatthe fnallest airways are the major site of
resistance. However, the contribution by the smallest airways.i8.very little to*%the overall total
resistance. This is because, the airway branches‘ in each generation exist.in parallel rather than in
series. As the parallel branching increases} the resistance decreases. (Fota cross-sectional
diameter of small airways put togethers high). Thus ‘the overall resistance of the small airways is
very small. Also, airflow velocity decreases substantially as theleffective cross-sectional area
increases (i.e., the flow becomes Pinay) se ‘ ms,

Other factors affecting airway resistance, ?

¢ Lung volume, as lung volume i inéreases the resistance decreases and vice versa. Lung volume
increases the transairway pressure thus i increasing the caliber of the airway.

* Airway mucus, edema abd contraction ‘of bronchial smooth muscle increase the airway
resistance. Its clinicalppplication j is Seen i in asthma, bronchitis, and emphysema

+ The density and Viscosity of inspired gas also contribute to airway resistance. A gas with more
density ingpgrtsimore resisfanice.

lan

Solution to Question 3:
Chronic inflammation of conducting airways is suggestive of asthma. Lamellar bodies are storage
bodies of surfactant present in the alveoli and not the conducting airway.

The conducting airways form the first 16 generations from the trachea to the terminal bronchial.
It is lined by pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium. It also consists of smooth muscles
which are responsible for bronchoconstriction and dilatation. It also consists of other specialized
cells such as:

* Basal cells - stem cells
* Clara/Club cells - specialized epithelial cells (non-ciliated cuboidal cells) that:
* Functions as stem cells

* Helps in lung defense.

357 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 358 ========================================
The conducting airways secrete several substances that in lung defense such as:
* Secretory immunoglobins - IgA

* Collectins - surfactant protein(SP)-A and SP-D

* Defensins

* Reactive oxygen species

¢ Reactive nitrogen species.

Solution to Question 8:

Surfactant helps to prevent alveolar collapse according to Law of Laplace.(Not Bernoulli's)
Physiologic advantages of surfactant

* By reducing surface tension it increases lung compliance

* Reduces the lung's tendency to recoil

* Helps to keep the alveoli dry

* Helps to prevent pulmonary edema (Option A) C5"

The surface tension generates a pressure that tends to collapse the spheres» ee

In accordance with Law of Laplace - P=2T/r, where co _~

P - Collapsing pressure on alveolus (dynes/em2) or brassure required to keep alveolus open
(dynes/cm2) ai - i) %:

T = Surface tension (dynes/cm) ( » ; a z ra ‘

aay, te 4"
} 7%,
*y

Ls

r = Radius of the alveolus (cm). . _*s hh ct'

7 it
Collapsing pressure is inversely.related to raditis i.e. large alveolus (one with a large radius) will
have a low collapsing pressareand therefore will require only minimal pressure to keep it open.
On the other hand, a small alveolus (one-with a smalh tadius) will have a high collapsing pressure

and require more pressure to keep-it‘open. co.”
By reducing surface tension; Surfactant redti¢es the collapsing pressure for a given radius.

1 4,
Option B: Surfactant deficiency is anjeyportant cause of infant respiratory distress syndrome
(IRDS) also known as hyaline membrane disease, the serious pulmonary disease that develops in
infants born before their surfactant system is functional.

ri ee ig, “ties
+ In IRDS},there is high surface tension in the lungs resulting in atelectasis
¢ Also, there is retention of fluid in the lungs.

* During fetal life, Cl- is secreted with fluid by the pulmonary epithelial cells. At birth, there is a
shift to Na+ reabsorption by these cells via ENaC and fluid is absorbed along with Na+. Prolonged
immaturity of ENaC contributes to pulmonary edema in IRDS.

Option D: Surfactant increases lung compliance, which reduces the work of expanding the lungs
during inspiration. Increasing the compliance of the lungs reduces the collapsing force at any
given volume so that it is easier for the lungs to expand.

Solution to Question 9:

The mechanism of action of surfactant is by breaking the structure of water in alveoli.

When water forms a surface with air, the water molecules on the surface of the alveoli have a
strong attraction for one another. This pulls the molecules together, leading to high surface

361 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 362 ========================================
tension.

The surfactant is amphipathic in nature (i.e., hydrophobic on one end and hydrophilic on the
other). They align themselves on the alveolar surface, with their hydrophobic portions facing the
alveolar lumen.

The cohesive force between a water molecule and an adjacent pulmonary surfactant molecule is
very low. So, they break up the attracting forces between water molecules lining the alveoli (which
had been responsible for the high surface tension). Thus, they reduce surface tension.

Lung surfactant action

Alveoli with surfactant

MARROW

4 oy
Solution to Question 10 sy
7 rk

Stability of alveoliis maintained ’by increased alveolar surface area by surfactant.

ri a ig, “Nee
Surfactant.feduces the lung's tendency to recoil so that they do not collapse easily and so the
stability of the alveoli is maintained.

Factors that maintain alveolar stability

* Pulmonary surfactant: J surface tension within alveoli

* Mechanical interdependence: prevents alveolar collapse
Interdependence

The tendency of one alveolus to collapse is opposed by the traction exerted by the surrounding
alveoli. Thus, the collapse of a single alveolus stretches and distorts the surrounding alveoli, which
in turn are connected to other alveoli. This support offered to lung units by those surrounding
them is termed Interdependence.

Solution to Question 11:

Surfactant appears in the amniotic fluid between 28 and 32 weeks of gestation.

362 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 363 ========================================
By week 20-24, primitive alveoli form and surfactant production begins but it does not reach the
surface of the lungs until later.

Mature levels of pulmonary surfactant are present usually after 35 wk of gestation.
Clinical aspects :

* Preterm infants develop respiratory distress syndrome or hyaline membrane disease due to
surfactant deficiency.

« Antenatal corticosteroids are given to increase the production of surfactants.

* Lecithin/Sphingomyelin ratio is a test for fetal lung maturity. L/S ratio &gt; 2 is suggestive of a
mature fetal lung.

Solution to Question 2:

Intrapleural pressure is usually negative. (normally -2.5 mmHg)

During inspiration, it becomes more negative. (-6 mmHg).

During forced expiration, contraction of the expiratory muscles raises intrapleural pressure to a

positive value.

Intrapleural pressure is the pressure of the fluid in the thin space between the visceral and

parietal pleura.

372 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 373 ========================================
Solution to Question 3:

The intrapleural pressure is negative both during inspiration and expiration because the thoracic
cage and lung recoil in opposite direction.

The 2 factors that lead to a negative or subatmospheric intrapleural pressure are:

* The lungs, with their elastic properties, tend to collapse, and the chest wall, with its elastic
properties, tends to spring out. When these two opposing forces pull on the intrapleural space, a
negative pressure, or vacuum, is created.

i. a

¢ The continuous suction of excess fluid into the lymphatic channels. SS
-

if

In cases of pneumothorax \intrapleural pressure suddenly becomes equal to atmospheric pressure.
Thus instead of its normal negative value, intrapleural pressure becomes zero. Hence lungs
collapse and the chest wall spring out.

Solution to Question 4:
The normal intrapleural pressure at the beginning of inspiration is about -3.4 cm H20 (-2.5
mmHg).

Intrapleural pressure is the pressure of the fluid in the thin space between the lung pleura and the
chest wall pleura. During normal inspiration, expansion of the chest cage pulls outward on the
lungs with greater force and creates more negative pressure.

At the peak of normal inspiration, it is about -8.15 cm H20 (-5.4 mm Hg).
Note: imm Hg = 1.36 cm H20

373 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 374 ========================================
Solution to Question 5:

At the end of inspiration, the alveolar pressure is zero with reference to the atmospheric pressure.

Whenever alveolar pressure equals atmospheric pressure, alveolar pressure is said to be zero. This
happens when no air is moving in or out of the lungs.

During inspiration, the diaphragm contracts, causing the volume of the thorax to increase. As lung
volume increases, the pressure in the lungs must decrease. (Boyle’s law states that P x V is
constant at a given temperature.) Halfway through inspiration, the alveolar pressure falls below
atmospheric pressure (—1 cm H20). The pressure gradient between the atmosnhecnid the alveoli
drives airflow into the lung. Air flows into the lungs until, at the end of inspirati n, alveolar
pressure is once again equal to the atmospheric pressure; "> ae gradient between the
atmosphere and the alveoli has dissipated, and airflomey the tangs

During expiration, alveolar pressure rises to about itm of Ha9
xP ao Ces |
ce ‘7 aS

Solution to Question 7:

374 Sold by @itachibot

Compliance is change in volume per unit change in pressure.

* C= AV/AP

In this case,

AV= 1000 mL

AP= -12-(-4) = -8 cm of H20

The minus sign indicates that the pressure has fallen by 8cms of H20 at the end of inspiration
Therefore the compliance is,

C= 1000/8= 125 mL/cm of H20

Solution to Question 8: No ~ i, %
The transpulmonary pressure would be 12 cmH2Q Waton she inhales 60omL of air.

Transpulmonary pressure (Ptp) is the differénce between alveolar pressuré (Palv) and intrapleural
pressure (Pip). It is given by the formula}, Ptp = Palv-Pip! Inspiration calisés a negative change in
both the Palv and Pip. Lung complianeg i is the extent to which the Jiings will expand for each unit
increase in transpulmonary pressure: i fs

In a normal adult, the pulmdiity complianiceis 0.2 L/emH20 or 200 mL/cmH20 of both lungs
together. Hence, expanding the lungs by 200 mL of inspired air will require an increase in Ptp by
1cm H20. ts + y

. 2 a
Given: ti he 7

Pulmonary compliance =$2 2L/em PG 200 ml/em H20
Alveolar pressure at, de beginning of i inspiration = 4 cmH20
Intrapleural pressure at the . béginning of inspiration = -5 cmH20
Volume oPatt inhaled = 6oomL (0.6L)

Step 1: Calculate the initial transpulmonary pressure (Ptp).

Transpulmonary pressure = Alveolar pressure - Intrapleural pressure; Initial transpulmonary
pressure = 4 cmH20 - (-5 cmH20) = 9 cmH20

Step 2: Use the lung compliance formula to find the change in transpulmonary pressure.
Lung compliance = Change in volume / Change in transpulmonary pressure.

Pulmonary compliance is 0.2 L/emH20 or 200 mL/cmH20. Hence, to expand the lungs by 200
mL of inspired air, it will require an increase in Ptp by 1 cm H20. So, for 600 ml inspired air, the
compliance is = 600 mL/ 200 mL/cm H20 = 3 cmH20

Step 3: Calculate the transpulmonary pressure (Ptp) after inhaling 600mL of air.

During inspiration, the alveolar pressure (Palv) decreases, and the intrapleural pressure (Pip)
becomes more negative.

Final transpulmonary pressure = Initial transpulmonary pressure + change in transpulmonary
pressure; Final transpulmonary pressure = 9 cmH20 + 3 cmH20 = 12 cmH20.

375 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 376 ========================================
Solution to Question 9:

A liquid-ventilated lung is more compliant compared to a gas-ventilated lung.

Compliance is a measure of distensibility. In a gas-filled lung, surface tension and elastic recoil
forces act to collapse the lung and thus reduce compliance.

¢ When the lungs are filled with, air, there is aujinterface between the alveolar fluid and the air in

+ But, in the case of thé'saline solution@filled lungs, there is no air—fluid interface and there is
no surface tension, making it mere compliant.

* Hence, the pressure—volumé-curve obtained with saline measures only the tissue elasticity.

- The difference in the curves generated during inflation and deflation, termed hysteresis, is not
present in the saline-generated curves.

Solution to Question 10:

Surfactant is the factor responsible for hysteresis.

Difference between the curve of inflation (inspiration) and deflation (expiration) in a
pressure—volume curve of the lung is termed as hysteresis.

The alveolar environment and, specifically, the secreted factors that help reduce surface tension
and keep alveoli from collapsing, contribute to hysteresis.

Solution to Question 12:

In the given pressure-volume graph:

« A— Compliance of chest

* B — Compliance of lung

* C— Compliance of lung and chest wall

The equilibrium position is at functional residual capacity (FRC), where the expanding force on
the chest wall is exactly equal to the collapsing force on the lungs. FRC is also known as relaxation
volume and represents the amount of air remaining in the lungs at the end of normal expiration.

379 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 380 ========================================
At the point where volume= FRC,
* Intraalveolar pressure is equal to atmospheric pressure and considered as zero.
* Intrapleural pressure is negative (-2.5 mmHg)

¢ Transpulmonary pressure = Intraalveolar pressure — intrapleural pressure = 0 — (-2.5 mmHg) =
+2.5 mmHg.

For compliance of lung — Pressure considered is Transpulmonary pressure. So at FRC,
Transpulmonary pressure is positive (+2.5 mmHg). Hence, B indicates compliance of lung.

For compliance of chest wall — Pressure considered is intrapleural — atmospheric pressure. At
FRC, this value is -2.5 mmHg. Hence, A represents compliance of the chest wall.

When the volume is FRC, the combined lung and chest-wall curve intersects the Xcakis at an
airway pressure of zero. Hence, C represents the combined chest wall and lung-compliance.

* The compliance of the lungs is about 0.2 L/em H20.
Applied aspect:

In case of emphysema, compliance is increased but specific compliance decreases (because FRC is
severely elevated).

Solution to Question 14:

Work done in quiet breathing is 0.5 kg-m/min.
Estimates of the total work of quiet breathing range from 0.3 up to 0.8 kg-m/min.

The work of breathing can be calculated from the pressure-volume curve.

380 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 381 ========================================
The value rises markedly during exercise, but the energy cost of breathing in normal individuals
represents less than 3% of the total energy expenditure during exercise.

Applied aspect:

In the case of COPD, airway resistance increases (obstruction to outflow). Hence, the patient
should take slow, deep breathing.

In the case of restrictive lung disease, viscous resistance increases. Hence, the patient is advised
rapid, shallow breathing.

Solution to Question 17:

The difference between the inflation and the deflation paths is called hysteresis.

It exists because a greater transpulmonary pressure is required to open a previously closed
airway, due to a deficit of surfactant at the air-water interface, than to keep an open airway from
closing, as then it has abundant surfactant.

The pattern of breathing seen in the image is CheynesBtdkes breathing. In this case, the person
breathes deeply for a short interval and then breathes slightly or not for an additional interval.

This pattern repeats itself about every minute. = & he

again to normal, but the respiratory mechanisins again over-responds to CO2. Breathing ceases,

Solution to Question 2:

Time taken by a red cell is about 0.75 seconds to traverse the pulmonary capillaries at rest and 0.3
seconds or less during exercise.

alveolo-capillary membrane and inversely proportional to its thickness.

* The partial pressure thai water molecules exert to escape through the surface is called the
vapor pressure of the water.

* The vapor pressure of water depends entirely on the temperature of the water.

* The greater the temperature, the greater the kinetic activity of the molecules and, therefore, the
greater the likelihood that the water molecules will escape from the surface of the water into the
gas phase.

¢ For instance, the water vapor pressure at 0°C is 5 mm Hg, and at 100°C it is 760 mm Hg.

¢ The most important value to remember is the vapor pressure at body temperature 47 mm Hg.
(Once the equilibrium is attained after diffusion, vapour pressure is called partial pressure).

O02 158.0 100.0 116.0
C02 | 0.3 40.0 32.0
H20 | 5.7 47.0 47.0

Solution to Question 8:
In healthy individuals, anatomical dead space and physiologic dead space are almost equivalent,
and can be estimated by body weight.
Dead space is the volume of the airways and lungs that do not participate in gas exchange.
Types

« Anatomic dead space- Volume of the air-occupying conducting zone where no exchange of gases
takes place.

¢ Alveolar/functional dead space- the volume of gas not equilibrating with blood (wasted
ventilation)

Solution to Question 10:

Water fall effect has been used to describe the blood flow in the middle portion of lung.

394 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 395 ========================================
In this region blood flows in pulses. At first there is no flow because of obstruction at the
collapsed venous end of the capillary bed. Pressure from the arterial side builds up until it exceeds
alveolar pressure and flow resumes. This dissipates the capillary pressure and returns to the start
of the cycle.

The blood flow in this region is determined by the pulmonary artery—alveolar pressure difference.
Blood “falls” into the pulmonary veins, which are compliant and take whatever amount of blood
can flow into them. This has been called the waterfall effect.

VA represents Weventilation in ‘alveoli and Q represents the blood flow through the alveolus. The
ratio of VAiand'Q explains;thé respiratory exchange when there is an imbalance between alveolar

ventilation*and alveolar blood flow. The ventilation-perfusion ratio is considered normal if there is
an equal amount of blood flow &amp; ventilation through the alveolus.

VA /Q ratio is infinity when:
¢ There is adequate ventilation but no perfusion.

* No exchange of gases ( O2 and CO2 )through the respiratory membranes of corresponding
alveoli.

* The alveolar air equilibrates the humidified inspired air.

* Partial pressures of the O2 and CO2 are 149 mmHg &amp; 0 mm Hg respectively.

Solution to Question 14:

In emphysema, the destruction of the alveolar walls brings down the diffusion area.

397 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 398 ========================================
Changes in Emphysema
¢ Due to the destruction of the alveolar walls, there is a decrease in surface/diffusion area.
* Cardiac output will be normal.

* Pulmonary artery pressure may be elevated because the destruction of alveoli leads to loss of
surrounding capillaries which forces blood into remaining capillaries, which tends to increase
pulmonary artery pressure.

¢ Alveolar Pco2 will tend to increase due to difficulty in expiration.

¢ In case of emphysema — Decreased diffusion (DLCO) because of decreased surface,area (trypsin

Solution to Question 15: yt . cS,

During moderate exercise, ere i is no hartge i in arterial p02, arterial pCO2 or arterial pH.

Ventilation increases abruptly at.the’ chset of e ercise due to psychic stimuli and afferent impulses
from proprioceptors in muscles,'téndons, and-joints. This is followed by a brief pause and then a
gradual increase in ventilation. This gradtial-increase in ventilation is believed to be due to
multiple factors such as‘an increase in body temperature, increase in plasma K+ level stimulating
peripheral chemoreceptors, or thetensitivity of the neurons controlling the response to CO2.

In strenuous exercise, at a certain point, the O2 uptake reaches its maximum levels, beyond which
it cannot im¢rease further! \F his leads to a rise in blood lactate levels. Buffering of lactic acid
liberates CO2 which further increases ventilation. The increased production of lactic acid, increase
in ventilation and CO2 production are proportional, and alveolar and arterial CO2 changes little to
nil. This is called isocapnic buffering.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Boyle’s law states that, at a given temperature, the product of pressure time’s volume for a gas is
constant.

PV = nRT

404 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 405 ========================================
Solution to Question 2: a

~~

Transport of CO is a becatige of the high affinity of CO to hemoglobin.
O ocapillary fnembrane:
* Blood takes ogo traversesptlmonary capillaries.

*CO diffuses into blood Ge tombines with Hb with very high affinity. Thus the partial pressure of
CO doesn't rise (Only dissolved form of gas can increase the partial pressure, Gas which combines
with Hb or plasma proteins cannot). Partial pressure gradient necessary for diffusion of CO is
maintained for the whole 0.75s and hence it is Diffusion limited.

Diffusion across the

* In the case of N20 it does not react and reaches equilibrium in about 0.1s. The amount of N20
taken up is not limited by diffusion but by the amount of blood flowing through the pulmonary
capillaries; that is, it is Flow-limited.

* O2 is intermediate between N20 and CO; it is taken up by haemoglobin, but much less avidly
than CO, and it reaches equilibrium with capillary blood in about 0.3 s. Thus, its uptake is
Perfusion-limited.

In the case of perfusion limited gases, an increase in pulmonary blood flow increases the amount
of gas transferred. Hence O2 transport increases with an increase in pulmonary blood flow.

Solution to Question 3:

405 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 406 ========================================
Percentage of O2 carried chemically (bound to Hb) is 97%.
Oxygen is transported in the blood in two forms:

* physically 3% - dissolved in plasma

* chemically 97% - bound to hemoglobin.

Remember:

Gases are transported in 3 forms:

* Dissolved gas - This is the form which contributes to partial pressure. Nitrogen is carried only in
dissolved form in blood.

* Bound gas - O2, CO are bound to Hb in RBC; CO2 is bound to Hb in RBC as wellas"plasma
protein. o* z]

* Chemically modified gas - CO2 in the form of HCO3-. oC oe

Solution to Question 4:

* Normal arterial blood has Po2 ofabout 100 mm He.
Amount of O02 transported, in'dissolved fone (Calculated by using Henry's law):

* Concentration of dissgigead ( O02 = Partial pressure of O02 x solubility of O2 = 100 mmHg x 0.003
mL 02/100 mL bload per mm Hee 6:3 mL 02/100 mL

* Hence 1L of. plasma has gmLof 02 in dissolved form.

* 5L of plasaia can carry maximum 15mL of 02.

¢ At rest, O2 demand is around 250mL/min.

* Hence dissolved O2 alone is not sufficient to meet body demands.
Amount of 02 transported bound to Hb:

* Concentration of Hb is 15g/100 mL

¢ Each gram of Hb binds to 1.34 mL of O02

* Hence, 15 g binds to 20.1mL of 02/100 mL

* On average, the 15 g of hemoglobin in 100 mL of blood can combine with a total of about 20 mL
of O02.

Thus, this value (20.1) in the presence of Hb is about 70 times the value (0.3) in the absence of
Hb.

Hemoglobin has 4 subunits &&ch of which. has heme molecule and polypeptide chain. Ferrous in

Solution to Question 8:

A relaxed (R) configuration, exposes more O 2 binding sites.
The quaternary structure of hemoglobin determines its affinity for O2.

In deoxyhemoglobin, the globin units are tightly bound in a tense (T) configuration, which
reduces the affinity of the molecule for O2.

When O 2 is first bound, the bonds holding the globin units are released, producing a relaxed (R)
configuration, which exposes more O2 binding sites. The net result is a 500-fold increase in O02

In tissues, these reactions are reversed, resulting in O2 dissociation .

Oxygenated and deoxygenated hemoglobin ~

Oxyhemoglobin Deoxyhemoglobin

Decreasing PH
ge Increasing 2,3-DPG

Falling [O,)

Relaxing Binding Tight Binding

Solution to éstion 9: se!
ou ©

The oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve is sigmoid shape because binding of one 02 molecule
increases the affinity of binding of subsequent O2 molecules.

The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve relates percentage saturation of the O2 carrying power
of hemoglobin (abbreviated as SaO2) to the Po2. It has a characteristic sigmoid shape due to the
T-R interconversion. Combination of the first heme in the Hb molecule with O2 increases the
affinity of the second heme for O2 and oxygenation of the second increases the affinity of the
third, and so on, such that the affinity of Hb for the fourth O2 molecule is many times that for the
first (Positive Co-operative Effect).

Option C: The effect of PCO2 and pH on the O2-hemoglobin dissociation curve is called the Bohr
effect.

Option D: O2 bound to hemoglobin changes its affinity for CO2, such that when less O2 is bound,
the affinity of hemoglobin for CO2 increases. This is referred to as the Haldane effect.

408 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 409 ========================================
Solution to Question 10:

When blood is equilibrated with 100% O2, the normal hemoglobin becomes 100% saturated.

* In vivo, the hemoglobin in the blood at the end of the pulmonary capillaries is about 97.5%
saturated with O2 (Po2 = 100 mm Hg).

* Because of a slight admixture with venous blood that bypasses the pulmonary capillaries (i.e,
physiologic shunt), the hemoglobin in systemic arterial blood is only 97% saturated.

* Graph relates the percentage of saturated hemoglobin (SaO2) to PO2 and dissolved O2.

Solution to Question 11:

Normal P50 is about 27 mm of Hg for adult Hb.

P50 is the partial pressure of O2 at which Hb is 50 % saturated. P50 is an index of the affinity of
Hb with O2.

Higher the P50 value, lower is the affinity: Oxygen-Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve shifts to right.

Lower the P50 value, the higher is the affinity: Oxygen- Hemoglobin Dissociation Curve shifts to
left.

Solution to Question 14:

411 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 412 ========================================
Facilitation of oxygen transport depends on the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve, explained
by the Bohr effect.

When the metabolic activity of tissues increases, the production of CO2 increases. This CO2
diffuses into the blood and in turn, raises the blood H2CO3 and causes an increase in the H+
concentration and a decrease in pH. This decreases the affinity of Hb for 02, shifting the 02-
hemoglobin dissociation curve to the right. This facilitates the unloading of O2 in the tissues. The
effect of Pco2 and pH on O2 hemoglobin dissociation curve is called the Bohr Effect.

Conversely, in the lungs, CO2 diffuses from the blood to the alveoli and the H+ concentration
decreases and theO2 hemoglobin dissociation curve shifts to the left increasing the affinity of Hb
to O2.

h

Note: a .-

blood and maternal blood move in opposite directions. _ (~~ os

it diffuse from the fetal blood into the matértial'blood. .. =< mts

* Maternal blood - increase in,COg - ‘increase in fi shift to the foht - Release of oxygen.

Solution to Question 15: a ie

wt wh
The level of 2,3—DPG is Ugifeised in acidosis.

2,3-DPG is produced from 3- phosphdglycerate which is a product of glycolysis. Acidosis inhibits
red cell glycolysis which decreasés*the levels of 2,3-DPG.

Solution to Question 16:

Increased affinity of HbF to O2 is due to decreased affinity of y chains of HbF to 2,3DPG.
HDF is the fetal variant of hemoglobin.

The B chains of adult hemoglobin (hemoglobin A) are replaced by y chains in HbF. This
modification results in increased affinity of hemoglobin for 02, a left shift of the O2-hemoglobin
dissociation curve, and decreased P50.

The mechanism of the left shift is based on the binding of 2,3-DPG. 2,3-DPG does not bind as
avidly to the y chains of HbF as it binds to the B chains of hemoglobin A. When less 2,3-DPG is
bound, the affinity for O2 increases and facilitates the movement of O2 from the mother to the
fetus.

This increased affinity is beneficial to the fetus, whose PaO2 is low (approximately 40 mm Hg).

412 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 413 ========================================
Solution to Question 17:

Binding of O2 with hemoglobin tends to displace CO2 from the blood. This effect is called the
Haldane effect. (Remember - Haldane effect occurs in lungs and causes left shift of O02-Hb
dissociation curve)

Haldane Effect

* Binding of O2 with hemoglobin tends to displace CO2 from the blood. This effect, called the
Haldane effect, is important in promoting CO2 transport.

* The Haldane effect results from the simple fact that the combination of O02 with hemoglobin in
the lungs causes the hemoglobin to become a stronger acid. This displaces CO2 from the blood

and into the alveoli. Pd

Chloride shift: me
oride shi a os, ot

Chloride shift

~N

MARROW

* Upon entering the red blood cell, CO2 is rapidly hydrated to H2CO3 by carbonic anhydrase.
¢ H2CO3 is in equilibrium with H+ and its conjugate base, HCO3-.

¢ H+ can interact with deoxyhemoglobin, whereas HCO3- can be transported outside of the cell via
anion exchanger 1 (AE1 or Band 3).

* In effect, for each CO2 molecule that enters the red cell, there is an additional HCO3- or Cl- in
the cell.

413 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 414 ========================================
* Because of this chloride shift, the Cl — content of the red cells in venous blood is significantly
greater than that in arterial blood.

* Note that for each CO2 molecule added to a red cell, there is an increase of one osmotically
active particles in the cell - either a HCO 3 — or a Cl — ion. Consequently, the red cells take up
water and increase in size.

Solution to Question 19:

The fall in 2, 3 DPG is less when the blood is stored in CPDA compared with ACD. _

Solution to Question 20:

The oxygen carrying capacity of a boy with haemoglobin 14¢/d] is 18.

When blood is saturated with 100% O2, the haemoglobin is also 100% saturated and each gram of
normal haemoglobin contains 1.39 mL of O2.

However, blood normally contains some inactive haemoglobin derivatives, and the measured
value in vivo is thus slightly lower hence, each gram of haemoglobin in vivo contains 1.31 mL of
O2 (Hufner’s constant).

According to the above question, when Hb=14¢/dl, O2= 1.31*14= 18.34
The oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve

* Demonstrates a progressive increase in the percentage of haemoglobin bound with oxygen as
blood Po2 increases, which is called the percent saturation of haemoglobin.

414 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 415 ========================================
* Blood leaving the lungs and entering the systemic arteries usually has a Po2 of about 95 mm Hg,
the usual oxygen saturation of systemic arterial blood averages 97 per cent.

* Conversely, in normal venous blood returning from the peripheral tissues, the Po2 is about 40
mm Hg, and the saturation of haemoglobin averages 75 per cent.

Solution to Question 1:

The tidal volume = inspiratory capacity - inspiratory reserve volume.

Solution to Question 2:

Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is expiratory reserve volume + residual volume.

From the picture given below, (FRC = ERV + RV).

Lung Capacities: ‘tl 5 ins!
The total lung capacity can be broken down into alternative capacities that help to define
functioning lungs. ra, aa

* The vital lung capacity réfers to the maximum amount of air expired from the fully inflated lung
or maximum inspiratory level.

¢ The inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air inspired from the end-expiratory level.

¢ The functional residual capacity represents the volume of the air remaining in the lungs after
expiration of a normal breath.

Solution to Question 3:
Expiratory Reserve Volume = Functional Residual Capacity - Residual Volume
In the given spirogram,
¢ Functional Residual Capacity = 3L
* Residual Volume = 1L

Therefore,

424 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 425 ========================================
Expiratory Reserve Volume = Functional Residual Capacity — Residual Volume = 3-1 = 2
Note:
The residual volume is the volume of air remaining in the lungs after the most forceful expiration.

The functional residual capacity equals the expiratory reserve volume plus the residual volume.
This capacity is the amount of air that remains in the lungs at the end of normal expiration

The expiratory reserve volume is the maximum extra volume of air that can be expired by forceful
expiration after the end of a normal tidal expiration.

Determinants of Lung Compliance
* Stretchability of the lung tissues, particularly their elastic connective tissue.

¢ Surface tension at the air-water interfaces within the alveoli, which is markedly reduced in the

Solution to Questipwps: is
*, F
The rate ofvalveolar ventilation in this patient is 7.5 L/min.

Alveolar ventilation per minute is the total volume of new air entering the alveoli and adjacent gas

exchange areas each minute. It is equal to the respiratory rate times the amount of new air that
enters these areas with each breath.

Alveolar ventilation=Respiratory rate X (Tidal volume — Physiologic dead space)
= 15 X (650-150)

= 7500mL/min or 7.5L/min.

Solution to Question 10:
The closing volume (CV) is the lung volume above the residual volume (RV) at which the airways

in the lower dependent parts of the lung begin to close off because of the lesser transmural
pressure in these areas.

427 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 428 ========================================
In young adults, the closing volume is about 10% of the vital capacity (VC). It increases steadily
with age and is equal to about 40% of the VC. It equals FRC, at about the age of 65 years.

Diseases of the small airways apparently increase the closing volume
* Normal young adult: CV &lt;&lt; FRC

¢ At 65 years of age: CV=FRC

¢ Small airway disease: CV &gt;&gt;FRC

* In case of hyaline membrane disease, FRC is smaller than closing volume.

Solution to Question 13:

elastic tendency of the lungs to collange.ts exactly palaiced by the elastic tendency of the chest

wall to expand. ai ‘cs eC

normally subatmospherio, becomes equal to atmospheric pressure. The chest wall springs
outward and the lungs ¢ollapse. This'in¢reases the thoracic volume and decreases the lung
volume. = “na = *,

Solution to Question 14:

The forced vital capacity of this man is 4.5 liters.

The forced vital capacity (FVC), the largest amount of air that can be expired after a maximal
inspiratory effort, is frequently measured clinically as an index of pulmonary function.

Forced vital capacity(FVC) = Total lung capacity (TLC) — Residual volume(RV)

* In the flow-volume curve, the point of intersection between abscissa and flow-volume curve gives
the TLC and RV.

* From the given flow-volume curve we can note that:
* Total Lung Capacity (TLC) = 5.5 L

* Residual volume (RV)= 1.0 L

¢ Thus,

Solution to Question 16:

The green curve in the above image and the case scenario denotes that the diagnosis is likely to be
emphysema.

Maximum expiratory flow volume (MEFV): When a person expires with great force, the expiratory
airflow reaches a maximum flow beyond which the flow cannot be increased anymore, even with
greatly increased additional force.

In obstructive airway disease like Emphysema, it is usually much more difficult to expire than to
inspire because the closing tendency of the airways is greatly increased by the extra positive
pressure during expiration. Due to the obstruction of the airways and because they collapse more
easily than normal airways, the maximum expiratory flow rate is greatly reduced (But, total lung
capacity and residual volume is increased) which is shown in the green curve below.

Other options given above will cause constricted lungs where the lungs cannot expand to a normal
maximum volume. Hence, even with the greatest possible expiratory effort, the maximum

431 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 432 ========================================
expiratory flow rate will be reduced. (Note: here both the total lung capacity and the residual
volume are reduced), which is shown as the blue curve in the graph below.

Following graph shows the MEFV of restrictive and obstructive lung diseases.

= L
In COPD, the functional residuabeapacity increases. Total lung capacity may be increased or
normal. Hence, both 1 and 2-axé correct, _ "Ps

ih
* In obstructive lung diseases, both FEV1 and FVC reduces, where the decrease in FEV1 is more
than the reduction in EVC. Asa result, the ratio of FEV1/FVC also decreases.

my, sl a
* The total Jung, eapacity is usually normal in chronic bronchitis, while increased in emphysema.

*h Aa
° Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is the volume of air present in the lungs at the end of passive
expiration. In COPD, FRC is increased.

Lung Capacities:

The total lung capacity can be broken down into alternative capacities that help to define
functioning lungs.

¢ The vital lung capacity refers to the maximum amount of air expired from the fully inflated lung
or maximum inspiratory level.

¢ The inspiratory capacity is the maximum amount of air inspired from the end-expiratory level.

¢ The functional residual capacity represents the volume of the air remaining in the lungs after
expiration of a normal breath. Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) is expiratory reserve volume +
residual volume.

Solution to Question 18:

432 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 433 ========================================
The stimulus to ventilation after strenuous exercise is increased mean arterial H+.

The respiratory rate after exercise does not reach a basal level until the 02 debt is repaid. The
stimulus for ventilation is increased mean arterial H+ due to an increase in blood lactic acid

levels. Most of the lactic acid is converted to glycogen and some amount is converted to CO2 and
H20.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:
Hypoxic hypoxia is characterized by low partial pressure of O2 in arterial blood (Pao2), while
other parameters like partial pressure of CO2 in arterial blood (Paco2) may remain normal.
Ventilation—Perfusion Imbalance:

* The patchy ventilation—perfusion imbalance is the most common cause of hypoxic hypoxia or
hypoxemia.

¢ If alveoli are not ventilated but perfusion is present - blood passing through that alveoli remains
unoxygenated.

439 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 440 ========================================
¢ When this unoxygenated blood mixes with blood from normal or overventilated alveoli, there is
a right to left shunt created.

* This results in reduced partial pressure of O2 in arterial blood (PaO2) i.e., hypoxemia and
consequently hypoxia.

Note:

¢ Hypoxemia can be defined as a condition where arterial oxygen tension or partial pressure of
oxygen (PaO2) is below normal.

* Hypoxia is defined as the reduction of oxygen supply at the tissue level, which is not measured

directly by a laboratory value.

Scenario 1: . &

* Generally, the presence of hypoxemia suggests hypoxia i.e. if there is less oxygen in blood, O02

supply to tissues will also be reduced. 3) * PA
ty

* But, in case of cyanide poisoning, there is hypoxia,ii the absenceof nypoxemia, i.e., arterial O2
content is normal but tissues are not able to utilize it because cyanide interferes with aerobic

metabolism in the tissues. ne i the

cs %,
¢ Hypoxia may not be present in pitignts with hifebeeaia if the patient compensates for a low
PaO2 by increasing oxygen delivery. This is typically achieved: by increasing cardiac output (by

raising the heart rate) or decreasing tissue, oxygen consurmption.

Scenario 2: fis

* Nevertheless, hypoxemia is definitely the most cemiaon cause of tissue hypoxia, and the correct
diagnosis should be made becaus¢“it is readily treatable.
ot: cs)
i. a i,
a) a
i |

Solution to Question)2: ra
In the cas¢‘of ahemic hypoxia O2 content is low but dissolved O2 is within normal limits, so it
does not stimulate periphéral chemoreceptors.

Peripheral chemoreceptors located in the carotid and aortic arteries sense changes in
Po2(&lt;60mm Hg), pH, and Pco2 and they respond to changes in dissolved O02(PaO2).

Histotoxic hypoxia will lead to metabolic acidosis that will lead to an increase in H+
concentration, which in turn will stimulate the peripheral chemoreceptors.

Hemorrhage that produces hypotension leads to chemoreceptor stimulation due to decreased
blood flow to the chemoreceptors and consequent stagnant anoxia of these organs.

Note: In case of stagnant hypoxia, chemoreceptors can be stimulated if the resultant decreased
oxygen delivery causes a significant drop in local tissue oxygen levels, which eventually can lower
the PaO2 detectable by chemoreceptors. Therefore, they may not be significantly stimulated
unless the reduction in oxygen levels is severe. Therefore, anaemic hypoxia is a better answer.

Solution to Question 3:

440 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 441 ========================================
In stagnant hypoxia, blood flow is very low; so, more oxygen is extracted causing an increase in
reduced hemoglobin — cyanosis.

Cyanosis - Cyanosis is the dusky bluish discoloration of the tissues occurring when the reduced
hemoglobin concentration is more than 5g/dL.

* Its occurrence depends upon

¢ The total amount of hemoglobin in the blood
* Degree of hemoglobin unsaturation

* The state of capillary circulation

Option a: In the case of anemia, the total hemoglobin content is low, so enough reduced

hemoglobin cannot be formed. * _
Option b: In histotoxic hypoxia, the amount of reduced hemoglobin formed is very less because
the defect is in the tissue O2. / al mn,
Me
Option c: In CO poisoning, there is the formation 6f,¢atboxyhemeglobin, which is red in color
obscure the color of reduced hemoglobin. | .."*s™ of i."

Solution to Question 4: i i

he i LL,
hs

f “ cs
Administration of oxygen has maximum beriefit in hypoxic hypoxia.

iF L J
Hypoxic hypoxia refers to a conditiow.in which therelis low atmospheric Po2, leading to low
arterial Po2. Hence, supplemental O02 can be, very beneficial when the cause of hypoxia is due to

limited oxygen availability. | ‘ ‘<

Administration of ojegiich gas has-limited value in anemic, histotoxic, and stagnant hypoxia

because: <

ms t
Anemic hypoxia*Oxygen delivéry is not beneficial because despite increasing the exogenous
oxygen délivery, an anemic person will not have the capacity to carry more oxygen due to the

hemoglobin/ red blood cell deficit in anemia.

Histotoxic hypoxia- The most common example is cyanide poisoning. Cyanide inhibits important
enzymes in the oxidative phosphorylation process, which is essential to the survival of our cells.
Additional oxygen supplementation will not be able to overcome this inhibition, and so it will have
limited benefit. Hyperbaric oxygen may be helpful to some extent.

Stagnant hypoxia- Stagnant hypoxia refers to ischemic hypoxia that results from inadequate
circulation due to conditions such as heart failure and hypoxia in the setting of shock. In such
conditions, administering intravenous fluids can improve the patient's condition because it is the
circulation that is affected. If the shock is prolonged it can result in acute respiratory distress
syndrome (ARDS), which can be treated with positive pressure ventilation and not simply by
increasing oxygen administration.

Solution to Question 5:

441 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 442 ========================================
With O2 therapy, increasing the inspired Po2 will result in an increased amount of dissolved O2.
At high altitudes, there is low barometric pressure and so less Po2, hence, supplemental 02 will
help.

Option A: With anemia, the hemoglobin is fully saturated and O2 will have minimal effect.
Treatment of anemia is more beneficial than providing oxygen to an anemic patient.

Option B: With COPD there is CO2 retention due to difficulty in the expiration, and there will be a
decreased Po2. So, there is a change in the regulation of respiration (drive for ventilation) from
being under CO2 control to being driven by the decrease in O02. Giving supplemental O2 to such a
person will increase arterial Po2, thus decrease the stimulus to breathe, resulting in patient
stopping breathing.

Option C: Cyanide poisoning results in decreased O2 utilization by the tissues and'so additional
O2 would be of no benefit. o*

1s aa "h,
i. J

Solution to Question 8:
Normal Arterial Po2, /Mixed venous Po2, T2,3-DPG are the set of changes that will be seen in the
above patient.

The oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood is reduced in an anemic person, but the arterial Po2
and oxygen saturation are both normal.

The decrease in arterial O2 content is compensated for by an increase in the extraction of oxygen
from hemoglobin, which reduces the Po2 of the venous blood.

The unloading of oxygen at tissue level is enhanced by an increased level of 2,3-DPG in an anemic
patient because 2,3-DPG causes a right shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve.

442 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 443 ========================================
Solution to Question 9:

Curve C corresponds to blood from an adult (dark blue line) and blood from a fetus (light blue
line). The fetal oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve is shifted to the left relative to the adult
curve.

The fetal hemoglobin (Hb F) has a greater affinity for O2 than adult hemoglobin (Hb A). This
facilitates the movement of 02 from the mother to fetus. The cause of this greater affinity is the
poor binding of 2,3-DPG by ™ polypeptide chains that replace 8 chains in Hb F. The fetal
oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve is thus shifted to the left relative to the adultcurve.
Typically, fetal arterial Po2 is low, hence leftward shift enhances placental uptake%of O2.

* F
ain He A
et ais Mh,

The right shift of the oxygen-hemoglobin-ciirve allows moreOxygen to befeleased to the muscle at
a given Po2 in the blood. The oxygen dissociation CUYE, shifts to theright when factors like:

Solution to Question 10:

¢ Increased temperature resulting NGhh heat gendihed by the gxertising muscle,
¢ Higher carbon dioxide gees feleased front the muscle tissue,

+ Elevated 2,3- Bisphosphoglycerate ( (2;33BPG), and. @

* Increased hydrogen ions concentration in the gustle capillary blood

Above factors decrease hemoglobin’ s oxygen “affinity. This shift promotes easier oxygen release to
muscles and other tissues"

Note: Despite the rightward shifty the 1 maximum hemoglobin oxygen saturation at high oxygen
levels remains unehanged. Theplateau on the curve represents the maximal saturation achievable
under optimal conditions, Siisuring hemoglobin can still be fully saturated with oxygen.

Solution to Question 11:

Decompression sickness is seen in pilots, mountaineers, and divers.

Decompression sickness (DCS) or diver’s disease, the bends, or Caisson disease is the name given
to a variety of symptoms experienced by a person exposed to a reduction in the pressure
surrounding their body.

It can be seen in
* Divers ascending rapidly from deep-sea (high pressure) to the surface of the earth (low pressure)

¢ Pilots traveling in unpressurized aircraft or mountaineers, rapidly ascending from the surface of
the earth (high-pressure zone) to a high altitude (low-pressure zone).

Under high-pressure, gases get compressed and their solubility increases. When a person is in a
high-pressure zone like the deep sea for a long time, an increased amount of nitrogen gets

443 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 444 ========================================
dissolved in his or her body. If the person suddenly comes back to an area of atmospheric
pressure or a low-pressure zone, the gases escape from a dissolved state and form bubbles. These
bubbles are almost entirely made of nitrogen. The bubbles can plug many small blood vessels in
different tissues.

Solution to Question 12:

Nitrogen narcosis is due to increased solubility of nitrogen in the nerve cell.

Concept:

h

: : '
* Below the sea level, atmospheric pressure increases at the rate of 1 atm for ew 33 feet depth.

* According to Boyle’s law, as pressure increases, volume decreases. Henegphings tend to collapse.
To prevent this and to keep lung expanded, gas is delivered at high pressire to the lungs.

* According to Dalton’s law, nitrogen contributes thie maximum to,barometric pressure and hence

the amount of nitrogen entering alveoli i incregsee c i cy

* Henry’s law: As the partial pressure of hitrogen in alveoli i iricreases, its,¢oncentration i in blood
also increases (dissolved form). Nitrogen is’5 times more soluble ince compared to water.

Ks
* As a result, nitrogen dissolves } in, the fatty substances i in neurénal membranes, and because of its
physical effect on altering i iomi¢ “eonductance through the membranes, it reduces neuronal
excitability and produces thé narcotic ffeet: a";

ty ie!
Solution to Question 13: ..*. ~ a“
Poms had a oi

A shift of Oxygen Haethoglobin pigsociation Curve (OHDC) to the right is seen in acclimatization.

On ascension-to higher altitudés over a period of days rather than a period of hours, the ability to
breathe niuch more deeply‘and withstand lower atmospheric 02 concentrations is improved. This
phenomenon is called acclimatization.

Variation of pH in Acclimatization:

Immediate exposure to low pO2 stimulates the arterial chemoreceptors. This increases alveolar
ventilation. This immediate increase in pulmonary ventilation upon rising to a high altitude blows
off large quantities of CO2, reducing the pCO2 and increases the pH of the body fluids.

These changes inhibit the brain stem respiratory center and thereby oppose the effect of low PO2

to stimulate respiration through the peripheral arterial chemoreceptors in the carotid and aortic
bodies.

There is a reduction of bicarbonate ion concentration in the cerebrospinal fluid, as well as in the
brain tissues. This reduction, in turn, decreases the pH in the fluids surrounding the
chemosensitive neurons of the respiratory center, thus increasing the respiratory stimulatory
activity of the center. An important mechanism for the gradual decrease in bicarbonate
concentration is compensation by the kidneys for the respiratory alkalosis. Gradually, the
metabolic compensation of the kidney will shift the pH towards normal.

444 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 445 ========================================
Solution to Question 14:

The symptoms of headache and breathlessness following rapid ascent indicate acute
mountain/altitude sickness and IV digoxin is not used to treat this condition.

On ascent to high altitudes, the low partial pressure of oxygen leads to hypoxia and systemic
arteriolar dilatation. Consequently, the capillary pressure rises with increased transudation of
fluid into the brain tissue and the following symptoms are seen:

formation of cerebrospinal fluid, thereby reducing intracranial tension.

Portable hyperbaric chambers‘atetincreasingly-being used in the treatment of high-altitude
illnesses when rapid descent isnot possiblé. These chambers are available in armed forces

hospitals in high altitude aréas ‘a a o.

Question 19:

Solution to Question 4:
The rate of inspiration is controlled by the pneumotaxic center.
Pneumotaxic centre:
Location: Upper pons (dorsally in nucleus parabrachialis)
Function:

¢ Transmits signals to the inspiratory area, i.e., it inhibits the apneustic center and thus the dorsal
respiratory group of neurons, to stop inspiration.

* It thus helps limit duration of inspiration and switching between inspiration and expiration.

* This has a secondary effect of increasing the rate of breathing because limiting the duration of
inspiration also shortens the duration of expiration, and hence, the entire period of respiration.

A note on inspiratory a signal: _ a

respiratory centers control depth and rate of respiration.

Solution to Question 8:
Transection of the brain stem at the mid-pontine level with bilateral vagotomy causes apneusis.

At mid pontine level lesion, the apneustic centre is intact while the pneumotaxic centre is
separated. Concurrent removal of vagus inputs causes this kind of abnormal pattern of breathing
characterized by deep, gasping inspiration with a pause at full inspiration followed by a brief
insufficient release.

Solution to Question 9:

Peripheral chemoreceptors respond minimally to changes in PO2 under normoxic conditions.

Because of the enormous blood flow per unit of tissue to the carotid bodies, the receptors are not
stimulated in conditions such as anemia or carbon monoxide poisoning, in which the amount of

459 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 460 ========================================
dissolved O2 in the blood reaching the receptors is generally normal, even though the combined
O2 in the blood is markedly decreased.

Peripheral chemoreceptors are responsive to changes in arterial 02, CO2, and H+ ions.

They are excited by a decrease in arterial O2. However, a graph plotted between the rate of firing
of afferent neurons in the chemoreceptor vs. arterial pO2 shows they are most sensitive to
changes in pO2 of less than 60mmHg. Thus, ventilation is driven by pCO2 and H+ under
normoxic conditions.

Other options :

* Option B: Peripheral chemoreceptors respond to changes in blood pH. They are excited by an

increase in H+ ions (i.e decrease in pH) 4

* Option C: The response of peripheral chemoreceptors to changes in Pco2 is faster than that of
central chemoreceptors. Increase in arterial pCO2 stimulates respiration. However, the

stimulation by way of peripheral chemoreceptors occurs+-times as rapidly as that from central
chemoreceptors. This helps increase the rapididty of'response to CO2°at the onset of exercise.

* Option D: Central chemoreceptors are more,iriiportant thant thie’ peripherall Chemoreceptors i in
the ventilatory response to increased CO2=% "s ~ —. ms

normal in case of cyanide poisoning the sensors in carotid and aortic bodies are not able to sense
oxygen in blood because of blockade of cellular respiration and hence, they go in for hypoxic state
and gets stimulated.

* Option b: Increased pCO2 mainly stimulates central chemoreceptors.

* Option c: The peripheral chemoreceptors have an enormous blood flow per unit tissue
(2000mL/100g of tissue/min) because of which the O2 needs of the cells can be met by dissolved

Solution to Question 14:

Central chemoreceptors are most sensitive to increased Pco2 in the blood.

Central chemoreceptors are most sensitive to increased Pco2 in the blood because CO2 moves into
the CSF, where it is promptly hydrated to form H2CO3 that dissociates to increase the CSF H+
concentration. This stimulates the central chemoreceptors, which monitor CSF H+ concentration
(not blood H+ concentration).

Solution to Question 15:

H2CO3 that dissociates so that local H+ concentration rises. The H+ concentration-in, the CSF
parallels the arterial Pco2. So the effect of arterial Pco2 and CO2 in CSF is indixéet ‘Or secondary.

Solution to Question 17:

Hypercapnia is a stimulus for central and peripheral chemoreceptors and not hypocapnia.

Option b: The peripheral chemoreceptors are stimulated by hypoxia (hypoxic, stagnant and
histotoxic but NOT anemic hypoxia)and exercise (increased K+ levels).

receptors per unit time is decreased. This stimulates the peripheral chemoreceptors.

Solution to Question 18:

The most important stimulus comes from the pH of the CSF on the central chemoreceptors. The
CO2 that enters the brain and CSF is hydrated to H2CO3 and dissociates, leading to rising H+
levels. The CSF H+ concentration hence parallels the arterial CO2 levels.

The effect of pO2 on peripheral chemoreceptors under normoxic conditions is very small. Changes
in pCO2 do affect the peripheral chemoreceptors, but the magnitude is less than that for the
central chemoreceptors.

The effect of changes in the pH on peripheral chemoreceptors under normal conditions is small:
and the changes in pO2 do not affect the central chemoreceptors.

Solution to Question 20:

The normal level of ventilation is controlled by the ventilatory response to CO2.

Option b: There is linear relationship between respiratory minute volume and the alveolar Pco2,
i.e. as alveolar Pco2 increases respiratory minute volume increases.

chemoreceptors.

Solution to Question 21:

Hering-Breuer reflexes are mediated by slowly adapting pulmonary stretch receptors and the
impulses travel in the vagus nerve via large myelinated fibers.

Hering-Breuer inflation reflex: "Prolonged inspiration inhibits further inspiration"

* Hyperinflation of lungs stimulates slowly adapting pulmonary stretch receptors and the impulses
travel in the vagus nerve via large myelinated fibers.

¢ This signal inhibits the inspiratory ramp signals of the dorsal respiratory group of neurons
(DRG), thus, inspiration is quickly inhibited and expiration follows.

« Advantages of Hering-Breuer inflation reflex: It cuts down the inspiration time, and therefore,

* It prevents overinflation of the lungs. he a

¢ Lungs operate at lower lung volumes, thereby reo = of epamey.

* The Hering-Breuer reflex is activated when the lung ‘volume i increases to more than three times
the normal tidal volume (tidal volume = 0.5 liters/breath; So, lung volume St; 1.5 liters per breath
activates the reflex). co i a

* It signifies that the reflex plays a protective role preventing the overififiation of the lung rather

than regulating the normal ventilation:\ ‘ ch =

Option d: Abolishing the reflex by cutting the vagal nerves in experimental animals causes slow
deep breathing.

Solution to Question 23:

Head’s paradoxical reflex is stimulated by hyperinflation of lungs. It refers to prolonged
inspiration stimulating further inspiration. Hyperinflation of the lungs causes strong and
long-lasting inspiratory effort (an effect opposite to the Hering-Breuer inflation reflex).

Physiological role:

* Occasional deep breaths which are thought to counteract any tendency to atelectasis in quiet
breathing and possibly in reinforcing the gasps or inspiratory efforts.

¢ First cry after birth.

* The possible explanation for this could be the inhibition of vagus nerve at cold temperatures
(due to the presence of pulmonary fluid in the unaerated newborn lungs).

¢ Lack of inhibitory impulses from the pulmonary stretch receptors and vagus has an effect
opposite to Hering-Breuer’s inflation reflex.

Solution to Question 25: —.— , os
; " fe
i. F L
Moderate exercise does not reduce-the arterial Po2;.increase the arterial Pco2, or reduce the
arterial pH. ue Fi a

Option A: The brain, upon{transmitting motor impulses to the exercising muscles, is believed to
transmit collateral impulsés into the-brainstem at the same time to excite the respiratory center.
This action is analogous to the stimulation of the vasomotor center of the brain stem during
exercise that causes a simultangous increase in arterial pressure.

“i, a
Option B*fhe Po2 of mixed venous blood does fall, but there are no known chemoreceptors that
are stimulated as a result. ©

Option C:
Two points are important regarding pH during moderate exercise:
* pH = pK+ log ([HCO3-)/ [CO2])

¢ The pH of the blood is dependent only on the ratio of the amount of CO2 to the amount of
HCO3-. The concentrations of both buffer components (HCO3- and CO2) are very large compared
to the amount of H+ added to the blood during moderate exercise. Thus, any change in HCO3-
due to the addition of H+ during moderate exercise is very tiny compared to the amount of HCO3-
already present in the blood. Thus, the pH of blood during moderate exercise remains constant.

¢ When sufficient oxygen is not available during exercise, there is increased accumulation of lactic
acid, which initially leads to an increase in PaCO2. However, this is counteracted by the
stimulation of ventilation and as a result, PaCO2 is decreased. This provides some respiratory
compensation for further lactic acid production and prevents a decline in blood pH. Thus, blood
pH remains constant in moderate exercise.

465 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 466 ========================================
Solution to Question 26:

The exercise-induced hyperventilation during vigorous exercise is produced due to the receptors
present in carotid bodies.

When exercise becomes more vigorous, buffering of the increased amounts of lactic acid that are
produced liberates more CO2, and this further increases ventilation. The additional increase in
ventilation produced by the acidosis depends on the carotid bodies and does not occur if they are
removed.

Note: The abrupt increase at the start of exercise is presumably due to psychi¢ stimuli and
afferent impulses from proprioceptors in muscles, tendons, and joints. rit, \

_y ot aia,

4 Te t
ri % bs ba
Solution to Question 27: al ik 4s,

a 2 = ‘ a
In moderate exercise, the respiratory rateds is fsliticreased atthe beginning ‘dite to the response of

joint proprioceptor stimulation. sa Pt is al
wok re ah”

Respiratory responses to exercises,

—

* During exercise, there i istan inicrease in ventilatory rate, that matches the increase in O2
consumption and CO2 production by the body. The stinntlus for increased ventilation rate is the
activation of joint proprioceptors at the beginning’ ‘of the exercise.

¢ The mean values for arterihPos and Pco2 a not change during exercise. Arterial pH does not
change during moderate ekertise, although’ it may decrease during strenuous exercise because of

lactic acidosis. « he ins

* On the other hand; venous Pco® increases during exercise because the excess CO2 produced by
exercising mus¢le is carried-to-the lungs in venous blood.

° Pulmonary blood flow inereases because cardiac output increases during exercise. As a result,
more pulmonary capillaries are perfused, and more gas exchange occurs. The distribution of V/Q
ratios throughout the lung is more even during exercise than when at rest, and there is a resulting
decrease in the physiologic dead space.

The abrupt increase at the start of exercise is presumably due to

¢ Psychic stimuli

¢ Afferent impulses from proprioceptors in muscles, tendons, and joints.

The more gradual increase in ventilation is presumably due to

* Increase in O2 consumption (Proportional increase)

* Increase in body temperature

* Increases in the plasma K+ level (Stimulate the peripheral chemoreceptors).

* Sensitivity of the neurons controlling the response to CO2 is increased or that the respiratory
fluctuations in arterial PCO2 increase so that, even though the mean arterial PCO2 does not rise,
it is CO2 that is responsible for the increase in ventilation.

466 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 467 ========================================
Solution to Question 28:

Respiratory rhythm generation is a function of dorsal respiratory group of neurons (and not
pneumotaxic center).

Pneumotaxic center:

* It is located dorsally in the nucleus parabrachialis of the upper pons and it transmits signals to
the inspiratory area.

¢ Primary effect of this center is to control the switchoff point of the inspiratory TAEBD, thus
controlling the duration of the filing phase of the lung cycle. he,

ry

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

The windkessel effect is seen in large elastic vessels.

The aorta, the pulmonary artery, and their major branches have a large amount of elastin in their
walls. Hence, they have a high elastic recoil effect. This property of elastic recoil is called
windkessel effect (windkessel is a German word for the elastic reservoir).

This recoil of the vessels that have been stretched during systole helps to propel the blood forward
during diastole thus maintaining a continuous flow throughout the cardiac cycle.

Solution to Question 2:

The greatest total cross-sectional area in the circulatory system is of the capillaries.

The total cross-sectional area is minimum for the aorta.

Solution to Question 6: no =

The largest compliatice i in the cifetilatory system is of veins.

Vascular edinpliance/capadifance i is the total quantity of blood that can be stored in a given
portion of the circulation for each mmHg pressure rise.

Vascular Compliance = distensibility X volume.

The compliance of a systemic vein is about 24 times that of its corresponding artery because it is
about 8 times as distensible and has a volume about 3 times as artery (8 x 3 = 24).

Solution to Question 7:

The Bernoulli's theorem states that in a tube or blood vessel, the sum of the kinetic energy of flow
and pressure energy is constant.
Other options:

* Option B: Travelling wave theory (Theories of hearing): Low tones produce maximum
stimulation at the apex of the cochlea.

475 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 476 ========================================
* Option C: Weber—Fechner law: The magnitude of sensation felt is proportional to the log of the

intensity of the stimulus.

* Option D: Frank—Starling law of the heart: Force of contraction is proportional to stretch of the

cardiac muscle.

Solution to Question 12: rt
he *,

The factor favoring filtration’af*the arterial end of the capillary bed is the increase in hydrostatic
pressure ofthe capillary»)

The rate of filtration at any point along the capillary bed depends on the balance of the Starling
forces, which are responsible for fluid movement.

Net filtration pressure = (Pc-Pi)-(1c- mi)
where,

Pc=capillary hydrostatic pressure
Pi=interstitial hydrostatic pressure
mc=capillary colloidal osmotic pressure
ni=interstitial colloidal osmotic pressure
Glomerular filtration rate = k x NFP
where,

k= capillary filtration coefficient

NFP= Net filtration pressure

Solution to Question 13:

The factor tend to increase lymph flow is increase in capillary hydrostatic pressure.

Since the lymph is derived from interstitial fluid, any factor that increases interstitial fluid
pressure also increases lymph flow provided, the lymph vessels are functioning normally. The
factors which increase interstitial fluid pressure include:

* Increased capillary hydrostatic pressure

* Increased interstitial fluid colloid osmotic pressure =
* Increased permeability of the capillaries a?

* Decreased plasma colloid osmotic pressure “ rs) t on

All of the above factors increase the filtration at the cagalary- -interstitium interface and therefore

increase the interstitial fluid volume and pressuré,_ cy .

Solution to Question 15>."

The coronary blood flow in systole has a greater decrease in subendocardium than epicardium.

The myocardial compression in systole compresses the subendocardial vessels to a greater extent,
and the force is sufficiently dissipated in the epicardium to permit some flow in this region
throughout the cardiac cycle.

Solution to Question 18:

Inferior vena cava has the highest oxygen concentration in fetal circulation.

The blood gets oxygenated in the placenta and this passes to the umbilical vein. From the
umbilical vein, 50% of the blood enters the hepatic circulation and 50% bypasses the liver to enter
the inferior vena cava. From here, the blood enters the heart via the right atrium. The right atrium
also receives deoxygenated blood from the superior vena cava. This mixed blood is distributed to
the body via aorta. ;

Therefore, inferior vena cava has the highest concentration of oxygen in the fetal circulation.

“~ Detailed Explanations
