Solution to Question 19:

Free water clearance is regulated by ADH.

Free water clearance is defined as the amount of solute-free water generated by the kidneys, either
when dilute urine or when concentrated urine is formed.

Free-water clearance is positive, when excess water is being excreted (dilute urine).

672 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 673 ========================================
Free-water clearance is negative, when excess solutes are being removed and water is being
conserved (concentrated urine).

Solution to Question 20:

Patient A shows positive free water clearance B is isotonic C shows negative free water clearance.

Free water clearance is defined as the amount of solute-free water generated by the kidneys, either
when dilute urine or when concentrated urine is formed.

Patient A has positive free-water clearance, since excess water is being excreted ( (dilute urine).

Patient C has negative free-water clearance, since excess solutes are being removed and water is

being conserved (concentrated urine). icf

Solution to Question 22 ts

Solution to Question’23: ts
ray, Maes

The requitemerts for exeneting concentrated urine are high ADH levels and hyperosmolar renal
medulla.

A high level of ADH, which increases the permeability of the distal tubules and collecting ducts to
water, thereby allowing these tubular segments to avidly reabsorb water, and a high osmolarity of
the renal medullary interstitial fluid, which provides the osmotic gradient necessary for water
reabsorption to occur in the presence of high levels of ADH.

Solution to Question 24:

Maximum osmotic gradient is found in the inner medulla. The gradient of osmolarity from the
cortex (300 mosm/L) to the inner medulla (1200 mosm/L) is produced by the loops of Henle as
countercurrent multipliers and maintained by operation of the vasa recta as countercurrent
exchangers.

673 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 674 ========================================
Solution to Question 25:

Hyperosmolarity of the medullary interstitium is mainly due to NaCl and urea.

The major factors that contribute to the buildup of solute concentration into the renal medulla
are

* Active transport of Na+ and co-transport of K+, Cl-, and other ions out of the thick portion of the
ascending limb of the loop of Henle into the medullary interstitium.

¢ Active transport of ions from the collecting ducts into the medullary interstitium.

¢ Facilitated diffusion of urea from the inner medullary collecting ducts into the medullary
interstitium. =

* Diffusion of only small amounts of water from the medullary tubules into the médullary
interstitium :

Solution to Question 26:

Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) does not play a role in countercurrent mechanism.
The countercurrent mechanism consists of 2 components:
* Countercurrent multiplier:

a. Absorption of water via Aquaporin 1 in thin descending limb

b. Active transport of Na+- K+- 2Cl- in thick ascending limb

c. Flow of fluid from PCT to DCT

* Countercurrent exchanger: Vasa recta are involved in this process. They help in maintaining the
osmotic gradient in the renal medulla. This is a passive process.

Solution to éétion 28: o
Qu 4
The eye does not have a countercurrent blood flow system.

Heat and substances, including gases, steroids and peptide hormones, can pass from venous
blood, interstitial fluid and lymph to the arterial blood; the process is called local counter-current
transfer.

Examples of countercurrent blood flow

¢ Heat exchange between the arteries and venae comitantes of the limbs.
¢ Vasa recta in the kidneys

* Testes

¢ Small intestine

Solution to Question 29:

675 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 676 ========================================
Thin ascending limb is not a component of the countercurrent mechanism.

The loop of Henle function as countercurrent multipliers and vasa recta as countercurrent
exchangers.

The functioning of the loop of Henle depends upon:

¢ High permeability of the thin descending limb to water ( via aquaporin-1)

* Active transport of Na+ and Cl- from the thick ascending limb

¢ Inflow of tubular fluid from proximal tubule and outflow to the distal tubule.

Note: The thin ascending limbs of juxtamedullary nephrons are permeable to Na+ and Cl- and
hence do contribute passively to countercurrent multiplication. However, among the options
provided, Option C is the best answer as the other options are directly responsible’ for the
countercurrent mechanism. ee

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1: Sie,
Principal site of acidification of urine is collecting duct. Acidification o. oceuts: in both distal tubule
and collecting duct, but collecting duct is more important.

The urinary pH of 4.5 (limiting pH) is normally, xéached i in the ilecting ducts.

In the late distal tubules and collecting ducts the tubular faitticlium seorétes H+ by primary
active transport. Primary active secretion-of H+ occursiin type A inter¢alated cells of the late distal
tubule and in the collecting tubules We concentration! can be inengased as much as 900-fold in
the collecting tubules.

Solution to Question 2: +, 2 if
The most important extr éelltilar buffer is bicarbonate. The source of bicarbonate is plasma by
filteration. It is a nonnducible buffer
Proteins: Are the: indst abundantintracellular buffer.

Phosphate Sanitior buffer Kidney. The source is plasma by filteration. It is a noninducible
buffer.

Ammonia is a minor buffer, locally produced by tubular cells. Its production increases in
metabolic acidosis. It is the only inducible buffer.

Solution to Question 3:

Normal anion gap is mostly due to unmeasured proteins.

Normally in the plasma, the concentrations of anions and cations are equal to maintain electrical
neutrality. Thus no real "anion gap" exists in the plasma and this is purely a diagnostic concept.

Diagnostically, when the electrolytes are measured in the blood, routinely only certain cations and
anions are measured. These are Na+, Cl- and HCO3-. The anion gap is the difference between the
unmeasured anions and unmeasured cations. This is estimated as :

Plasma anion gap= [Na+] - [Cl-] - [HCO3-]

685 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 686 ========================================
The unmeasured cations include calcium, magnesium and potassium.
The unmeasured anions include albumin, phosphate, sulphate and organic anions.

This is primarily used in measurement of different causes of metabolic acidosis.

Solution to Question 4:

The normal anion gap ranges between 8 and 16 mEq/L.

The concentrations of anions and cations in plasma must be equal to maintain electrical
neutrality. Therefore, there is no real “anion gap” in the plasma. 1

However, only certain cations and anions are routinely measured in the clini Gl Yaboratory. The
cation normally measured is Na+, and the anions are usually.Cl- and HEGS-.

=
The “anion gap” (which is only a diagnostic conceptyimy e differenge ‘pétween unmeasured anions
and unmeasured cations and is estimated as oh Ri ‘ ,

I

Plasma anion gap = [Na+ ] — [HCO3- J foersa- -24- 108-512 mEd/L , Pata

The anion gap will increase if unmea: age othions rigesori if unmeasuped cations fall.

The most important unmeasureghcations includesatcium, maggigetim and potassium and the
major unmeasured anions arealbumin, phosphate, sulfategue other organic anions.

Usually the unmeasured anens exceed inmensun ion and the anion gap ranges between
8 and 16 mEq/L. oO

Solution to Question 6:

PGE2 is the major prostanoid synthesized in the kidney.

It is secreted by:

* RMICs (Renal Medullary Interstitial Cells)

* Macula densa

* Cells in the collecting ducts.

Prostacyclin (PGI2) and other prostaglandins are secreted by the arterioles and gldmeruli

Renal medullary interstitial cells (RMICs) are specialized fibroblast-like cells Which are major site

Solution to Question 8:

Angiotensin II is one of the most potent vasoconstrictors in the body.

It constricts efferent arteriole, thereby increasing filteration fraction and glomerular filteration
rate, when there is decreased renal bloodflow

Solution to Question 9:
Vasopressin (ADH) secretion increases in response to decreased ECF volume to bring about water
retention to restore the ECF volume.

ADH exerts its antidiuretic effect by acting on the V2 receptors and involves the insertion of
aquaporin 2 (AQP2) into the apical (luminal) membranes of the principal cells of the collecting
ducts.

======================================== PAGE 688 ========================================
Solution to Question 10:

The renin-angiotensin system plays the most important role for sodium and water retention in
this patient as the patient has hypovolemia due to blood loss. (refer to figure)

The plasma concentration of renin is the rate-limiting step in the renin-angiotensin system; it is
the major determinant of plasma angiotensin II concentration.

Angiotensin II stimulates the aldosterone and ADH secretion; it also constricts the blood vessels
and causes thirst, which helps in maintaining blood pressure.

Solution to Question 113

ANP acts on the collecting duct.

ANP is secreted by the atria in response to an increase in ECF volume. It decreases Na+
reabsorption in the late distal tubule and collecting ducts. It causes natriuresis.

ANP causes dilation of afferent arterioles and constriction of efferent arterioles resulting in an
increased RBF(Because the dilatory effect of ANP on afferent arterioles is more than the
constrictor effect on efferent arterioles, there is an overall decrease in renal vascular resistance
and resulting

increase in RBF) and increased GFR.

Solution to Question 12:

It is T in ECF volume and not hypovolemia that causes increased secretion of ANP.

+ Tisotonic saline administration

« TECF volume

Stimuli that decrease ANP secretion

* Rising from supine to standing position (as this decreases the central venous pressure).

Solution to Question 14: Nt
The micturition center is located in the pons. The paracentral lobule is also associated with
micturition.

The micturition reflex is an autonomic spinal cord reflex, but it can be inhibited or facilitated by
centers in the brain.

These centers include-
¢ A strong facilitative and inhibitory centers in the brain stem, located mainly in the pons.

* Centers located in the cerebral cortex that are mainly inhibitory but can become excitatory.

Solution to Question 17: *

The antidiuretic hormone acts through aquaporin channel 2.

ADH binds to V2 receptors in the late distal tubules, collecting tubules, and collecting ducts, and
stimulates the movement of an intracellular protein, called aquaporin-2 (AQP-2), to the luminal
side of the cell membranes.

Solution to Question 18:

Urine flowing from the collecting ducts into the renal calyces stretches the calyces and increases
their inherent pacemaker activity, which in turn initiates peristaltic contractions occurring 1 to 5
times per minute that spread to the renal pelvis and then downward along the length of the
ureter, thereby forcing urine from the renal pelvis toward the bladder.

690 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 691 ========================================
* The walls of the ureters contain smooth muscle and are innervated by both sympathetic and
parasympathetic nerves, as well as by an intramural plexus of neurons and nerve fibers that
extends along the entire length of the ureters.

¢ As with other visceral smooth muscle, peristaltic contractions in the ureter are
¢ Enhanced by parasympathetic stimulation

* Inhibited by sympathetic stimulation

* Micturition Reflex +

* Micturition is fundamentally a spinal reflex faciitared and inhibited by higher brain centers and

¢ The micturition reflex is ‘the basic cause "if micturition; “put the higher centers normally exert
final control of micturition. i ,

“i, io
* Micturition Reflex is the resylt'ef a stretch reflex initiated by sensory stretch receptors in the
bladder wall, especially bene receptors ‘ithe posterior urethra when this area begins to fill with
urine at the higher bladde pressures.

* Sensory signals.from ‘the bladdérstretch receptors are conducted to the sacral segments of the
cord through-the pelvic neryes‘and then reflexively back again to the bladder through the
parasympathetic nerve fibers by way of these same nerves. (refer figure)

¢ As the bladder continues to fill, the micturition reflexes become more frequent and cause greater
contractions of the detrusor muscle.

* Once a micturition reflex begins, it is “self-regenerative.”
* It is a single complete cycle of

* The progressive and rapid increase of pressure,

¢ A period of sustained pressure, and

* Return of the pressure to the basal tone of the bladder.

¢ As the bladder becomes more and more filled, micturition reflexes occur more and more often
and more and more powerfully.

* Once the micturition reflex becomes powerful enough, it causes another reflex, which passes
through the pudendal nerves to the external sphincter to inhibit it. If this inhibition is more
potent in the brain than the voluntary constrictor signals to the external sphincter, urination will
occur. If not, urination will not occur until the bladder fills still further and the micturition reflex

Solution to Question 20:
The first urge to void is felt at a bladder volume of about 150 mL, and a marked sense of fullness
at about 400 mL.
Cystometrogram (refer figure)

A plot of intravesical pressure against the volume of fluid in the bladder is called a
cystometrogram.

The curve shows
¢ An initial slight rise in pressure when the first increments in the volume are produced(Ia);

« A long, nearly flat segment as further increments are produced(Ib);

692 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 693 ========================================
« A sudden, sharp rise in pressure as the micturition reflex is triggered(II).
* The flatness of segment Ib is a manifestation of the law of Laplace.

* This law states that the pressure in a spherical viscus is equal to twice the wall tension divided by
the radius.

¢ P= 2T/R

* In the case of the bladder, the tension increases as the organ fill but so does the radius.
Therefore, the pressure increase is slight until the organ is relatively full.

Cystometrogram

Intravesical pressure (cm water)

0 100 200 300 400 S500

Volume of fluid in the bladder (mL)

Solution to Question, 21: an

ms

This patient Has respiratory-deidosis with compensatory metabolic alkalosis.
pH is decreased (&lt:7.35) $0 it is acidosis.

The CO2 levels are increased (&gt;45mmHg), which is in line with the change in pH (in
respiratory acidosis CO2 is expected to raise), thus the primary problem is respiratory.

The HCO3- levels are increased (&gt;30mmol/L), which is opposite to change in pH (i.e there is
acidosis and HCO3- levels are high), thus, there is compensatory metabolic alkalosis.

Thus, this is a case of respiratory acidosis with compensatory metabolic alkalosis.

Solution to Question 22:

The acid-base disorder here is a mixed disorder of metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis.

It should not be confused with metabolic acidosis with uncompensated respiratory alkalosis. This
can be understood by the following calculation:

Step 1: The pH in this patient is 7.1 (pH &lt;7.35) which indicates acidosis

693 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 694 ========================================
Step 2: HCO3- is 12 mmol/L (Normal HCO3- - 22-30 mmol/L). In metabolic acidosis,
HCO3- levels decrease.

Hence, this is a case of metabolic acidosis.

Step 3: As per Winter's formula, the expected respiratory compensation in metabolic acidosis can
be calculated using,

pCO2 =( 1.5 x [HCO3-] ) +8 + 2
In this case, Expected pCO2 = (1.5 x [12] ) +8 + 2
=261+2
= 24 - 28 mmHg _

The pCO2 in this patient is 18 mmHg, which implies there is an overcompensaign of pcO2, This
overcompensation can happen only if there is an additiongigomponent ofgespiratory alkalosis

contributing to reduced pCO2. ‘ ae) a, i
Hence, this patient has a mixed disorder - metaboli¢’ ndidosis and. aWepiratory alkalosis.

Solution to Question 25:
Diarrhoea is a cause of normal anion gap metabolic acidosis produced as a result of increased
HCO3- loss in feces.

This is because a decrease in HCO3- is replaced by an increase in the concentration of Cl-. This is
a measurable anion and hence the anion gap is normal.

Extra - edge : Siggard-Anderson curve normogram is used to plot the acid-base composition of the
blood

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1: KE fl :
Growth hormone is secreted from acid he cells of dnterior pituitary:
Acidophilic cells secrete growth hormone and prolactin. i >

Solution to Question 2: it
Me |
ACTH is more responsible than MSEfor the hyperpigmentation seen in a patient with Cushing
syndrome due to.pituitary cause\(ACTH hypersecretion).

ACTH isa a detivative of PQME (Pro- opiomelanocortin). POMC is the precursor of ACTH,
melanocyté stimulating hérmone (MSH), 6 lipotropin and B endorphin. Since the
melanocyte-stimulating hormone is the byproduct of ACTH synthesis from POMC, any disease
process which leads to ACTH overproduction will also lead to an increased level of MSH. ACTH
has mild MSH activity, hence elevated levels of ACTH acts on melanotropin-1 receptors causing
generalized hyperpigmentation.

A similar manifestation will also be seen in Addison's disease where the ACTH level is high.

Solution to Question 3:

Inhibitory control by the hypothalamus is exerted mainly on prolactin and growth hormone.
The major hypothalamic releasing hormones are:
¢ Thyrotropin releasing hormone (TRH)

* Corticotropin releasing hormone (CRH)

702 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 703 ========================================
* Growth hormone releasing hormone (GHRH)

* Gonadotropin releasing hormone (GnRH)

The major hypothalamic inhibitory hormones are:
* Prolactin inhibitory hormone or dopamine

* Growth hormone inhibitory hormone (GHIH), also called Somatostatin.

Solution to Question 4:

The secretion of prolactin is increased after the sectioning of the pituitary stalk, = i
Prolactin is unique among the pituitary hormones because:the predominant Sehtral control
mechanism is inhibitory, i.e., dopamine inhibits prolactini. secretion. Hente; sectioning of the
pituitary stalk (Ex: In head trauma or compressive skulh lesions) prevents this inhibition and leads
to increased secretion of prolactin. 4 cy ,
a 2 ! ae

Note: zh ma a

If the pituitary stalk is cut above the pituitary gland but the entire papothalamus i is left intact, the
posterior pituitary hormones (ADH Ramp; Oxytéeitn) Show a transient decrease because they are
initially synthesized in the suprdoptic and paraventricular n nuclei of the hypothalamus and are
then transported down to ithé.tierve endings, to the posterior pituitary gland, requiring several

days to reach the gland. ° Cy i

ha, |

Solution to Question 6:

Continuous GnRH administration suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
Pulsatile GnRH stimulates the pulsatile release of LH and FSH.

Note: Long-acting GnRH agonists are used to suppress gonadotropin levels in children with
precocious puberty and in men with prostate cancer.

Solution to Question 7:

Antilipolysis is mediated by IGF-1.

The actions of growth hormone-mediated via IGF-1 (somatomedin-C) are the indirect effects of
growth hormone and include:

703 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 704 ========================================
¢ Antilipolytic activity

* Insulin-like activity

* Protein synthesis

¢ Epiphyseal growth

The direct effects of GH include:
* Sodium retention

* Decreased insulin sensitivity

* Lipolysis

* Protein synthesis io"

Solution to Question 8: : al © A

Growth hormone (GH) has both dinette arid-indirect effects on bone bath

GH stimulates prechondrocytes to “Gitetentiate ibe chondrocytes°CH strongly stimulates
osteoblasts and chondrocytes-causing increased-deposition,of proteins > bone growth. GH also
increases insulin-like growth factor ( (IGF)Mevels, which stimulate bone growth.

Note: The pygmies of Africa have a congenital inability to synthesize significant amounts of

somatomedin C (IGF-I). Therefore, even thougli:their plasma concentration of growth hormone is
either normal or high, they havé diminished’amounts of IGF-I in the plasma, which accounts for
the short stature. Some other dwarfs Cs the Levi-Lorain dwarf) also have this problem.

Solution to Question 9: a

Growth hormone demonstrates the depicted fluctuations. Growth hormone is secreted in a
pulsatile fashion, with increase during strenuous exercise and during the early period of sleep,
(Non-REM Sleep) as shown in the graph. It is reduced during the later stages of sleep. (REM
sleep)

Other options:
Option A: ACTH levels are high in the early morning and low in the late evening.

Option C: TSH is secreted in pulses, but the fluctuations are less in comparison to other pituitary
hormones because of the low amplitude of the pulses and the relatively long half-life of TSH.

Option D: Prolactin is secreted in a pulsatile fashion, with the highest secretory peak between
4:00 and 6:00 a.m. (REM Sleep)

Solution to Question 10:

704 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 705 ========================================
Growth hormone(GH) level is decreased during deep sleep. Specifically, it is reduced during REM
sleep as the levels of GHRH reduces and levels of cortisol are higher compared to slow wave sleep.

Hypoglycemia, fasting, and exercise cause an increase in GH secretion. GH levels are increased
during non-REM sleep.

GH levels also decline with age and obesity.

Solution to Question 11:

The given clinical scenario is suggestive of acromegaly and is due to excess growth-hormone (GH).

Acromegaly occurs when excessive GH is secreted after puberty (after fusion oflong bone
epiphysis). Features include e

“~
=

* Prognathism (protrusion of jaw), hypertrophy of dG one
* Acral enlargement in hands and feet (due to thigkening of softies) >

* Visceromegaly: Enlargement of most orgaais especially thetongue, thypatd Salivary gland, liver,

spleen, kidney, and prostate al ra

ae

Solution to Question 16:
Iodide trapping (iodide uptake) is not catalyzed by thyroid peroxidase. Oxidation, organification,
and coupling are catalyzed by it.
Steps in synthesis and secretion of thyroid hormone:

* Iodide uptake: The basolateral sodium-iodide symporter (NIS) actively takes up I-(iodide) from
the circulation into the follicular cells. It is then transported into the colloid through a pendrin
(chloride-iodide) transporter

706 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 707 ========================================
* Thyroglobulin synthesis: Thyroglobulin (Tg), is a large glycoprotein rich in tyrosine residues. It is
synthesized by follicular cells.

* Iodination of thyroglobulin: This is regulated by the thyroid peroxidase (TPO) enzyme. TPO has
three functions:

* Oxidation: TPO oxidizes I- to I2 (iodine) in the presence of H202

* Organification: TPO links tyrosine residues of thyroglobulin with iodine (I2) to form
monoiodotyrosine (MIT), diiodotyrosine (DIT)

* Coupling reactions: TPO combines iodinated tyrosine residues to make MIT + DIT = T3
(triiodothyronine), DIT + DIT = T4 (tetraiodothyronine)
¢ Storage: Thyroid hormones are bound to thyroglobulin and stored in the follicyla liimen as

colloids. The colloid has hormone reserves for 2-3 months o*

* Release: Iodinated thyroglobulin is taken up by the thyn id-follicles via-endocytosis and fuses
with lysosomes. It is cleaved by lysosomal proteolytic enzymes into MIT’ DIT, T3, and T4. T4

(80%) and T3 (20%) are released into the blood , hs 7

4 7
+ Peripheral T3 conversion: T4 undergoes deibation in extrathyroidal tissue to form T3, which
is the active form of the hormone 1, af, ih, ‘

i .

Synthesis and Secretion of
Thyroid Hormone

Solution to Question 17:

Introduction of exogenous iodine in the form of lugol's iodine solution leads to transient
inhibition of organification and synthesis of thyroid hormones. This is known as the wolff-chaikoff
effect.

In individuals with a normal thyroid, the gland escapes from this inhibitory effect and iodide
organification resumes; but the suppressive action of high iodide may persist in patients with
hyperthyroidism due to underlying autoimmune thyroid disease.

707 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 708 ========================================
Note: Jod-Basedow effect is iodine-induced hyperthyroidism, usually occurring after the use of an
I-based contrast agent. It is often seen in the presence of pre-existing thyroid conditions like
multinodular goitre.

Solution to Question 18:

Thyroxine is stored in the thyroid follicles for 2-3 months, bound to thyroglobulin.

Serves as a reserve of T3 and T4, thus delaying the occurrence of hypothyroidism, if thyroid
secretion ceases.

Cells of the endocrine pancreas are divided into:

The close interrelations among these cells allow direct control of secretion of some of the

Solution to Question 3:

The given clinical vignette strongly suggests hypoglycemia due to surreptitiousayse of exogenous
insulin, characterized by a low C-peptide level. i

x 7
Insulin abuse is a method for increasing muscle bulk EQ formancatencement in athletes,
although less common than anabolic steroid use Hen insulin does not contain C-peptide.
Therefore blood level of C-peptide will be low in.such patients. On the other hand, endogenous
production of insulin will be characterized byhigh C-peptide level in bloods

Structure of Proinsulin

“K . B chain (30 amino acids)
©MARROW

Insulin is composed of two chains A and B, and a connecting peptide called C peptide. The insulin
and C peptide are packaged in the secretory granules and secreted in equimolar amounts.

Importance of C-peptide:

* C peptide is measured by radioimmunoassay, and its level in the blood provides an index of
beta-cell function in patients receiving exogenous insulin. If they are unable to produce insulin,
they have greatly decreased levels of C peptide.

* C peptide levels can be used to differentiate between an insulinoma (high C-peptide) and
exogenous insulin overdose (low C-peptide) in a patient with hypoglycemia.

719 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 720 ========================================
The half-life of insulin is 4-6 minutes.

Insulin is degraded by the insulinase enzyme in the liver, and to a lesser extent, in the kidneys and
muscles.

Note: Half-life of C-peptide is 20-30 minutes.

Solution to Question 5:

Glucose-mediated insulin release occurs through the closure of ATP sensitive K+ channels.
Therefore the new drug being initiated is most probably a sulfonylurea which acts jaa similar
mechanism. al

ge.

Increased blood glucose concentration is the primary contreller of insulingsecretion. Inside the
beta cells, glucose is converted to glucose-6-phosphat ani later ATP is'génerated. This ATP closes
ATP sensitive potassium channels, leading to a vise dee? and de olarization. This in turn causes
the opening of voltage-gated Ca 2+ channels and. influx of Ca+. “This ultimately leads to the
secretion of insulin via exocytosis. H = _=_ re

MARROW

Diazoxide keeps the ATP sensitive K+ channels open and prevents depolarization, resulting in a
decreased insulin release. It is used in patients with insulinoma.

Sulfonylureas cause closure of the ATP sensitive K+ channels, leading to increased insulin
secretion. It is used in patients with diabetes mellitus.

Solution to Question 6:

Insulin mainly stimulates glucose uptake by skeletal muscles.

Glucose uptake by skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle and adipose tissues is via GLUT-4 which is
insulin-dependent. In the presence of insulin, GLUT-4 receptors translocate to the cell membrane

720 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 721 ========================================
and hence, glucose uptake by the cell occurs.

Most of the other GLUT transporters are not insulin sensitive and are constitutively expressed in
the cell membrane. Ex: GLUT-1 in RBCs, brain and kidneys. (GLUT-3 is also present in the brain
and kidneys)

Note: Insulin promotes glucose uptake in the liver. However, this is done by increasing the activity
of glucokinase and inactivating glycogen phosphorylase. The net effect is the synthesis of glycogen
and decreased breakdown of glycogen.

number of glucose transporters (GLUTs) in the cell membrane, oO
This type of insulin-dependent glucose transport vign CLUI 4is observed i in skeletal muscle,

cardiac muscle, and adipose tissues. ’ ~ ce a

Solution to Question 9:
In patients who require intravenous insulin therapy, a rapid insulin infusion results in
hypokalemia.

Therefore such patients should undergo serum electrolyte level assessment at frequent intervals
while on insulin infusion for prompt diagnosis and early correction of hypokalemia.

Insulin increases the activity of Na-K ATPase in cell membranes so that more K+ is pumped into
cells. Hence, serum K+ falls.

Note: Since insulin decreases serum potassium level, insulin-glucose infusions are used to treat
hyperkalemia.

======================================== PAGE 722 ========================================
Solution to Question 10:

Arginine increases insulin secretion.
Option B: B-adrenergic blockers like propranolol inhibits insulin secretion.

Option C: Epinephrine both stimulates (by beta-adrenergic agonistic action) and inhibits (by
alpha-adrenergic agonistic action) the secretion of insulin. However, the inhibitory effect of
epinephrine is more dominant than the stimulatory effect.

Option D: Insulin promotes glucose uptake by cells, causing reduced serum K+. Low serum K+
does not cause an increase in insulin secretion.

Solution to Question 13:

Insulin secretion is normally stimulated by GLP-1.

Glucagon-like peptide—1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP) are called
incretins and are potent stimulators of insulin secretion. They also inhibit glucagon secretion.
They are released in the gastrointestinal tract after a person eats a meal. They then cause an
“anticipatory” increase in blood insulin in preparation for the glucose and amino acids to be

722 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 723 ========================================
absorbed from the meal.

Note: Drugs that mimic the action of incretins are used in the management of diabetes.

Solution to Question, 16: an

ms

An insulin receptor has 4 pafts. It is a tetramer made up of two ° and two B subunits.

bee .
The ° subunits are extracellular, whereas the B subunits penetrate through the cell membrane
into the cytoplasm. The intracellular portions of the B subunits have tyrosine kinase activity.

Once insulin binds to the alpha subunits, the portions of the beta subunits protruding into the cell
become autophosphorylated. Autophosphorylation of the beta subunits, in turn, activates a local
tyrosine kinase, which causes phosphorylation of multiple intracellular enzymes. The net effect is
to activate some of these enzymes while inactivating others.

Solution to Question 18:

724

Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 725 ========================================
Increased transport of K+ into adipocytes will occur first on giving an injection of insulin.
Based on time on onset, actions of insulin are classified as:

Rapid (seconds): Increased permeability, leading to:

* Increased transport of glucose, amino acids, and K+ into insulin sensitive cells
Intermediate (minutes): Changes in activity levels of intracellular enzymes, leading to:

¢ Stimulation of protein synthesis

* Inhibition of protein degradation

* Activation of glycolytic enzymes and glycogen synthase

* Inhibition of phosphorylase and gluconeogenic enzymes %,

Delayed (hours to days): Changed rates of transcription of DNA, leading tox, ’

* Increase in mRNAs for lipogenic and other enzymes‘) —_
a = a
1 Me ih. 4,
" i i ! a) a
Solution to Question 19: i oa, as te
ra 4

The incorrect pair is thyroxine - ackeintea blood’eficose. Thyroxitte i is a diabetogenic hormone

leading to an increase in blood‘ghicose levels», ie

Effects Of Hormones On Ca®bohydraté:Metabolism: eX

* Catecholamines: Activation of glycogen phosphorylase and therefore glycogenolysis occurs.
Hepatic glucose output is ineyeased, producing,hyperglycemia.

* Glucocorticoids: Glucocoftitoids from the’ adrenal cortex elevate blood glucose by stimulating
gluconeogenesis and inhibiting glucose“utilization by the cells.

* Growth Hormoné"The effects of! growth hormone are partly direct and partly mediated via IGF-I
(Insulin- like Growth Factor-l)=The growth hormone mobilizes FFA from adipose tissue, thus
favoring kétégenesis. It decreases glucose uptake into some tissues (“anti-insulin action’),
increases hepatic glucose output, and may decrease tissue binding of insulin.

Note: Infusing catecholamine (Epinephrine) mainly promotes glycogenolysis and it is also
associated with enhanced rates of aerobic glycolysis in skeletal muscles during exercise (resulting
in adenosine triphosphate production).

Solution to Question 20:

A meal rich in arginine and alanine stimulates both insulin and glucagon secretion. Hence,
hypoglycemia does not occur.

Increased blood amino acids stimulate secretion of glucagon, which in turn causes the amino
acids to get converted to glucose. So, the mechanism by which hypoglycemia is prevented after a
protien rich meal is secretion of glucagon.

725 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 726 ========================================
Solution to Question 21:

A fall is plasma amino acid concentration is least likely to be seen in this case.

Injury to the beta cells of the pancreas impairs insulin production rendering the rat insulin
deficient. Due to insulin deficiency, catabolism of proteins increases and amino acids in plasma
increase.

Insulin deficiency increases blood glucose levels, which lead to glycosuria and osmotic diuresis
with extracellular dehydration. There is increase in the plasma osmolality.

Insulin deficiency causes increased utilization of fats, excess production of ketoacids and
metabolic acidosis. Metabolic acidosis - a rise in the plasma H+ develops.

Solution to Question 22: ae | “4

Insulin inhibits glucose-6-phosphatase. cS GW »

Insulin promotes liver uptake, storage, and: the use of glucdse- After a meal, insulin causes
glucose to be rapidly stored in the liver in the form of f glycogen. This oecurs by:

* Inactivation of liver phosphorylase, Which j is the; principale enzyigy Sthat causes liver glycogen to
split into glucose. " .

hs

¢ Enhanced uptake of gludose-by the liver" eats by i incregaihe the activity of the enzyme
glucokinase which causes phosphorgjgeiin of glucose“after it enters the liver cells. Once
phosphorylated, the glucose is tenyporarily trapped inside the liver cells because phosphorylated
glucose cannot diffuse back through the cell: inembrane.

* Increased activity of enzymes that promote glycogen synthesis, like glycogen synthase.

When insulin levels.drop ( (Ex: Fasting) glucose phosphatase gets activated. This causes phosphate
to get detached from glucose, phosphate, allowing free glucose from the liver cells to re-enter

circulation’, “ Cc
“ah,

Solution to Question 23:

Diazoxide acts by opening ATP sensitive K+ channels and causing a decrease in insulin secretion.

Glucose enters beta cells of the pancreas via GLUT-2 receptors. Once inside, it is phosphorylated
by glucokinase (rate-limiting step) to glucose-6-phosphate which is oxidized to form adenosine
triphosphate (ATP). This ATP causes closure of ATP-sensitive potassium channels and hence
depolarization. This leads to opening voltage-gated calcium channels and Ca2+ influx. This
stimulates fusion of the insulin-containing vesicles with the cell membrane and secretion of
insulin into the extracellular fluid by exocytosis.

Diazoxide keeps the ATP sensitive K+ channels open and prevents depolarization and subsequent
insulin release.

Sulfonylureas cause closure of the ATP sensitive K+ channels, leading to increased insulin
secretion.

Solution to Question 3:

60% of aldosterone is bound to plasma proteins, not 80%.

90% of cortisol is bound to plasma proteins. Most of it is bound to transcortin, a
corticosteroid-specific ° 2 globulin, and the rest to albumin. This high degree of binding to plasma
proteins slows the elimination of cortisol. Therefore, cortisol has a longer half-life of 60 to 90
minutes.

Since only about 60% of aldosterone is in bound form, it has a relatively short half-life of about 20

minutes.

Solution to Question 6: -_'_) + i
e 7 = 9
Inhibition of phospholipase A2 is ananti- inflamniafory effect of steroids, thus being the rationale
for starting steroids in this patient. he
as a |

Patients with severe COVIDiare at high risk ‘of developing a systemic inflammatory response.
Steroids help in mitigating these deleterious effects.

Phospholipase A2:isja precursorjof arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid is the precursor for
prostaglandins\¢ and leukotrienés, “which are involved in inflammation. Steroids induce the
synthesis‘ofilipocortin, which inhibits phospholipase A2.

Other anti-inflammatory and i immunosuppressive effects of steroids are:
* Inhibit the production of interleukin-2 (IL-2) and proliferation of T lymphocytes

¢ Inhibit the release of histamine and serotonin from mast cells and platelets

Solution to Question 7:

Inhibition of inflammatory response requires high levels of glucocorticoids.

Anti-inflammatory and anti-allergic responses (inhibition of histamine from mast cells and
basophils) are produced only at high levels of glucocorticoids. These effects cannot be produced by
normal circulating glucocorticoids in the body and thus require exogenous supplementation of
steroids to produce them.

======================================== PAGE 738 ========================================
Solution to Question 8:

ACTH level is higher during the early morning and low during late evening. 75% of the ACTH
production occurs between 4:00 AM to 10:00 AM.

Note: Cortisol levels are also high in the early morning and low in the late evening.

Solution to Question 9:

Glucocorticoids inhibit ACTH secretion by a direct negative feedback mechanism. They also
inhibit CRH secretion. 1

Solution to Question 12: ‘
Ch tg, Tier

*h en
Aldosterone increases transport of ENaCs (epithelial sodium channels) from the cytoplasm to the
cell membrane. This is a rapid effect of aldosterone.

Mechanism of action of aldosterone:

¢ Aldosterone diffuses into the renal tubular epithelial cells and combines with a mineralocorticoid
receptor (MR) in the cytoplasm.

* The aldosterone-receptor complex diffuses into the nucleus and induces transcription.

* The mRNA diffuses back into the cytoplasm and undergoes translation to form proteins. These
have two effects - Rapid and Slow.

* The slower effect is increase in synthesis of ENaCs.
¢ The important products formed are:
¢ Na+K+ATPase

* membrane transport proteins like epithelial sodium channel

Solution to Question 14:

In a patient with hypoaldosteronism, alkalosis is least likely to be found. Hypoaldosteronism is
rather responsible for causing metabolic acidosis.

Patients with hypoaldosteronism have:

¢ Hyponatremia- due to increased Na+ loss in urine

¢ Hyperkalemia- due to K+ retention

¢ Hypotension- due to reduced ECF volume o 2

¢ Mild metabolic acidosis because hydrogen ions are not tgaweted i in the jumine

@MARROW

Solution to Question 17:

Insulin does not increase during stress.

In response to stress, the usual physiological functions like gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, and
inhibition of insulin actions occurs, resulting in increased blood levels of fuel sources (glucose,

fatty acids) needed to cope.

742 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 743 ========================================
Hormones that are increased in the blood by stress are CRH, ACTH, cortisol, B-endorphin(to
reduce pain), vasopressin, aldosterone, growth hormone, and glucagon.

Solution to Question 19: _*,»
i hy L

he ts
Hirsutism and masculinization in a female suggest excessive androgens like
dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA).

Adrenogenital syndrome is due to an adrenocortical tumor secreting excessive quantities of
androgens that cause intense masculinizing effects. In boys, it presents as precocious puberty. In
women, virile characteristics develop, including:

* Growth of a beard

* Deeper voice

¢ Masculine distribution of hair on the body and the pubis and
* Growth of the clitoris

The excretion of 17-ketosteroids (which are derived from androgens) in the urine maybe 10 to 15
times elevated. This finding can be used in diagnosing the disease.

Solution to Question 3:

Vit D does not decrease phosphate reabsorption by the renal tubules. It increases calcium and
phosphate reabsorption by the renal tubules, leading to a decrease in excretion of these
substances in the urine.

Solution to Question 5: ~~“

‘

The conversion of 25-hydroxycholecalciferol to 1,25 dihydroxycholecalciferol is done by enzyme
1° -hydroxylase which is present in the kidney. In chronic renal failure (CRF), there is a deficiency
of this enzyme and formation of the active form of vitamin D does not take place.

Factors which increase activity of 1° -hydroxylase are:
* Ca2+ deficiency

* Increased parathyroid hormone (PTH)

Solution to Question 6:

The lung is not involved in calcium homeostasis.

There are two sources of cholecalciferol in the body:

753 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 754 ========================================
* Diet
¢ Synthesized in the skin from 7-dehydrocholesterol in the presence of ultraviolet light.

Cholecalciferol is hydroxylated in the liver to form 25-hydroxycholecalciferol, which is further
hydroxylated in the kidney to forn 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol. At higher calcium
concentrations, when PTH is suppressed, the 25-hydroxycholecalciferol is converted to a different
compound—24,25dihydroxycholecalciferol— that has almost no vitamin D effect.

Note: This is a controversial question because new evidence suggests an association between
calcium homeostasis and pulmonary fibroblasts. Therefore:

+ If Lung is given in the options and spleen is provided as one of the choices instead of Kidneys,

Skin, Liver or Intestine, then the best answer would be the spleen. o.1
¢ If other options are Kidneys, Skin, Liver, or Intestine, then the best answer would be Lung as the
role of these organs in calcium metabolism is beyond doubt... ms :

Solution to Question 7:

Increased calcium levels lead to an increase in 24,25 dihydroxycholecalciferol.

The levels of calcium control formation of 1,25-dihydroxyxholecalciferol. When calcium is low,
PTH (parathyroid hormone) promotes the conversion of 25hydroxycholecalciferol to 1,25-
dihydroxycholecalciferolH in the kidneys. At higher calcium concentrations, when PTH is sup-
pressed, the 25hydroxycholecalciferol is converted to a different compound—24,25-
dihydroxycholecalciferol— that has almost no vitamin D effect.

Solution to Question 9:

In the given scenario, increased formation of 24,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol will take place.

Infusion of calcium chloride solution will lead to raised calcium concentration in blood. At higher
calcium concentrations, PTH is also suppressed. So the 25-hydroxycholecalciferol is converted to a
different compound : 24,25dihydroxycholecalciferol— that has almost no vitamin D effect.

When plasma calcium concentration is high, formation of 1,25 dihydroxycholecalciferol is also
greatly depressed. Lack of 1,25 dihydroxycholecalciferol, in turn, decreases the absorption of
calcium from the intestines, bones, and renal tubules, thus causing the calcium ion concentration
to fall back toward its normal level. rit, ~

Option A: Since PTH levels are supressed bone demineralisation does not “take place.

Option B: Calcitonin is a calcium regulatory hormage, whose level inicteases when the calcium

concentration in plasma is high. ~e ce sa

increases or remains the same with high plasma Cate levels.

Solution to Question 11: —"
"hy _ ‘
Ca 2+ absorption is enhanced by proteins.

It is inhibited by phosphates, phytic acid, and oxalates because these anions form insoluble salts
with Ca 2+ in the intestine.

Solution to Question 12:
Ionic calcium is the active form that is important for most functions of calcium in the body,
including the effect of calcium on the heart, the nervous system, and bone formation.

Most of the calcium (99%) is stored in the bones. Only about 0.1 percent of the total body calcium
is in the extracellular fluid and 1 percent is in the cells and its organelles.

From the 0.1% Ca+2 in the plasma, the distribution of calcium is as follows:
p

* About 41 % is combined with the plasma proteins (non-diffusible through the capillary
membrane)

756 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 757 ========================================
* About 9 % is combined with anionic substances of the plasma and interstitial fluids like citrate

and phosphate in such a manner that it is not ionized (diffusible through the capillary membrane);
and

* The remaining 50 % of the calcium in the plasma is both diffusible through the capillary
membrane and ionized.

Solution to Question 14:

Osteoclast cells do not have PTH receptors. PTH stimulates osteoclast activity and bone
resorption by an indirect mechanism.

757 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 758 ========================================
PTH has receptors on the osteoblasts in the bone. When PTH binds to these receptors, osteoblasts
release cytokines like RANK-ligand and M-CSF. These cytokines allow the differentiation of
macrophages to osteoclasts.

Osteoclasts, in turn, cut the collagen in the bone, which is bound to calcium. This causes the
release of calcium, phosphorus, and collagen breakdown products in the blood.

Osteoblasts produce osteoprotegerin (OPG), or osteoclastogenesis inhibitory factor, a cytokine
that inhibits bone resorption by preventing interaction of RANKL with its receptor. PTH
decreases the production of osteoprotegerin (OPG).

Glucocorticoids promote osteoclast activity and increase bone resorption by increasing RANKL
production and decreasing the formation of OPG. On the other hand, the hormone estrogen
stimulates OPG production.

Solution to Question 16:

PTH promotes the reabsorption of calcium from the renal tubules.

Option A: About 41 percent of the plasma calcium is bound to plasma proteins and is therefore
not filtered by the glomerular capillaries. The rest of the calcium is filtered through the glomeruli
into the renal tubules.

Option B: Of the filtered calcium, approximately 90 percent is reabsorbed in the proximal tubules,
loops of Henle, and early distal tubules. However, when calcium levels are low and PTH levels
rise, PTH promotes calcium reabsorption from the later DCT.

Option D: Calcitonin tends to decrease plasma calcium concentration and, in general, has effects
opposite to those of PTH.

758 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 759 ========================================
Solution to Question 17:

PTH does not increase phosphate reabsorption in kidney, rather it causes decreased phosphate
reabsorption in the kidney.

Solution to Question 21:

761 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 762 ========================================
The given clinical vignette is suggestive of chvostek sign, seen in hypocalcemia due to accidental
parathyroid injury during thyroid surgery. The muscle hyperexcitability seen in hypocalcemia is
due to increased permeability of cells to Na+.

During thyroid surgery, parathyroid gland is prone to accidental injury due to proximity with the
thyroid gland. Damage to the parathyroid gland will lead to a low PTH level and low Ca2+ level.
When the ECF concentration of Ca2+ falls below normal, the nervous system becomes
progressively more excitable. Because there is increased membrane permeability to Na+. This
leads to easy initiation of the action potentials.

The peripheral nerve fibres become so excitable that they begin to discharge spontaneously,
initiating impulses that pass to the skeletal muscles to elicit tetanic muscle contraction. Tetany
occurs usually when calcium concentration falls to 6 mg/dL. 1

4 < A

et ais Mh,

4.0 represents the average albumin loval.

levels. hs .,

Solution to Question 23:

Osteoblasts are involved in the initiation and control of osteoclastic activity.

Osteoblasts produce receptor activator for nuclear factor «B ligand (RANKL) and macrophage
colony stimulating factor (M-CSF), which are necessary for formation of mature osteoclasts and
hence osteoclastic activity.

Specific receptors for parathormone are present only on the osteoblasts and not on osteoclasts.

Solution to Question 25:
Parathyroid adenoma is a benign tumor of the parathyroid gland, which causes primary
hyperparathyroidism (not secondary).

Secondary hyperparathyroidism is characterized by high levels of parathyroid hormone, to
compensate for the low levels of serum calcium, due to some other underlying condition.

Causes of secondary hyperparathyroidism, include:

* Chronic kidney disease: The damaged kidneys are unable to produce sufficient amounts of the
active form of vitamin D, 1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol. This further causes hypocalcemia and
triggers PTH secretion.

* Vitamin D deficiency: Causes hypocalcemia, which triggers the increased secretion of PTH.

¢ Lithium: May cause an increased set-point of calcium for parathyroid hormone suppression,
which leads to parathyroid hyperplasia.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

The decrease in sex hormone-binding globulin will have the maximum effect on testosterone.

Around 98% of the circulating testosterone is bound to plasma proteins such as sex
hormone-binding globulin (65%) and albumin. So, the decrease in sex-hormone-binding globulin
(SHBG) or gonadal steroid-binding globulin (GBG) will have the maximum effect on testosterone
levels by increasing the levels of its free form.

Therefore patients with polycystic ovary syndrome will show features of hyperandrogenism (due
to elevated free testosterone). i.

¢ Surround the sperm cells ahd'provide structural support within the epithelium.
* Guide sperm cells towards the lumen es Sthey advance to later stages of spermatogenesis.
* Produce inhibin and Mullerian- inhibiting substance (MIS).

* Produce andyogen- binding pioteins that maintain high androgen levels within the lumen of the
seminiferous tubules and the proximal part of the male reproductive tract.

* Perform phagocytic function by engulfing residual bodies (cytoplasm shed by spermatozoa
during spermiogenesis) and dead sperm cells.

¢ Express aromatase, which converts testosterone to potent estrogen.

Solution to Question 3:

The negative feedback mechanism involving the Sertoli cells causes |FSH.

FSH stimulates inhibin production by the Sertoli cells. This inhibin inhibits FSH production
by directly acting on the anterior pituitary. Thus, inhibin keeps FSH levels within a set point.

Solution to Question 5:

Testosterone production is mainly contributed by Leydig cells.

Testosterone is synthesized from cholesterol in the Leydig cells and also from androstenedione
secreted by the adrenal cortex.

The diagnosis of testosterone deficiency should be made only on the basis of clinical signs and
symptoms consistent with androgen deficiency, and consistently subnormal serum testosterone

h

concentrations at 8 to 10 AM on three occasions. ke

th a
Testosterone has been used to treat primary or secondary hypogonadism in adult men.

The term androgen means any stetoid hormone that Has masculitibing effects. Testosterone,

Solution to Question 7:

(Anti Mullerian Hormone) is secreted by both sertoli cells and granulosa cells.

Sertoli cells produce Anti Mullerian Hormone (Mullerian inhibitory substance) during
development. It induces the regression of the mullerian duct that is programmed to give rise to a
female reproductive tract.

Granulosa cells of the ovary produce AMH during the reproductive years. It limits the formation
of primary follicles by inhibiting excessive follicular recruitment by FSH.

Solution to Question 8:

774 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 775 ========================================
The blood-testis barrier is formed by the tight junctions between adjacent sertoli cells.
This Sertoli-Sertoli cell tight junctions divide the seminiferous epithelium into 2 compartments:
* Basal compartment - contains the spermatogonia and early-stage primary spermatocytes.

¢ Adluminal compartment - contains later-stage primary spermatocytes and all subsequent
stages of sperm cells.

It imparts a selective permeability, allowing few substances such as testosterone to cross but
preventing noxious substances that might damage the developing sperm. It also protects sperms
from antibodies by creating a specialized, immunologically safe microenvironment for developing
sperm.

Solution to Question 9:

Spermiogenesis refers to the formation of spermatozoa from spermatids.

Spermiogenesis is a process by which there is a formation of extremely specialized mobile
spermatozoa from spermatids. This process requires extensive remodeling or packaging of cell
elements. It occurs in the recesses formed by invaginations of the sertoli cell plasma membrane.

Maturation from spermatids to spermatozoa depends on androgen acting on sertoli cells. When
sperm maturation is complete, the cytoplasm of the sertoli cell around sperm retracts and sperms
are released into the lumen to be bathed by the luminal fluid.

The following changes occur during the formation ofspermatozoafrom spermatids :
¢ The nucleus becomes eccentric and decreases in size, and the chromatin becomes condensed.

* The cytoplasmic content is redistributed and discarded.

775 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 776 ========================================
* The acrosome buds from the golgi apparatus.

* The centrioles migrate to the caudal pole and form a long axial filament made of microtubules.
This becomes the axoneme or major portion of the tail.

Spermiation is the release of the sperm from the sertoli cells. During spermiation, most of the
remaining cytoplasm is shed in the form of residual bodies.

Spermatogenesis refers to the formation of spermatozoa from spermatogonia.

Solution to Question 10:

The time required to produce mature spermatozoa from the earliest stage of spermatogonia is 65
to 70 days.

776 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 777 ========================================
Since several developmental stages of spermatogenic cells occur during this time frame, the stages
are collectively known as the spermatogenic cycle.

Solution to Question 11:

All three of them (testosterone, FSH, LH) are required for spermatogenesis.

Pulses of hypothalamic GnRH stimulate the anterior pituitary to secrete FSH and LH, which then
act on the testes

FSH acts on the Sertoli cells to stimulate spermatogenesis and inhibin secretion. LH acts on the
Leydig cells to stimulate testosterone secretion. Testosterone, acting locally ors Sertoli cells, is
essential for maintaining spermatogenesis. ‘

Hormonal factors that stimulate spermatogenesis: % Pia

* Testosterone, secreted by the Léydig cells.of'the testis, is-essential for growth and division of the

specifically promotes early division of the spermatogonia themselves. In its absence, as in
pituitary dwarfs, spermatogenesis is severely deficient or absent, thus causing infertility.

Solution to Question 13:

Negative feedback in spermatogenesis is by inhibin.

FSH stimulates inhibin production by the Sertoli cells. This inhibin inhibits FSH production
by directly acting on the anterior pituitary.

LH stimulates Leydig cells to secrete testosterone, which in turn inhibits LH. Testosterone
inhibits LH secretion by acting directly on the anterior pituitary and by inhibiting the secretion of
gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) from the hypothalamus.

Solution to Question 15:
Sperm is stored in the epididymis, where they undergo a process of maturation before they are
capable of progressive motility and fertilization.
Changes during the sperm maturation in the epididymis:
* Progressive increase in forward motility
* Increase ability to fertilize
* Maturation of acrosome

* Molecular reorganization of plasma membrane: Lipid (stabilization of plasma membrane)
protein (shedding as well as the acquisition of new proteins)

¢ Ability to bind to zona pellucida

¢ Acquisition of receptors for proteins of the zona pellucida

778 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 779 ========================================
* Increase disulfide bond between cysteine residues in sperm nucleoproteins
* Topographic regionalization of glycosidic residues
¢ Accumulation of mannosylated residues on periacrosomal plasma membrane

* Decreased cytoplasm and cell volume

Solution to Question 16:

Capacitation of sperm takes place in uterine tubes.

Once ejaculated into the female genital tract, the spermatozoa move up the uterus’ to the isthmus
of the uterine tubes, where they slow down and undergo final conditioning called capacitation.

This process involves two components: Re, i
Ti }
* Increasing the motility of the spermatozoa : oy
"4 *s,
¢ Facilitating their preparation for the acrosomal teaction. i) put
a ~~ £4
i - “* 4, ,
* i. ot >"
Solution to Question 17: 2, c

Sperm motility is iereased By vaginal, cotttions. g

On coming in contact with the fluidsof the female “genital tract, multiple changes occur that
activate the sperm for the finakprocesses of. fertilization. These collective changes are called
capacitation of the spermatozoa, which normally requires from 1 to 10 hours.

The following changes. are believed tosotcur:

* The uterine and fallopian tube flttids wash away the various inhibitory factors that suppress
sperm activity in the male genital ducts.

* While the spermatozoa remain in the fluid of the male genital ducts, they are continually
exposed to many floating vesicles from the seminiferous tubules containing large amounts of
cholesterol. This cholesterol is continually added to the cellular membrane covering the sperm
acrosome, toughening this membrane and preventing the release of its enzymes.

After ejaculation, the sperm deposited in the vagina swim away from the cholesterol vesicles
upward into the uterine cavity, and they gradually lose much of their other excess cholesterol
during the next few hours. In doing so, the membrane at the head of the sperm (the acrosome)
becomes much weaker.

* The membrane of the sperm also becomes much more permeable to calcium ions, so calcium
now enters the sperm in abundance and changes the activity of the flagellum, giving it a powerful
whiplash motion in contrast to its previously weak undulating motion. In addition, the calcium
ions cause changes in the cellular membrane that cover the leading edge of the acrosome, making
it possible for the acrosome to release its enzymes rapidly and easily as the sperm penetrates the
granulosa cell mass surrounding the ovum, and even more so as it attempts to penetrate the zona
pellucida of the ovum.

779 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 780 ========================================
Solution to Question 18:

Prolonged injection of GnRH will lead to decreased LH levels.

A constant administration of GnRH produces an initial agonistic action known as "flare", followed
by a downregulation of GnRH receptors ( decrease in GnRH receptor concentration). This
desensitizes pituitary to the action of GnRH and a hypogonadotropic-hypogonadal state is
induced.

This is characterized by low LH, low FSH levels and thereby reduced testosterone production (i.e.,

chemical castration). % &
ms ! *,
. . te

Solution to Question 20:,"» lh

"e

_ %
The normal pH of human semerhis 7.4 (isotonic).

The pH in the Toumen of thé @pididymis is relatively acidic (6.5 to 6.8) as the result of H+ secretion
by clear cells that are analogous to intercalated cells in the nephron. Addition of the relatively
alkaline secretions of the seminal vesicles raises the final pH of seminal plasma to between 7.2
and 7.8.

Spermatozoa generally tolerate alkalinity better than acidity.

A pH near neutrality or slightly higher is optimal for the motility and survival of sperm cells in
humans and in other species as well.

Solution to Question 21:

Prostaglandins in semen are secreted by seminal vesicle.
The seminal vesicles secrete fluid that is rich in fructose, citrate, prostaglandins, and fibrinogen.
The prostaglandins present in the seminal fluid may assist in fertilization in two ways:

* Prostaglandins react with cervical mucus to make it more penetrable by sperm.

780 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 781 ========================================
* Prostaglandins induce peristaltic contractions in the female reproductive tract (i.e., the uterus
and fallopian tubes) to propel the sperm up the tract.

Solution to Question 22:

Normal sperm count on an average is 100 million/ml (90-120 million/mL).

Note: The WHO 2010 minimal criteria for semen analysis requires a minimum sperm count
&gt;15 million/mL or &gt;40 million/per ejaculate.

Solution to Question 25:

The ovum remains viable up to 24hours following ovulation.

If not fertilized, the ovum degenerates in the fallopian tube without leaving behind any trace
after 24 hours.

Solution to Question 26:
Insulin has no role in the attainment of puberty. GH, leptin and testosterone are all required for
the onset of puberty.

The onset of puberty is marked by enhanced androgenic activity. The growth spurt of adolescent
males is influenced by a complex interplay between androgens, growth hormone (GH), nutrition,
and genetic factors.

781 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 782 ========================================
Leptin, the satiety-producing hormone secreted by fat cells, may be the link between body weight
and puberty.

Solution to Question 27:

Pubarche or the development of axillary and pubic hair is due to testosterone.

The growth of pubic and axillary hair in both sexes is primarily due to androgens.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

Gonadotropins (FSH, LH) are not secreted by the ovary. They are secreted by the anterior
pituitary.

Ovaries secrete estrogen, progesterone, and inhibin B.

Solution to Question 2:

Ovulation occurs 14 days before menses, regardless of cycle length.

If we calculate the day of menstruation from the beginning of the cycle, in a 28-day cycle,
ovulation occurs on day 14 and in a 35-day cycle, ovulation will occur on day 22.

791 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 792 ========================================
Option B: LH surge leads to ovulation. Ovulation does not occur before LH surge.

Option C: The ruptured ovarian follicle (after ovulation) is converted into corpus luteum.
Ovulation does not occur after corpus luteal maturation.

Option D: Ovulation will occur 14 days before menstruation, i.e. on day 22 of a 35-day cycle. It
does not occur 22 days before menstruation.

Solution to Question 3:

The human corpus luteum starts regressing ~10 days after ovulation. +4

menstrual cycle. ot A

Solution to Question 4:

Estrous cycle is not seen in humans.

Mammals other than primates do not menstruate, and their sexual cycle is called an estrous cycle.
It is named for the conspicuous period of “heat” (estrus) at the time of ovulation, normally the
only time during which the sexual interest of the female is aroused.

792 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 793 ========================================
In spontaneously ovulating species with estrous cycles, such as the rat, no episodic vaginal
bleeding occurs, but the underlying endocrine events are essentially the same as those in the
menstrual cycle.

In other species, ovulation is induced by copulation (reflex ovulation).

Solution to Question 5:

¢ Progesterone is the dominant hormone during the luteal phase.

* The corpus luteum in the non-pregnant woman secretes large quantities of Prope tone anda
small amount of estrogen, as well as inhibin. ba he

* The luteal phase begins after ovulation and, lasts ‘until the cath of the conn luteum.

MARROW

Follicular phase (days 0-14)

¢ A primordial follicle develops to the Graafian stage, with atresia of neighboring follicles.
¢ LH and FSH receptors are up-regulated in theca and granulosa cells.

¢ Estradiol levels increase and cause proliferation of the uterus.

* FSH and LH levels are suppressed by the negative feedback effect of estradiol on the anterior
pituitary.

* Progesterone levels are low.
Ovulation (day 14)

* Occurs 14 days before menses, regardless of cycle length. Thus, in a 28-day cycle, ovulation
occurs on day 14; in a 35-day cycle, ovulation occurs on day 22.

793 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 794 ========================================
¢ A burst of estradiol synthesis at the end of the follicular phase has a positive feedback effect on
the secretion of FSH and LH (LH surge).

* Ovulation occurs as a result of the estrogen-induced LH surge.

* Estrogen levels decrease just after ovulation (but rise again during the luteal phase).

* Cervical mucus increases in quantity; it becomes less viscous and more penetrable by sperm.
Luteal phase (days 14-28)

* The corpus luteum begins to develop, and it synthesizes estrogen and progesterone. Vascularity
and secretory activity of the endometrium increase to prepare for receipt of a fertilized egg.

* Basal body temperature increases because of the effect of progesterone on the hyppthalamic

thermoregulatory center. “5

¢ If fertilization does not occur, the corpus luteum regresses: cat the end of the huteal phase. As a
result, estradiol and progesterone levels decrease abru et

Menses (days 0-4) ht mi #

Solution to Question 6: cai = 0 3
he

Progesterone is mainly responsible for the maintenance of endfaietrial thickness in the
post-ovulatory phase. AO , % i

The menstrual cycle consists of three. phases: the menstrual phase, the proliferative phase, and the

secretory, or progestational, phasé~ % e

Menstrual phase: This phase? is s characterized By the discharge of blood and endometrial debris
from the vagina. ae =

* It coincides with the'ehd of the ovata luteal phase and onset of a new follicular phase.

¢ Withdrawal.of progesterone,on ‘the degeneration of the corpus luteum (CL) leads simultaneously
to sloughing, of the endometrium (menstruation) and development of new follicles in the ovary
under the influence of rising gonadotropic hormone levels

Proliferative Phase: Thus, menstrual flow ceases, and the proliferative phase of the uterine cycle
begins concurrently with the last portion of the ovarian follicular phase.

* When the menstrual flow ceases, a thin endometrial layer less than 1-mm thick remains.
Estrogen stimulates proliferation of epithelial cells, glands, and blood vessels in the endometrium,
increasing this lining to a thickness of 3 to 5 mm.

¢ The estrogen-dominant proliferative phase lasts from the end of menstruation to ovulation. Peak
estrogen levels trigger the luteinizing hormone (LH) surge responsible for ovulation.

Secretory or Progestational Phase: After ovulation, when a new corpus luteum is formed, the
uterus enters the secretory, or progestational, phase, which coincides in time with the ovarian
luteal phase. The corpus luteum secretes large amounts of progesterone and estrogen.
Progesterone converts the thickened, estrogen-primed endometrium to a richly vascularized,
glycogen-filled tissue.

* This period is called either the secretory phase, because the endometrial glands are actively
secreting glycogen into the uterine lumen for early nourishment of a developing embryo before it

795 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 796 ========================================
implants, or the progestational (“before pregnancy”) phase, referring to the development of a lush
endometrial lining capable of supporting an early embryo after implantation.

¢ If fertilization and implantation do not occur, the corpus luteum degenerates and a new
follicular phase and menstrual phase begin again.

surge, which is characterized by an increased level of LH in serum and urine. level of 75
ng/mL of LH in serum is required for ovulation. LH surge geours 24-36 hagriocrore ovulation.

Solution to Question 112-4

* Granulosa cells express the FSH receptor.
¢ Theca interna cells express LH receptor.

Theca interna cells have many LH receptors. LH acts via cAMP to increase conversion of
cholesterol to androstenedione. The theca interna cells supply androstenedione to the granulosa
cells. The granulosa cells make estradiol when provided with androgens.

Granulosa cells have many FSH receptors. FSH facilitates their secretion of estradiol by acting via
cAMP to increase their aromatase activity. Mature granulosa cells also acquire LH receptors, and
LH also stimulates estradiol production.

Solution to Question 12:

797 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 798 ========================================
An increase in basal body temperature in the mid-luteal phase is due to the effect of progesterone
on the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre.

Immediately after ovulation, corpus luteum is formed, which secretes progesterone in preparation
of the body for potential pregnancy during that cycle. This progesterone acts on the hypothalamus
and increases basal body temperature. Charting the daily basal body temperature will show an
increase of approximately 0.5 to 1 degrees fahrenheit shortly after ovulation.

Since the rise in basal body temperature coincides with ovulation, abstaining from sexual
intercourse until at least 3 days after the woman’s temperature has risen above her regular
temperature significantly lowers the chance of pregnancy. Alternatively, using barrier
methods (e.g. condom, diaphragm) during this time will prevent pregnancy.

However several factors (e.g fever, sleep deprivation etc.) that change the basal body temperature,
can affect the efficacy and reliability of this method. o*

Solution to Question 15:

Estrogen inhibits FSH and LH secretion in the luteal phase.
Feedback regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis

Luteal phase: the corpus luteum secretes large quantities of progesterone and estrogen. They exert
a negative feedback effect on the anterior pituitary gland causing the suppression of both FSH and
LH secretion.

Preovulatory period: The high level of estrogens at this time (or the beginning of Repgesterone
secretion by the follicles) causes a positive feedback stimulatory effect on the anterior pituitary
leading to a surge of LH and to a lesser extent, FSH. ae

Follicular phase: secretion of estrogen, progesterone froin the corpus tern decreases which
leads to loss of negative feedback effect of these honmioifes on the anterior pituitary. Therefore,
pituitary secretion of FSH begins to increase again. as much asitwofold, LH secretion increases
slightly as well. =," x"

Me

GnRH stimulates FSH and LH secretion. Sowth hormine and Ice amy mise an increase in FSH
and LH levels. yok a4 =,

Solution to Question 16: A i,

, a ty
The inhibins inhibit FSH secretion by the gonadotrophs of the anterior pituitary (hence the name
inhibin) in a classic negative-feedback arfahgement.

Solution to Question 17:_ ‘

‘

The inhibins are produced by the granulosa cells of the ovarian follicle, as well as other tissues,
including the pituitary, the brain, the adrenal gland, the kidney, the bone marrow, the corpus
luteum, and the placenta. Inhibin A is produced by the corpus luteum whereas inhibin B is mainly
produced by granulosa cells of the follicle

FSH specifically stimulates the granulosa cells to produce inhibins.

Just before ovulation, after the granulosa cells acquire LH receptors, LH also stimulates the
production of inhibin by granulosa cells.

Solution to Question 18:

The source of progesterone during the normal menstrual cycle is corpus luteum.

Progesterone is also secreted by the placenta, and the ovarian follicle (in small amounts).

799 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 800 ========================================
Solution to Question 19:

Estrogen are secreted primarily by the granulosa cells of the ovarian follicles, the corpus luteum,
and the placenta .

¢ Estradiol is the most abundant in the reproductive age group and is 12 and 80 times more

* The principal sex steroids in females are estrogen, progestin, and androgen. Three estrogens are

Solution to Question 25:

Pubarche or development of axillary and pubic hair is not due to estrogen. It is due to testosterone
(in both the sexes).

Puberty in females :

* Involves the beginning of menstrual cycles (menarche), breast development (thelarche), and an
increase in adrenal androgen secretion (adrenarche).

¢ Estradiol induces the development of secondary sex characteristics, including the: breasts and

reproductive tract, and increased fat in the hips. ns, ry

Solution to Question 27:

Progesterone has 21 carbon atoms.

Solution to Question 28:

The neuroendocrine part of the anterior pituitary is not involved in milk ejection. The posterior
pituitary is involved in milk ejection reflex.

The posterior pituitary secretes oxytocin, which is responsible for milk ejection. The anterior
pituitary produces prolactin, which is responsible for lactogenesis.

Solution to Question 29:

* Oxytocin causes contraction of the myoepithelial cells lining the mammary ducts and ejects milk
during suckling. But, it has no galactogenic effect.
* Prolactin is responsible for milk secretion.

* Estrogens are primarily responsible for the proliferation of the mammary ducts and the
progesterone for the development of lobules.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

The strength of a muscle is determined mainly by its size, with a maximal contractile force
between 3 and 4 kg/cm2 of muscle cross-sectional area.

809 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 810 ========================================
* The female muscle can achieve almost exactly the same maximal force of contraction as that of
the male muscle per square centimeter of cross-sectional area.

* Most of the difference in total muscle performance between males and females lies in the extra
percentage of the male body that is muscle, which is caused partly by endocrine differences:

* Testosterone secreted by the male testes has a powerful anabolic effect in causing greatly
increased deposition of protein in the muscles.

¢ Estrogen increases the deposition of fat in the female, especially in the breasts, hips, and
subcutaneous tissue.

Solution to Question 3:

The phosphagen system is utilized by the muscles of the athlete's body during a dash race.

The combined amounts of cell ATP and cell phosphocreatine are called the phosphagen energy
system. These substances together can provide maximal muscle power for 8 to 10 seconds, almost
enough for the 100-meter run. Thus, the energy from the phosphagen system is used for maximal
short bursts of muscle power.

Energy systems:

¢ The phosphocreatine-creatine system: The combined amounts of cell ATP and cell
phosphocreatine are called the phosphagen energy system. These substances together can provide
maximal muscle power for 8 to 10 seconds.

* The glycogen—lactic acid system: can provide 1.3 to 1.6 minutes of maximal muscle activity.

810 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 811 ========================================
* The aerobic system: The aerobic system is the oxidation of foodstuffs in the mitochondria to
provide energy. It can sustain mucle activity for an unlimited time (as long as nutrients last).

Solution to Question 4:

Slow-twitch fibres, which are adapted for endurance running, are not larger in diameter than
fast-twitch fibres. Fast-twitch fibres are about twice as large in diameter compared with
slow-twitch fibres.

Fast-Twitch and Slow-Twitch Muscle Fibers

h

+ Fast-twitch fibres can deliver extreme amounts of power from a few seconds tg-a minute.

* The enzymes that promote rapid release of energy from the . Phosphagen, and glycogen-lactic acid

energy systems are more active in fast-twitch fibres. _«_=. e nh,
.

¢ Fast-twitch fibres are about twice as large in diagtfet comparg¢ with slow- twitch fibres.

* Gastrocnemius muscle has a higher prepondéraiice of fast- tiitch fibres, whigh gives it the
capability of forceful and rapid contraction of the type used i in jumping» ms

¢ Slow-twitch fibres are mainly orgqaipad for enduranée, especially'for the generation of aerobic
energy. .

¢ They have far more mitochbiiria than doi the fast- twitcl fibres.

* They contain considerably more myidelbbin. The extra myoglobin increases the rate of diffusion
of oxygen throughout the fibre. a >

1.

* The enzymes of the aerobi¢ tnétabolic system are considerably more active in slow-twitch fibres.
¢ The number of capillafies is greater, in, the vicinity of slow-twitch fibres.

* Soleus muscle has.a higher preponderance of slow-twitch muscle fibres and therefore is used to a
greater extent.for prolonged lower leg muscle activity.

Solution to Question 5:

Signals from joint proprioceptors play a role in stimulating the respiratory drive during exercise.
Regulation of respiration in exercise

¢ During strenuous exercise, O02 consumption and CO2 for mation can increase as much as 20-
fold. Yet in the healthy athlete, the arterial PO2, PCO2, and pH remain almost exactly

normal. This is because the alveolar ventilation increases along with the increased level of oxygen
metabolism.

¢ Part of this stimulation of alveolar ventilation results from direct stimulation of the respiratory
center by nervous signals. An additional part is believed to result from sensory signals transmitted
into the respiratory center from the contracting muscles and joint proprioceptors.

¢ During strenuous exercise, the utilization of O2 in the entire body can increase to 75% to 85%.
So, essentially all the oxygen is given to the exercising muscles leading to an increased
